AMZN_10K_2023Q4_2024-02-02_full.txt.document_context: Amazon.com Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2023-12-31. The company says it operates in three segments—North America, International, and Amazon Web Services (AWS)—and serves consumers, sellers, developers, enterprises, content creators, advertisers, and employees through online and physical stores, devices, media, and Amazon Prime.

sections.Item 1: Amazon.com, Inc. describes itself as an e-commerce, consumer devices, digital media, and cloud computing company organized into three segments: North America, International, and Amazon Web Services (AWS). The business emphasizes customer obsession, operational excellence, and long-term thinking, serving consumers, sellers, developers, enterprises, content creators, advertisers, and employees through online and physical stores, fulfillment networks, and customer service operations. Key offerings include Amazon Prime, Kindle, Fire tablet, Fire TV, Echo, Ring, Blink, and eero, with low prices, fast and free delivery, and broad product selection as core consumer value propositions.
sections.Item 1A: Amazon.com Inc. says its business faces intense and rapidly evolving competition across physical and e-commerce retail, omnichannel retail, e-commerce services, web and infrastructure computing services, electronic devices, digital content, advertising, grocery, and transportation and logistics, with rivals often having greater resources, longer operating histories, larger customer bases, or stronger brand recognition. The company warns that competitors may win better vendor terms, use more aggressive pricing, and spend more on technology, infrastructure, fulfillment, and marketing, while new business models, alliances, and well-funded entrants continue to increase pressure. Amazon also highlights that advances in search, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, machine learning, digital content, and devices, along with global economic and geopolitical conditions, could materially harm its business, financial condition, cash flows, liquidity, and stock price.
sections.Preamble: Amazon.com, Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023 with the SEC under Commission File No. 000-22513, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 410 Terry Avenue North, Seattle, Washington 98109-5210, with IRS Employer Identification No. 91-1646860 and telephone number (206) 266-1000. The filing states that Amazon’s common stock trades on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol AMZN, that it has no securities registered under Section 12(g), and that it is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1C: Amazon.com Inc. says it maintains centralized cybersecurity and data protection programs, led by its chief security officer and supported by chief information security officers across businesses including AWS, to assess, identify, and manage material risks to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its systems and data. The company describes layered controls such as application security assessments, vulnerability management, penetration testing, security audits, incident response plans, annual employee training, and oversight of third-party technology and vendors, with cybersecurity risks reviewed by senior leadership, the Audit Committee, and the Board of Directors.
sections.Item 1B: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) states in Item 1B of its 2024 Form 10-K that there are no unresolved staff comments from the SEC. The section is brief and indicates no outstanding issues or requests from the Securities and Exchange Commission staff requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 2: As of December 31, 2023, Amazon.com Inc. operated 664.1 million square feet of leased space and 52.2 million square feet of owned space across office space, physical stores, and fulfillment, data centers, and other facilities in North America and International markets. By segment, Amazon reported 424.1 million leased and 15.4 million owned square feet for North America, 165.3 million leased and 7.9 million owned square feet for International, and 20.4 million leased and 17.8 million owned square feet for AWS, excluding corporate facilities. The company also stated that it owns and leases corporate headquarters in Washington’s Puget Sound region and Arlington, Virginia, and that its physical stores included 600 in North America and 28 internationally.
sections.Item 4: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 2, 2024. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or related metrics, and simply transitions to Part II of the filing.
sections.Item 5: Amazon.com, Inc. common stock trades on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol “AMZN.” As of January 24, 2024, the company had 11,656 shareholders of record, with a much larger number of beneficial owners, and reported no recent sales of unregistered securities and no issuer purchases of equity securities.
sections.Item 7A: Amazon says it is exposed to market risk from interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, and changes in the market values of its investments, with interest rate risk tied mainly to its investment portfolio and long-term debt. As of December 31, 2023, Amazon held $39.160 billion in money market funds and additional marketable debt securities including $28.026 billion of corporate debt securities, $1.774 billion of U.S. government and agency securities, and $1.700 billion of asset-backed securities, with weighted-average interest rates ranging from 0.67% to 5.32%.
sections.Item 7: Amazon.com Inc.’s Item 7 MD&A emphasizes that its 2024 Form 10-K contains extensive forward-looking statements subject to material uncertainty, with risks tied to foreign exchange, inflation, interest rates, global economic and geopolitical conditions, customer demand, labor constraints, litigation, acquisitions, and fulfillment, sortation, delivery, and data center optimization. The section also states that Amazon’s primary revenue source is the sale of a wide range of products and services through its stores, including first-party merchandise and content as well as third-party seller offerings, with additional detail on risks and uncertainties referenced in Item 1A, “Risk Factors.”
sections.Item 9: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) reports no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2024 Form 10-K. The section states simply: “None.”
sections.Item 9A: Amazon.com Inc. reports that, as of December 31, 2023, its disclosure controls and procedures were effective under Rule 13a-15(e) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, providing reasonable assurance that required information is recorded, processed, summarized, reported on time, and communicated to management, including the principal executive officer and principal financial officer. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective under the COSO Internal Control — Integrated Framework (2013), with Ernst & Young independently assessing the controls, and stated there were no changes during the quarter ended December 31, 2023 that materially affected internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Amazon.com, Inc.’s 2024 Form 10-K presents the index to the consolidated financial statements, including the consolidated statements of cash flows, operations, comprehensive income (loss), balance sheets, stockholders’ equity, and notes to consolidated financial statements. Ernst & Young LLP, the independent registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID: 42), audited Amazon’s 2023 and 2022 consolidated financial statements and the three years ended December 31, 2023, and issued an unqualified opinion that they fairly present the company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP. The auditor also reported an unqualified opinion on Amazon’s internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2023 under the PCAOB and COSO 2013 framework.
sections.Item 9B: Amazon.com, Inc. disclosed multiple Rule 10b5-1 trading plans adopted in November 2023 by directors and senior executives, including Jonathan Rubinstein, Douglas Herrington, Jeffrey Bezos, Shelley Reynolds, David Zapolsky, Andrew Jassy, Brian Olsavsky, and Judith McGrath. The plans authorize sales of Amazon common stock in varying amounts, ranging from 5,760 shares to 50,000,000 shares, with end dates spanning March 8, 2024 through February 9, 2026 and subject to specified conditions.
sections.Item 9C: Amazon.com, Inc. sections.Item 14: Amazon.com, Inc. states that the information required by Item 14 of Part III, including principal accountant fees and services, is provided in its Proxy Statement for the 2024 Annual Meeting of Shareholders and incorporated by reference into this Form 10-K. This section is a brief cross-reference to the company’s proxy materials rather than a substantive disclosure, and it appears at the start of Part IV of the annual report.

It also states that all financial statement schedules were omitted because the required information is included in the financial statements or notes, and provides an index of exhibits such as the amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, plus debt-related indentures and officers’ certificates involving Amazon.com, Inc., Wells Fargo Bank, National Association, and Computershare Trust Company, National Association.

sections.Item 1C: Amazon.com Inc. says it maintains centralized cybersecurity and data protection programs to assess, identify, and manage material risks to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its systems and data, including confidential, proprietary, business, and personal information processed for Amazon and third parties. The company describes layered controls and processes such as application security assessments, vulnerability management, penetration testing, security audits, incident response plans, annual employee training, and oversight of third-party technology and systems, including artificial intelligence, encryption and authentication tools, email, content delivery, and back-office support. Amazon’s cybersecurity organization is led by its chief security officer with chief information security officers supporting businesses including AWS, and senior leadership reviews cybersecurity risks as part of enterprise risk assessments.
sections.Item 4: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2026 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC. sections.Item 7: Amazon.com Inc.’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis opens with a broad forward-looking statements disclaimer under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995, emphasizing that guidance and expectations about future results, financial position, and industry prospects are uncertain. It highlights key risks that could materially affect results, including foreign exchange and energy price volatility, tariffs and trade policy, inflation and interest rates, labor and supply constraints such as memory chips, customer demand and spending, competition, litigation and government investigations, and execution risks across fulfillment, sortation, delivery, data center optimization, and international expansion.
sections.Item 9: Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2026 Form 10-K. The section states simply: “None.”

The section is a brief Part III placeholder and contains no additional substantive information, metrics, or regulator-specific findings.
sections.Item 7A: Amazon.com Inc. says it is exposed to market risk from interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, and changes in the market values of its investments, with further discussion referenced in Item 7 of Part II. Its interest rate risk is concentrated in its investment portfolio and fixed-rate long-term debt, and as of December 31, 2025 it held $29.046 billion of money market funds, $69.579 billion of corporate debt securities, $5.222 billion of U.S. government and agency securities, and $1.7 billion of asset-backed securities, with estimated fair values and weighted-average rates disclosed for each category.
sections.Item 9A: Amazon.com Inc. reports that, as of December 31, 2025, its disclosure controls and procedures were effective under Rule 13a-15(e) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, providing reasonable assurance that required information is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time and communicated to management, including the principal executive officer and principal financial officer. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective under the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013), with Ernst & Young independently assessing the controls, and stated there were no material changes in internal control during the quarter ended December 31, 2025.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Amazon.com, Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including the Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows, Operations, Comprehensive Income, Balance Sheets, Stockholders’ Equity, and Notes, with the index pointing to pages 34–41. Ernst & Young LLP, the independent registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID: 42), audited Amazon’s December 31, 2025 and 2024 balance sheets and the three years ended December 31, 2025, and issued an unqualified opinion that the statements fairly present the company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP, along with an unqualified opinion on internal control over financial reporting under the COSO 2013 framework.

Bezos, covering sales of up to 64,059, 132,937, 1,800, 9,744, 142,224, and 15,000,000 shares of Amazon common stock, respectively, over periods ending between November 2026 and February 2027. Olsavsky, Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, terminated a previously adopted 10b5-1 plan that had covered up to 53,249 shares through March 2, 2026.

sections.Item 1A: Amazon.com Inc. says its businesses face intense and rapidly evolving competition across physical retail, e-commerce, omnichannel retail, e-commerce services, web and infrastructure computing services, electronic devices, digital content, advertising, grocery, and transportation and logistics, with rivals often having greater resources, longer operating histories, more customers, or stronger brand recognition. The section also shows heavy capital investment through purchases of property and equipment of $32.2 billion for the quarter and $57.2 billion year-to-date, alongside cash flows from acquisitions, non-marketable investments, and marketable securities activity, reflecting Amazon’s continued spending on infrastructure and growth.
sections.Item 5: Amazon.com, Inc. disclosed that two senior executives adopted Rule 10b5-1 trading plans for sales of company common stock: Matthew S. Garman, CEO of Amazon Web Services, planned to sell up to 80,246 shares through May 29, 2026, and Brian T. Olsavsky, Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, planned to sell up to 53,249 shares through March 2, 2026. The section highlights insider stock-sale plans under SEC Rule 10b5-1(c) and identifies key Amazon leadership figures and their respective share-sale limits and time horizons.
sections.Item 6: Amazon.com, Inc.’s Item 6 lists the exhibits to its Form 10-Q for the quarter ended June 30, 2025, including amended and restated charter documents (Exhibits 3.1 and 3.2), Sarbanes-Oxley and Exchange Act certifications by CEO Andrew R. Jassy and CFO Brian T. Olsavsky (Exhibits 31.1, 31.2, 32.1, 32.2), and Inline XBRL financial statement exhibits (101 and 104). The section also includes the signature page showing the report was signed for Amazon.com, Inc. by Shelley L. Reynolds, Vice President, Worldwide Controller and Principal Accounting Officer, dated July 31, 2025.

AMZN_10Q_2025Q3_2025-10-31_full.txt.document_context: Amazon.com Inc. filed a 10-Q for the quarter ended September 30, 2025. In the excerpt, net income was $21,187 million for the quarter and net cash provided by operating activities was $35,525 million, while purchases of property and equipment were $35,095 million for the nine months ended September 30, 2025.

sections.Item 1: Amazon.com, Inc. reported consolidated cash flow results for the periods ended September 30, 2025, showing net income of $21.2 billion for the quarter and $56.5 billion for the nine months, with operating cash flow of $35.5 billion and $85.1 billion, respectively. The statement highlights heavy capital spending on property and equipment of $35.1 billion in the quarter and $92.3 billion year-to-date, along with stock-based compensation, depreciation and amortization, deferred tax effects, and working-capital changes in inventories, receivables, payables, accrued expenses, and unearned revenue.
sections.Preamble: Amazon.com, Inc. filed a Form 10-Q quarterly report with the SEC for the period ended September 30, 2025, under Commission File No. 000-22513, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation with IRS Employer Identification No. 91-1646860 and principal executive offices at 410 Terry Avenue North, Seattle, Washington 98109-5210. The filing states that Amazon’s common stock trades on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under ticker AMZN, that the company had timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files, and that it qualifies as a large accelerated filer rather than an accelerated, non-accelerated, smaller reporting, or emerging growth company.
sections.Item 2: Amazon.com Inc. states that its Form 10-Q contains forward-looking statements under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995, including guidance and expectations for future results, financial position, and operations, and that actual outcomes may differ materially. The company highlights major risk drivers such as foreign exchange, global economic conditions, tariff and trade policies, customer demand and spending, inflation, interest rates, labor constraints, internet/online commerce/cloud growth, investments in new businesses, product and service mix, taxes, competition, litigation and government investigations, fulfillment and data center optimization, inventory management, acquisitions, payments risk, and fulfillment throughput and productivity, with further detail referenced in Item 1A Risk Factors and the 2024 Annual Report on Form 10-K.
sections.Item 3: Amazon.com Inc. says it is exposed to market risk from interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, and changes in the market values of its investments, with excess cash generally invested in investment-grade short- to intermediate-term marketable debt securities and AAA-rated money market funds. In Q3 2025, the International segment represented 23% of consolidated revenues, and foreign exchange movements in currencies including the Euro, British Pound, and Japanese Yen increased International segment net sales by $1.5 billion versus Q3 2024, while also affecting AWS and intercompany balances.
sections.Item 4: Amazon.com Inc. states that, under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 13a-15(e), its principal executive officer and principal financial officer evaluated disclosure controls and procedures as of the end of the period and concluded they were effective to provide reasonable assurance that required SEC information is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time. The company also reports no material change in internal control over financial reporting during the most recent fiscal quarter, while noting that controls can only provide reasonable, not absolute, assurance against error or fraud; Item 4 Mine Safety Disclosures is marked not applicable.
sections.Item 1A: Amazon.com Inc. says its business faces significant risk from intense and rapidly evolving competition across e-commerce, physical and omnichannel retail, e-commerce services, web and infrastructure computing services, electronic devices, digital content, advertising, grocery, healthcare, communications, and transportation and logistics, with rivals often having greater resources, longer histories, more customers, or stronger brand recognition. The company warns that competitors may win better vendor terms, use more aggressive pricing, and invest more heavily in technology, infrastructure, fulfillment, and marketing, while new business models, alliances, and entrants—along with technologies such as search, artificial intelligence, machine learning, satellites, and other digital services—could further pressure Amazon’s growth, financial condition, operating results, cash flows, liquidity, and stock price.
sections.Item 5: Amazon.com, Inc. entered into a new 364-day unsecured revolving credit agreement on October 29, 2025 with Citibank N.A. as administrative agent and other lenders, replacing its prior $5.0 billion facility from October 30, 2024, which was terminated the same day. The 2025 Short-Term Credit Agreement provides up to $5.0 billion of borrowing capacity, bears interest at the applicable benchmark rate plus 0.45% with a 0.03% commitment fee on undrawn amounts, may be extended for an additional 364 days with lender approval, and is intended for general corporate purposes including backstopping commercial paper, with no financial covenants.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 lists Amazon.com, Inc.’s exhibits for the quarter ended September 30, 2025, including its amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, a new 364-Day Revolving Credit Agreement dated October 29, 2025 with Citibank N.A. as administrative agent, and certifications by CEO Andrew R. Jassy and CFO Brian T. Olsavsky under Rule 13a-14(a) and 18 U.S.C. Section 1350. It also identifies the Inline XBRL financial statement exhibits (Exhibit 101 and cover page Exhibit 104) and the report’s signature by Shelley L. Reynolds, Vice President, Worldwide Controller and Principal Accounting Officer, dated October 30, 2025.

AXP_10K_2026-02-06_full.txt.document_context: American Express Company (AXP) filed a 10-K annual report covering its fiscal year ended 2025. The company describes itself as a global payments and premium lifestyle brand with four reportable operating segments—U.S. Consumer Services, Commercial Services, International Card Services, and Global Merchant and Network Services—built around an integrated payments platform spanning card issuing, merchant acquiring, and network services.

sections.Item 1B: American Express Company (AXP) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. This section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no outstanding regulatory review issues were identified in the company’s 10-K annual report filed on 2026-02-06.
sections.Preamble: American Express Company (NYSE: AXP) filed a Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, under Commission File No. 1-7657, identifying itself as a New York corporation with principal executive offices at 200 Vesey Street, New York, NY 10285 and telephone (212) 640-2000. The filing lists registered securities including Common Shares (par value $0.20, trading symbol AXP) and 3.433% Fixed-to-Floating Rate Notes due May 20, 2032 (AXP32), both on the New York Stock Exchange, and indicates no Section 12(g) securities. It also states that American Express is a well-known seasoned issuer, has filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files for the prior 12 months, and qualifies as a large accelerated filer.
sections.Item 1: American Express Company (AXP), founded in 1850 and headquartered in New York, is a global payments and premium lifestyle brand with card-issuing, merchant-acquiring, and card network businesses serving consumers, small businesses, mid-sized companies, and large corporations worldwide. Its offerings include credit and charge cards, travel/dining/lifestyle and expense management products, banking and non-card lending, merchant acquisition and processing, fraud prevention, point-of-sale marketing, and network services distributed through mobile and online channels, affiliates, service providers, business partners, sales teams, direct mail, and advertising. American Express and its principal subsidiary, American Express Travel Related Services Company, Inc. (TRS), are bank holding companies supervised by the Federal Reserve under the Bank Holding Company Act, and the company operates through four reportable segments: U.S. Consumer Services (USCS), Commercial Services (CS), International Card Services (ICS), and Global Merchant and Network Services (GMNS), plus Corporate
sections.Item 1A: American Express Company says its risk profile is driven by macroeconomic conditions and broader business and consumer activity, which can materially affect card spending, borrowing, fee-based product demand, and customer deposits. The company highlights risks from slow growth, recession, persistent inflation, unemployment, geopolitical instability, tariffs, interest rates, taxes, and other policy or market shifts, all of which can pressure profitability and customer behavior. It also notes that despite significant investment in risk management and controls, it may not meet regulatory expectations or fully manage strategic, reputational, operational, compliance, credit, market, and liquidity risks.
sections.Item 1C: American Express Company (AXP) describes its Technology Risk and Information Security (TRIS) program as the enterprise cybersecurity and information security framework led by its Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and embedded in its operational risk management and risk governance framework. The section defines cybersecurity risk as threats to the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information systems from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction, and says the program uses layered controls, advanced security technology, a security operations center, an Enterprise Incident Response Program, and a Cyber Crisis Response Plan to identify, protect, detect, respond to, and recover from incidents in alignment with global regulatory requirements.
sections.Item 2: American Express Company (AXP) says its principal executive offices are in a 2.2 million square foot lower Manhattan building on land leased from the Battery Park City Authority, with American Express owning about 49% and an affiliate of Brookfield Financial Properties owning the remaining 51%, and it also leases space from Brookfield’s affiliate. The company’s principal owned or leased locations include offices in Phoenix, Sunrise, Gurgaon, Bangalore, Manila, Brighton, Tokyo, Kuala Lumpur, Rome, and Sydney; data centers in Phoenix and Greensboro; headquarters for AENB in Sandy, Utah, American Express Services Europe Limited in London, American Express Europe, S.A. in Madrid, Amex Bank of Canada and Amex Canada Inc. in Toronto, and American Express Company (Mexico) S.A. de C.V. in Mexico City, plus multiple airport lounges for Card Members.
sections.Item 3: American Express Company (AXP) states that its Item 3, Legal Proceedings, is addressed by reference to Note 12 to the Consolidated Financial Statements, indicating that the detailed discussion of litigation, claims, and contingent liabilities is contained elsewhere in the 10-K. This section does not provide substantive legal proceeding details in the excerpt, but it directs readers to the company’s financial statement note for information on any material proceedings involving American Express and related disclosures.
sections.Item 4: American Express Company (NYSE: AXP) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2026 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 6, 2026. The section contains no mine safety incidents, citations, or related metrics, and simply transitions to Part II of the filing.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of American Express Company’s 2026 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure beyond a table of contents reference. The section provides no operational, financial, regulatory, or product-specific information.
sections.Item 7A: American Express Company (AXP) states that its Item 7A quantitative and qualitative disclosures about market risk are provided by reference to the “Risk Management” discussion in Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A). This section does not present additional market risk metrics or analysis itself, but directs readers to the company’s broader risk management disclosures for details on interest rate, credit, liquidity, and other market exposures.
sections.Item 8: American Express Company’s Item 8 discusses management’s report on internal control over financial reporting, stating that its controls are designed to provide reasonable assurance over GAAP financial reporting, accurate transaction records, authorized receipts and expenditures, and protection of assets from unauthorized use. Management assessed the effectiveness of internal control as of December 31, 2025 using the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) and concluded the controls were effective, with PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP issuing the independent audit report on that effectiveness.
sections.Item 5: American Express Company’s common stock trades principally on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker AXP, and as of December 31, 2025 it had 15,910 common shareholders of record. This Item 5 section also points readers to dividend restrictions in “Supervision and Regulation — Dividends and Other Capital Distributions,” notes the upcoming 2026 proxy statement and May 5, 2026 annual meeting, and presents a five-year stock performance graph comparing American Express’s cumulative total shareholder return against the S&P 500 Index and S&P Financial Index.
sections.Item 9: American Express Company (AXP) states in Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” that the section is not applicable. The filing reports no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its auditors regarding accounting or financial disclosure matters in this 2026 Form 10-K.
sections.Item 7: American Express Company (AXP) describes itself as a global payments and premium lifestyle brand powered by technology, organized into four reportable operating segments: U.S. Consumer Services (USCS), Commercial Services (CS), International Card Services (ICS), and Global Merchant and Network Services (GMNS), with Corporate & Other covering corporate functions and certain other businesses. The MD&A outlines its core products and services—credit and charge cards, banking and non-card lending, merchant acquisition and processing, fraud prevention, point-of-sale marketing, and network services—and explains that discount revenue is its largest revenue source, supplemented by interest income, net card fees, and service fees tied to network partnerships, alternative payment solutions, foreign currency fees, and loyalty/merchant programs.
sections.Item 9B: American Express Company (AXP) reported in Item 9B that during the three months ended December 31, 2025, none of its directors or officers, as defined in Rule 16a-1(f) under the Exchange Act, adopted or terminated any contract, instruction, or written plan for buying or selling company securities intended to satisfy Rule 10b5-1(c) affirmative defense conditions. The section also states that no “non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” as defined in Item 408(c) of Regulation S-K was adopted or terminated during the period.
sections.Item 9C: American Express Company (AXP) states that Item 9C is not applicable regarding disclosure of foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections. The section also previews its Part III governance disclosures, noting that a definitive proxy statement under SEC Regulation 14A will be filed in March 2026 for the May 5, 2026 Annual Meeting of Shareholders, where directors will be elected, and that incorporated materials cover board independence, committee responsibilities, director attendance, director compensation, stock ownership, director nominees, executive compensation, and related-party transactions. American Express further identifies its governance framework as its Corporate Governance Principles, Certificate of Incorporation, By-Laws, committee charters for Audit and Compliance, Compensation and Benefits, Nominating, Governance and Public Responsibility, and Risk, plus its Code of Conduct and Board Code of Business Conduct.
sections.Item 9A: American Express Company (AXP) states that management, including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, evaluated disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) and concluded they were effective as of the end of the reporting period, ensuring required information is recorded, processed, summarized, reported on time, and communicated to management for timely disclosure decisions. The company also reports no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of 2025 that materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, internal control, and references the Management’s Report on Internal Control over Financial Reporting and the independent registered public accounting firm’s report as of December 31, 2025.
sections.Item 14: American Express Company (AXP) incorporates by reference the “Audit Committee Matters — PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP Fees and Services” disclosure from its definitive proxy statement for the Annual Meeting of Shareholders scheduled for May 5, 2026. This Item 14 section of the 2026 Form 10-K points readers to the proxy statement for details on principal accountant fees and services, specifically those of PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, and appears in Part IV of the annual report.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of American Express Company’s 2026 Form 10-K states that the financial statements are provided in the “Index to Consolidated Financial Statements,” while all financial statement schedules are omitted because they are not applicable, not material, or already included in the consolidated financial statements. The section then lists exhibits, including the amended and restated certificate of incorporation, by-laws, senior indenture and supplemental indentures with The Bank of New York Mellon as trustee, a description of securities registered under Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, and notes that exhibits 10.1 through 10.24 are management contracts or compensatory plans or arrangements.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains American Express Company’s 10-K signatures and confirms the report was duly authorized and signed on February 6, 2026 by Chief Financial Officer Christophe Y. Le Caillec, Chairman and CEO Stephen J. Squeri, Principal Accounting Officer Jessica Lieberman Quinn, and directors including Karen L. Parkhill, Charles E. Phillips, Jr., Randal K. Quarles, and Christopher D. Young. It also begins the Appendix statistical disclosure for bank holding companies, introducing consolidated average balance, interest income, interest expense, and average yield tables for 2025, 2024, and 2023, with U.S. and non-U.S. office segregation under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.

BAC_10K_2025-02-25_full.txt.document_context: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024. The company says it is one of the world’s largest financial institutions and is continuing to streamline its organizational structure by reducing the number of corporate subsidiaries, including through intercompany mergers, to lower complexity and costs.

sections.Preamble: Bank of America Corporation (NYSE: BAC), incorporated in Delaware and headquartered at Bank of America Corporate Center, 100 N. Tryon Street, Charlotte, North Carolina, filed this Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The preamble identifies the company’s IRS Employer Identification No. 56-0906609, telephone number (704) 386-5681, and a broad set of NYSE-listed securities, including common stock (BAC) and multiple preferred/depositary share series such as BAC PrE, BAC PrB, BAC PrK, BAC PrL, BML PrG, BML PrH, BML PrJ, BML PrL, BAC/PF, and BAC/PG.
sections.Item 1: Bank of America Corporation is a Delaware bank holding company and financial holding company, one of the world’s largest financial institutions, serving consumers, small- and middle-market businesses, institutional investors, large corporations, and governments with banking, investing, asset management, and risk management products and services. The company says it is simplifying its organizational structure by reducing corporate subsidiaries through intercompany mergers, and it maintains its principal executive offices at Bank of America Corporate Center in Charlotte, North Carolina. Bank of America also emphasizes its website and Investor Relations portal for distributing information and SEC filings, including Form 10-K, Form 10-Q, and Form 8-K reports, while noting compliance with Regulation FD and availability of governance documents such as its Code of Conduct and Corporate Governance Guidelines.
sections.Item 1B: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) states that there are no unresolved staff comments in Item 1B of its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 25, 2025. The section indicates that the company had no outstanding comments from the Securities and Exchange Commission staff requiring further resolution.
sections.Item 4: Bank of America Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K Item 4, “Mine Safety Disclosures,” states that there are none. The section appears in Part II of the annual report for Bank of America Corporation and Subsidiaries and contains no mine safety-related metrics, incidents, or regulatory disclosures.
sections.Item 1C: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) directs readers to its Compliance and Operational Risk Management discussion in the MD&A beginning on page 80 for cybersecurity-related information, indicating that cybersecurity is addressed as part of the company’s broader operational risk framework. This Item 1C section contains no additional substantive disclosure beyond the cross-reference incorporated by reference.
sections.Item 7: Bank of America Corporation’s MD&A emphasizes intense competition across traditional banking and nonbank rivals, including investment banks, credit unions, insurers, fintech and internet-based firms, digital asset providers, and other companies offering financial services online. It highlights that competitive success depends on customer service, pricing, product breadth, technology, reputation, and the ability to attract, retain, and motivate employees while controlling compensation and other costs. The section also describes Bank of America’s human capital strategy, noting approximately 213,000 employees, a focus on inclusion and career development, and oversight by the Board and its Compensation and Human Capital Committee.
sections.Item 1A: Bank of America Corporation says its bank subsidiaries are subject to evolving capital, liquidity, operational, and compliance requirements from the Federal Reserve, OCC, and FDIC, which may force it to hold additional capital and liquidity and incur higher compliance costs. The section highlights restrictions on dividends and common stock repurchases under CCAR and FDICIA, noting that Federal Reserve stress testing can affect the stress capital buffer (SCB), which rose to 3.2% after the 2024 CCAR, and that the G-SIB surcharge increased to 3.0% on January 1, 2024. It also warns that the Federal Reserve could limit capital actions or impose additional conditions if a bank is not “well-capitalized” or “well-managed.”
sections.Item 3: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) states that its Item 3 “Legal Proceedings” discussion is incorporated by reference to Note 12, “Commitments and Contingencies,” in the Consolidated Financial Statements. The section directs readers to the company’s Litigation and Regulatory Matters disclosure for details on ongoing legal proceedings, regulatory matters, and related contingencies.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Bank of America Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure. The section appears only as a placeholder with the page reference “Bank of America 24,” and does not add any financial, regulatory, or operational information.
sections.Item 2: Bank of America Corporation reported that as of December 31, 2024 it owned or leased approximately 64.2 million square feet across more than 19,700 facilities and ATM locations worldwide, including about 57.9 million square feet in the U.S. and 6.3 million square feet in more than 35 countries. Principal properties included the Bank of America Corporate Center in Charlotte, NC (principal executive offices, owned, 1,212,177 square feet), Bank of America Tower at One Bryant Park in New York, NY (leased, 2,024,684 square feet, with a 49.9% joint venture interest), Bank of America Financial Centre in London, and Cheung Kong Center in Hong Kong. The company stated its owned and leased properties are adequate and well maintained, while noting ongoing reviews of real estate and potential sale or sale/leaseback transactions.
sections.Item 9: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2025 Form 10-K. The section states “None,” indicating no auditor-related disputes or reporting issues for the period.
sections.Item 5: Bank of America Corporation’s common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “BAC,” and as of February 24, 2025 it had 130,019 registered shareholders. For the three months ended December 31, 2024, BAC repurchased 78,407 thousand shares at a weighted-average price of $45.19 per share, including 78,224 thousand shares under its publicly announced program, leaving remaining buyback authority of $18.875 billion at quarter-end. The section also notes that dividends to shareholders are primarily funded by dividends from bank subsidiaries, which are subject to regulatory capital and dividend restrictions, and that all outstanding preferred stock ranks ahead of common stock for dividends.
sections.Item 7A: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 7A directs readers to the Market Risk Management discussion on page 74 of the MD&A for quantitative and qualitative disclosures about market risk. The section itself is a cross-reference rather than substantive disclosure, indicating that the detailed market risk analysis is contained in the referenced MD&A materials and related sections.
sections.Item 9A: Bank of America Corporation (BAC) states that, as of the end of the reporting period under Rule 13a-15 of the Exchange Act, management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer evaluated the design and effectiveness of disclosure controls and procedures and concluded they were effective. The filing also references the Report of Management on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting and the Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm on pages 87-89, and says there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended December 31, 2024 that materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, internal control.
sections.Item 8: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 8 presents the core audited financial statements and supplementary data for the 2025 10-K, including the Consolidated Statement of Income, Balance Sheet, Cash Flows, Changes in Shareholders’ Equity, and extensive notes covering derivatives, securities, loans and allowance for credit losses, deposits, debt, contingencies, income taxes, fair value measurements, business segments, and regulatory requirements. It also includes management’s report on internal control over financial reporting, emphasizing the Corporation’s responsibility for maintaining effective controls and preparing U.S. GAAP financial statements with reasonable assurance.
sections.Item 9B: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 9B states that no directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 or other trading arrangements during the quarter ended December 31, 2024. It also updates Section 13(r) Exchange Act disclosures, noting prior 2024 Iran-related reporting and that Bank of America, National Association (BANA) processed 69 authorized wire payments totaling $13,965,455 under a U.S. Department of the Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control general license for Afghanistan, with payments to Afghan state-owned banks subject to Executive Order 13224 and only nominal fees earned.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C states that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is not applicable for Bank of America Corporation and Subsidiaries in this 2025 Form 10-K. The section appears at the start of Part III and contains no additional substantive discussion, metrics, or regulatory findings.
sections.Item 11: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 11 incorporates by reference executive compensation disclosures from its 2025 Proxy Statement, including “Compensation discussion and analysis,” “Compensation and Human Capital Committee Report,” “Executive compensation,” “CEO pay ratio,” “Corporate governance,” and “Director compensation.” The section signals that detailed pay, governance, and human capital information for BAC’s named executive officers and directors is contained in the proxy materials rather than repeated in the 10-K.
sections.Item 10: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 10 lists current executive officers and their business experience, highlighting senior leadership across Regional Banking, Technology & Information, Global Markets, Human Resources, Risk, Merrill Wealth Management, The Private Bank, Global Corporate & Investment Banking, and International. Named officers include Dean C. Athanasia, Aditya Bhasin, Darrin Steve Boland, Alastair M. Borthwick, Sheri Bronstein, James P. DeMare, Geoffrey S. Greener, Lindsay D. Hans, Kathleen A. Knox, Matthew M. Koder, and Bernard A. Mensah, with Boland noting his February 2025 retirement decision.
sections.Item 13: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 13 states that the disclosure on certain relationships and related transactions, as well as director independence, is incorporated by reference from the company’s 2025 Proxy Statement. The referenced proxy statement sections are “Related person and certain other transactions” and “Corporate governance – Director independence,” indicating that the filing relies on proxy materials for governance and related-party disclosure details.
sections.Item 12: Bank of America Corporation’s Item 12 incorporates by reference the 2025 Proxy Statement disclosure on stock ownership of directors, executive officers, and certain beneficial owners, and provides its equity compensation plan table as of December 31, 2024. The company reported 269,213,186 outstanding equity awards under shareholder-approved plans, including 4,225,165 vested RSUs subject to post-vest holding periods, and 158,387,893 shares remaining available for future issuance under the Bank of America Corporation Equity Plan; the table excludes 421,369 vested RSUs and stock option gain deferrals assumed in prior acquisitions.
sections.Item 14: Item 14 states that Bank of America Corporation incorporates by reference the “Principal Accounting Fees and Services” information from its 2025 Proxy Statement, specifically the section titled “Proposal 3: Ratifying the appointment of our independent registered public accounting firm for 2025.” The excerpt indicates that the disclosure concerns the company’s independent registered public accounting firm and related audit fee/services matters, with no additional financial metrics or named regulators provided in this section.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Bank of America Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K lists the filed financial statements and exhibits, including the PCAOB ID 238 independent auditor’s report, consolidated income, comprehensive income, balance sheet, shareholders’ equity, cash flow statements, and notes for 2024, 2023, and 2022, with no financial statement schedules included. The exhibit index also references the corporation’s Restated Certificate of Incorporation, Amended and Restated Bylaws, and a series of senior debt indentures and supplemental indentures involving Bank of America, NationsBank, BankAmerica National Trust Company, U.S. Bank Trust National Association, The Bank of New York, and The Bank of New York Mellon Trust Company, N.A., while noting that the 2025 Proxy Statement is not deemed filed except as expressly incorporated by reference.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 of Bank of America Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K contains no Form 10-K summary and consists primarily of the required signatures under Section 13 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The filing was signed on February 25, 2025 by CEO Brian T. Moynihan (also President, Chair and Director), CFO Alastair M. Borthwick, Chief Accounting Officer Rudolf A. Bless, and directors including Sharon L. Allen, José E. Almeida, Pierre J.P. de Weck, Arnold W. Donald, Linda P. Hudson, Monica C. Lozano, Maria N. Martinez, Lionel L. Nowell III, Denise L. Ramos, Clayton S. Rose, Michael D. White, Thomas D. Woods, and Maria T. Zuber, with Ross E. Jeffries, Jr. signing as attorney-in-fact.

BA_10K_2026-01-30_full.txt.document_context: The Boeing Company (BA) filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025. Boeing said it operates in three reportable segments—Commercial Airplanes, Defense, Space & Security, and Global Services—and noted that its commercial aircraft lineup includes the 737, 767, 777 and 787, with development continuing on the 777X and 737-7/737-10 derivatives.

sections.Preamble: The Boeing Company (NYSE: BA) filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, under Commission file number 1-442, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 929 Long Bridge Drive, Arlington, Virginia 22202, with IRS Employer Identification No. 91-0425694 and telephone number (703) 465-3500. The filing lists Boeing’s registered securities as Common Stock ($5.00 par value, symbol BA) and Depositary Shares representing a 1/20th interest in 6.00% Series A Mandatory Convertible Preferred Stock ($1.00 par value, symbol BA-PRA), both traded on the New York Stock Exchange, and indicates Boeing is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1B: The Boeing Company (NYSE: BA) states in Item 1B of its 2026 Form 10-K that there are no unresolved staff comments from the SEC. sections.Item 1A: Boeing’s Item 1A Risk Factors section emphasizes its 2025 culture transformation, including new Values and Behaviors, a revised performance management system, expanded training and leadership development, and a compensation and recognition program designed to attract, reward, and retain talent. It highlights workforce metrics such as approximately 5.8 million hours of employee learning and about 12,000 employees using tuition assistance, alongside commitments to safety, quality, integrity, sustainability, zero workplace injuries, and annual Code of Conduct certification with channels to report ethics, safety, and quality concerns. The excerpt also notes intense competition in the commercial jet aircraft market, with Boeing Commercial Airplanes (BCA) facing Airbus and Chinese entrants while pursuing product and process improvements and cost reductions.
sections.Item 3: The Boeing Company (BA) states that its Item 3 “Legal Proceedings” disclosure is incorporated by reference to Note 23 of its Consolidated Financial Statements in the 2026 Form 10-K. This section provides no additional case-specific details in the excerpt, but directs readers to the financial statement note for Boeing’s litigation, claims, and other legal contingencies.
sections.Item 1C: The Boeing Company (BA) says its cybersecurity strategy emphasizes detection, analysis, response, and resiliency against known, anticipated, and unexpected threats, using risk-based controls, employee training, monitoring systems, contractual safeguards, and third-party tools and services to protect company, customer, supplier, and product-related information and systems. Boeing states its program is aligned with the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Cybersecurity Framework and other industry standards, informed by government partnerships, threat intelligence, internal and third-party audits, and regular pen-testing and red-teaming, while cyber risk is integrated into its Enterprise Risk Management and Compliance Risk Management programs overseen by the Board of Directors.
sections.Item 4: The Boeing Company’s 2026 Form 10-K Item 4, “Mine Safety Disclosures,” states that the disclosure is not applicable. This section contains no mine safety metrics, citations, or regulatory matters involving the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) or other mine-related entities.
sections.Item 5: Boeing’s common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol BA, and as of January 23, 2026 it had 78,957 common shareholders of record. For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, The Boeing Company reported 33,665 shares acquired at an average price of $194.23 per share, all tied to employee tax-withholding transfers from restricted stock unit vesting, and noted that its open-market common stock repurchase program was terminated in March 2020.
sections.Item 1: The Boeing Company (BA) is a major aerospace firm organized into three reportable segments: Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), and Global Services (BGS). BCA develops and markets commercial jetliners including the 737, 767, 777, and 787, with development ongoing on the 777X and 737-7/737-10, while BDS covers military aircraft, weapons systems, missile and defense systems, satellites, and space exploration, and BGS provides worldwide sustainment services such as supply chain and logistics, maintenance, training, spare parts, and digital analytics. Boeing also holds a broad intellectual property portfolio, including patents and licenses, with certain government-use rights in patents developed under U.S. government contracts.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of The Boeing Company’s 2026 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive discussion, financial data, or operational disclosure. sections.Item 2: Boeing reported approximately 111 million square feet of floor space at December 31, 2025, used for manufacturing, warehousing, engineering, administration and other productive uses, with about 86% located in the United States. The portfolio was concentrated in Commercial Airplanes (64.982 million square feet), Defense, Space & Security (30.202 million), Global Services (8.361 million), and Other (7.379 million), with 80.681 million square feet owned, 28.180 million leased, and 2.063 million government-owned, including 1.75 million square feet of rent-free U.S. government space. Major locations included Greater Seattle, Wichita, St. Louis, Greater Charleston, Greater Los Angeles, Tulsa, Portland, Salt Lake City, San Antonio, Philadelphia, Mesa, Huntsville, Oklahoma City, Kennedy Space Center, and sites in China, Australia, Canada, Malaysia, Mexico, Great Britain, Germany, India
sections.Item 7: Boeing’s Item 7 MD&A describes its global aerospace and defense businesses—Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), and Global Services (BGS)—and emphasizes execution, safety, quality, efficiency, and support for commercial jetliners, military aircraft, satellites, missile defense, and human space flight. It highlights the January 5, 2024 737-9 mid-exit door plug accident, the Federal Aviation Administration’s (FAA) investigation and added restrictions, and Boeing’s response of slowing production, delaying rate increases, and investing in workforce training, process simplification, defect elimination, and safety culture improvements. The section also notes that these actions materially affected Boeing’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in 2024 and 2025, and references labor disruption involving IAM 751, which represents about 30,000 Boeing employees.
sections.Item 9B: In Item 9B, The Boeing Company (BA) states that during the three months ended December 31, 2025, none of its directors or officers adopted, modified, or terminated any “Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” or “non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” as defined under Item 408 of Regulation S-K. This section is a brief governance disclosure focused on insider trading plan activity and confirms there were no such changes in the quarter.
sections.Item 9C: The Boeing Company’s Item 9C disclosure states that there are no foreign jurisdictions preventing inspections relevant to its 10-K filed with the SEC on 2026-01-30. sections.Item 9: The Boeing Company (NYSE: BA) reports no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2026 Form 10-K. sections.Item 8: Boeing’s Item 8 presents the consolidated financial statements and notes for The Boeing Company and Subsidiaries, including the Consolidated Statements of Operations, Financial Position, Cash Flows, Equity, and detailed notes on the Spirit Acquisition, Digital Aviation Solutions divestiture, debt, leases, contingencies, derivatives, and segment/revenue information. For 2025, Boeing reported total revenues of $89.463 billion, with sales of products of $75.356 billion and sales of services of $14.107 billion, compared with $66.517 billion in 2024 and $77.794 billion in 2023. The company recorded earnings from operations of $4.281 billion in 2025, aided by a $9.672 billion gain on dispositions, versus a $10.707 billion operating loss in 2024 and a $773 million operating loss in 2023, with interest and debt expense of $2.771 billion and income tax
sections.Item 7A:
Boeing’s Item 7A says interest rate risk on its principally fixed-rate debt obligations is not material because investors generally cannot demand early repayment, while foreign currency risk from customer receipts and supplier payments is managed with foreign currency forward contracts. At December 31, 2025, a 10% move in exchange rates would have changed unrealized gains by $425 million, but the hedges and offsetting underlying commitments are not expected to create material market risk.  

The company also faces commodity price risk on production inputs and uses commodity swaps and commodity purchase contracts to hedge those exposures; a 10% change in commodity prices would have changed unrealized gains by $38 million. Boeing notes that these swaps and purchase contracts, together with offsetting underlying transactions, do not create material market risk, and it also references deferred compensation plan investments subject to fair value changes.
sections.Item 9A: Boeing’s CEO and CFO concluded that disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of December 31, 2025, ensuring Exchange Act reports are recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time under SEC rules. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective under the COSO Internal Control – Integrated Framework (2013), with Deloitte & Touche LLP auditing the controls, while noting that the assessment excluded Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, Inc. because it was acquired on December 8, 2025 and represented about 9% of total assets but less than 1% of revenues and earnings from operations. Boeing stated there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the period and that Spirit’s controls will be assessed within one year of acquisition.
sections.Item 10: Boeing Company (NYSE: BA), originally incorporated in Washington in 1916 and reincorporated in Delaware in 1934, states that its principal executive offices are at 929 Long Bridge Drive, Arlington, Virginia 22202, and that company filings are available through Boeing’s website and the SEC’s EDGAR system. The section also contains a broad cautionary note on forward-looking statements under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995, warning that Boeing’s projections, plans, and operating outlook may differ materially due to risks and uncertainties disclosed in the 10-K and other SEC filings.
sections.Item 14: The Boeing Company states that its independent registered public accounting firm is Deloitte & Touche LLP (PCAOB ID No. 34) and that the required principal accountant fees and services disclosure will be provided in the 2026 Proxy Statement under “Ratify the Appointment of Independent Auditor (Item 3) - Independent Auditor Fees.” This Item 14 section of the 2026 Form 10-K incorporates that proxy disclosure by reference and contains no additional fee detail in the filing excerpt.

sections.Item 12: Boeing’s Item 12 discloses equity compensation plan information as of December 31, 2025, including 7,159,329 shares issuable under shareholder-approved plans and a weighted-average exercise price of $239.49 for 1,299,095 outstanding stock options. The plans include the employee stock purchase plan, the 2023 Incentive Stock Plan, deferred compensation, and other stock units, with 13,770,650 securities remaining available for future issuance and no equity compensation plans not approved by shareholders. The section also notes 585,184 performance restricted stock units, 162,081 assumed Spirit AeroSystems Holdings, Inc. awards converted into Boeing RSUs in connection with the Spirit Acquisition, and that additional stock ownership details will appear in Boeing’s 2026 Proxy Statement.

Cleary (Senior Vice President and Controller), and directors including Lynn J. Good, Stayce D. Harris, Akhil Johri, Robert A. Bradway, David L. Joyce, Mortimer J. Buckley III, Steven M. Mollenkopf, Lynne M. Doughtie, John M. Richardson, David L. Gitlin, and Bradley D. Tilden.

(NYSE: BLK) states that it has no unresolved comments from the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) staff regarding its periodic or current reports filed under the Exchange Act. sections.Item 7: BlackRock Inc. reports that total assets under management (AUM) reached $10.009 trillion at December 31, 2023, up from $8.594 trillion in 2022, with long-term AUM of $9.244 trillion and cash management AUM of $764.8 billion. The section breaks out AUM by product type—equity, fixed income, multi-asset, alternatives, and advisory—and shows five-year CAGR growth rates of 11% for total AUM, driven by net inflows, acquisitions, and market appreciation, with references to non-GAAP financial measures in the 2022 Form 10-K.
sections.Item 2: BlackRock Inc. states that its principal leased office is at 50 Hudson Yards in New York, New York, and that it leases additional office space globally, including Atlanta, Belgrade, Budapest, Edinburgh, Gurgaon, Hong Kong, London, Mumbai, Princeton, San Francisco, and Singapore. The company also owns an 84,500 square foot office building in Wilmington, Delaware, and a 43,000 square foot data center in Amherst, New York.
sections.Item 1: BlackRock, Inc. is a leading publicly traded global investment management firm with $10.0 trillion of assets under management at December 31, 2023, serving institutional and retail clients in more than 100 countries with approximately 19,800 employees across over 30 countries. Its business spans active, index, and cash management strategies across equities, fixed income, alternatives, and money market instruments, distributed through mutual funds, iShares and BlackRock ETFs, separate accounts, collective trust funds, and other pooled vehicles. BlackRock also provides technology and advisory services through Aladdin, Aladdin Wealth, eFront, and Cachematrix, and emphasizes its highly regulated fiduciary role, absence of proprietary trading, and independent governance with no majority shareholder and over 85% independent directors.
sections.Item 1A: BlackRock Inc. says that as a global investment manager, its business is exposed to market, operating, legal, compliance, reputational, fiduciary, and investment risks that could materially hurt financial condition, operating results, and stock price. In Item 1A, the company highlights that declines in equity, debt, real asset, commodity, and foreign exchange markets, along with global trade policies, tariffs, interest-rate changes, and currency movements, can reduce assets under management (AUM), base fees, performance fees, net income, and operating cash flows. BlackRock also warns that volatility, illiquid or disrupted markets, client redemptions, asset reallocation into lower-fee products, and impairments to goodwill, intangible assets, or seed and co-investment capital could further pressure revenue and earnings.
sections.Item 3: BlackRock Inc. (BLK) states that its legal proceedings are discussed in Note 15, “Commitments and Contingencies,” in the notes to the consolidated financial statements included in Part II, Item 8 of the 2024 Form 10-K. This section contains no additional substantive details on specific lawsuits, regulators, claims, or material contingencies beyond directing readers to the financial statement footnote for the Company’s legal matters.
sections.Item 1C: BlackRock Inc. describes cybersecurity as a core part of its enterprise risk management framework, using a multi-lines-of-defense model with global information security, internal audit, and technology risk management teams to identify, prevent, and mitigate threats to the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its data. Its program is aligned with recognized frameworks including NIST CSF, FFIEC CAT, FedRAMP, SOC 1/2, and ISO 27001/2, and is informed by engagement with regulators, clients, insurers, vendors, partners, peers, government, and industry groups. Governance is overseen by the Board’s Risk Committee, the Chief Risk Officer (CRO), Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), and the Technology Risk and Cybersecurity Committee (TRCC) under the Enterprise Risk Committee (ERC).
sections.Item 4: BlackRock Inc. (NYSE: BLK) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 23, 2024. sections.Item 9: BlackRock Inc. (NYSE: BLK) reports that there were no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure matters during the period covered by the 10-K. The company also states that it did not change accountants in either of the two most recent fiscal years.

sections.Item 5: BlackRock Inc.’s common stock is listed on the NYSE under the symbol “BLK,” with 197 stockholders of record as of January 31, 2024, and a February 22, 2024 closing price of $813.44. The company declared quarterly cash dividends of $5.00 per share in each quarter of 2023, and on January 12, 2024 the Board approved a $5.10 quarterly dividend payable March 22, 2024 to holders of record on March 7, 2024. During the quarter ended December 31, 2023, BlackRock repurchased 571,581 shares at an average price of $666.42, primarily for employee and director tax withholding and under its share repurchase program, which was expanded in January 2023 to authorize up to approximately 7.9 million shares.
sections.Item 7A: BlackRock Inc. says its investment advisory and administration fees are largely based on average or period-end AUM, so declines in equity prices, interest rates/credit spreads, or foreign exchange rates could reduce AUM and lower fee revenue. The company also describes corporate investment portfolio risks overseen by its Board of Directors, including exposure to equity, interest rate/credit spread, and FX risk, and notes investments in sponsored products across real assets, private equity, and hedge funds, plus a seed capital hedging program using futures with notional value of about $1.8 billion at December 31, 2023 ($1.5 billion in 2022). BlackRock further reports approximately $6.0 billion of investments in consolidated sponsored investment products at December 31, 2023, versus $4.7 billion in 2022.
sections.Item 9A: BlackRock Inc. (BLK) reported that, under the direction of its Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, its disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of the end of the 2023 fiscal year under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e). The company also stated there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of fiscal 2023 that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, its controls, and reiterated management’s responsibility for maintaining ICFR to provide reasonable assurance over GAAP financial reporting and asset safeguarding.
sections.Item 11: BlackRock Inc. incorporates by reference the executive compensation disclosures from its Proxy Statement, including “Management Development & Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation,” “Executive Compensation – Compensation Discussion and Analysis,” and “Corporate Governance – 2023 Director Compensation.” This Item 11 section therefore directs readers to the company’s proxy materials for details on compensation governance, director pay, and related committee participation for 2023.

(Commission File No. 001-15305) and lists governance, equity, debt, and compensation-related exhibits, including the amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, senior debt indenture with The Bank of New York, and note forms due 2024 through 2033.
