It is signed on February 23, 2024 by Laurence D. Fink, Chairman, Chief Executive Officer and Director, along with Martin S. Small (Chief Financial Officer), Marc D. Comerchero (Chief Accounting Officer), and multiple directors including Robert S. Kapito, Pamela Daley, William E. Ford, Fabrizio Freda, Murry S. Gerber, Margaret L. Johnson, Cheryl D. Mills, Amin H. Nasser, Gordon M. Nixon, Kristin Peck, and Charles H. Robbins, and appoints Fink, Small, Christopher J. Meade, Laura Hildner, and R. Andrew Dickson III as attorneys-in-fact for SEC amendments.

BRK_10K_2025-02-24_full.txt.document_context: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024. The company is a diversified holding company with major businesses in insurance, freight rail transportation, and utilities/energy, and it reported approximately 392,400 employees worldwide at the end of 2024, with about 80% in the U.S. and 20% represented by unions.

sections.Item 1: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. is a Delaware-based holding company headquartered in Omaha, Nebraska, with a highly decentralized structure and operating subsidiaries spanning insurance (primary and reinsurance), BNSF freight rail transportation, utilities and energy generation/distribution, and diverse manufacturing, services, and retail businesses. The company says its Chairman and CEO, Vice Chairman of Insurance Operations, and Vice Chairman of Non-Insurance Operations oversee major capital allocation, investment, and CEO-selection decisions, while the Board handles succession and governance under the Berkshire Code of Business Conduct and Ethics. Berkshire and its subsidiaries employed approximately 392,400 people worldwide at the end of 2024, about 80% in the U.S. and 20% unionized, with subsidiary-level policies focused on workforce attraction, retention, safety, and compensation.
sections.Item 1B: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff as of the 2025 Form 10-K filing dated February 24, 2025. The section indicates that the company had no outstanding regulatory review issues requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 1A: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. says its businesses face broad operational risks from terrorist acts, including nuclear, biological or chemical attacks, armed incursions, and resulting loss of life, facility destruction, property damage, and disruption to worldwide operations. The company also highlights cybersecurity threats—viruses, malicious code, phishing, denial-of-service attacks, unauthorized access, and cyber intrusions—that could cause service interruptions, safety and security failures, regulatory compliance failures, loss of assets and critical information, remediation costs, reputational damage, and adverse effects on results of operations, financial condition, and liquidity despite business continuity and disaster recovery planning.
sections.Preamble: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, listing its principal executive office at 3555 Farnam Street, Omaha, Nebraska and commission file number 001-14905. The filing identifies Berkshire Hathaway as a Delaware corporation with Class A and Class B common stock and multiple senior notes outstanding and registered on the New York Stock Exchange under symbols including BRK.A, BRK.B, BRK25, BRK27, BRK28, BRK30, BRK34, BRK35, BRK39, BRK41, and BRK59. It also indicates the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and that it had timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files during the prior 12 months.
sections.Item 3: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. and its subsidiaries are involved in routine legal actions arising in the ordinary course of business, including claims under insurance contracts and reinsurance contracts issued by Berkshire subsidiaries, and plaintiffs sometimes seek punitive or exemplary damages. The company also faces other pending legal actions that may assert claims or seek fines and penalties, but Berkshire states it does not expect any resulting liability to have a material effect on its consolidated financial condition or results of operations, with additional litigation details referenced in Note 27 to the Consolidated Financial Statements.
sections.Item 1C: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. describes a companywide cybersecurity framework spanning its diverse Business Groups, allowing each group’s CISO to tailor risk identification, mitigation, and incident reporting while maintaining consistent reporting of material cyber events across Berkshire. The company says its Business Groups rely on third-party service providers and that cyberattacks on those providers could create significant financial, operational, or reputational impacts, so Berkshire continuously monitors these risks and uses internal audit and annual CISO reports to inform management. Oversight is assigned to the Audit Committee of Berkshire’s Board of Directors, which receives periodic reports on cybersecurity incidents, trends, and the internal audit workplan focused on information technology and cybersecurity risks.
sections.Item 2: Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s Item 2 describes the heavy physical-asset base of its railroad and utilities businesses, especially BNSF Railway and Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE). BNSF operates over 32,500 route miles of track across 28 states and three Canadian provinces, with more than 50,000 operated miles in total as of December 31, 2024, and owns or leases about 6,800 locomotives and 71,400 freight cars, while recording approximately $2.4 billion of repairs and maintenance expense in 2024. BHE’s properties include electric generation, transmission and distribution facilities, coal mining assets, and natural gas distribution, pipeline, storage, liquefied natural gas, compressor, and meter stations.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, financial data, or discussion of operations, risks, or results. The section appears only as “[Reserved]” on page K-32, with no named entities, metrics, or additional information beyond the placeholder designation.
sections.Item 5: Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s Class A and Class B common stock trade on the New York Stock Exchange under BRK.A and BRK.B, with about 1,100 record holders of Class A and 16,800 record holders of Class B as of February 10, 2025, including nominees holding at least 319,000 Class A shares and 1,332,000,000 Class B shares for beneficial owners. Berkshire has not declared a cash dividend since 1967, and its repurchase program authorizes buybacks only when Warren Buffett, as Chairman and CEO, believes the price is below intrinsic value; no Class A or Class B shares were repurchased in the fourth quarter of 2024, the program has no fixed share limit or expiration date, and Berkshire will not repurchase if consolidated cash, cash equivalents, and U.S. Treasury Bills would fall below $30 billion.
sections.Item 7A: Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s Item 7A cross-references the “Market Risk Disclosures” in Item 7 and includes management’s report on internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2024. Management, under the supervision of the principal executive officer and principal financial officer and using the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) under SEC Exchange Act Rule 13a-15, concluded that Berkshire’s internal control over financial reporting was effective, and Deloitte & Touche LLP audited that conclusion.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 10-K presents the consolidated balance sheets, statements of earnings, comprehensive income, shareholders’ equity, and cash flows for 2024, 2023, and 2022, along with the notes to the financial statements. The report of the independent registered public accounting firm states that Berkshire Hathaway and subsidiaries’ financial statements fairly present the company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP, and that internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 31, 2024 under COSO’s Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013).
sections.Item 9: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. states that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure for the 2025 Form 10-K. The Item 9 disclosure is limited to a single “None” statement, with no additional facts, regulators, products, or metrics reported.
sections.Item 4: Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s Item 4 Mine Safety Disclosures states that information on mine safety violations and other legal matters required by Section 1503(a) of the Dodd-Frank Reform Act is provided in Exhibit 95 to the 2025 Form 10-K. The section also identifies Berkshire’s named executive officers—Warren E. Buffett, Gregory E. Abel, Ajit Jain, and Marc D. Hamburg—and notes that each serves under the company’s bylaws until the next annual shareholder meeting or until a successor is chosen. It further includes a standard forward-looking statements caution describing risks, uncertainties, and assumptions affecting Berkshire and its subsidiaries.
sections.Item 9A: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. reported that, under the supervision of management including Chairman/Chief Executive Officer and Senior Vice President/Chief Financial Officer, it evaluated disclosure controls and procedures pursuant to Exchange Act Rule 13a-15 and concluded they were effective in timely alerting management to material information for inclusion in periodic SEC filings. The company also incorporated by reference its Management’s Report on Internal Control Over Financial Reporting and the independent registered public accounting firm’s attestation under Item 308 of Regulation S-K, and stated there was no material change in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended December 31, 2024.
sections.Item 7: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. reported 2024 net earnings attributable to shareholders of $88.995 billion, down from $96.223 billion in 2023 but far above the $22.759 billion loss in 2022, with major contributions from investment gains ($41.558 billion), insurance investment income ($13.670 billion), manufacturing/service/retailing ($13.072 billion), insurance underwriting ($9.020 billion), BNSF ($5.031 billion), and Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE) ($3.730 billion). Management highlighted that results are affected by macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions and noted that insurance underwriting improved materially due to GEICO, though 2024 included estimated after-tax claims from Hurricanes Helene and Milton of $1.2 billion and bankruptcy-related accruals, while higher short-term interest rates boosted insurance investment income.
sections.Item 10: Item 10 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K covers Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance, indicating the section addresses the company’s board composition, senior management, and governance framework. This section is part of the annual report filed with the SEC and is identified in the filing as “Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance | K-117.”
sections.Item 9C: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspection,” is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K. The filing also notes that most Part III information for Items 10 through 14 is incorporated by reference from the company’s definitive proxy statement filed under Regulation 14A for the Annual Meeting of Shareholders on May 3, 2025, which includes the election of directors, except for “Executive Officers of the Registrant” disclosed in Part I.
sections.Item 11: Item 11 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is the Executive Compensation section, which addresses compensation practices for the company’s named executive officers and related governance matters. The section is identified in the filing as “Executive Compensation | K-117,” indicating the location of detailed disclosure on Berkshire Hathaway’s pay arrangements, incentives, and oversight.
sections.Item 13: Item 13 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K addresses certain relationships and related transactions, along with director independence matters. The section is identified as “Certain Relationships and Related Transactions and Director Independence” and appears on page K-117.
sections.Item 12: Item 12 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K covers security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management, along with related stockholder matters. The section is identified in the filing as “Security Ownership of Certain Beneficial Owners and Management and Related Stockholder Matters” and appears on page K-117.
sections.Item 14: Item 14 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K (Part IV) identifies the section for Principal Accountant Fees and Services, which covers disclosure of fees paid to the company’s principal independent registered public accounting firm. This section is part of the annual report’s governance and audit-related disclosures and is associated with Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK) and its external auditor.
sections.Item 9B: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. states that it has not adopted any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement under Item 408(a)(1)(i) of Regulation S-K, and that no directors or executive officers adopted or terminated either a Rule 10b5-1 or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement during the fourth quarter of 2024. This Item 9B disclosure is a compliance update focused on insider trading plans and confirms no such arrangements were in place or changed in Q4 2024.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Berkshire Hathaway Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K lists the incorporated audited consolidated financial statements for 2024, 2023, and 2022, including the Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm (PCAOB ID No. 34), balance sheets, earnings, comprehensive income, shareholders’ equity, cash flows, and notes. It also includes Schedule I for parent company condensed financial information and states that other schedules are omitted because they are not required, not applicable, or already reflected in the consolidated financial statements or notes, with exhibits referenced in the Exhibit Index.

CAT_10K_2026-02-13_full.txt.document_context: Caterpillar Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025. The excerpt highlights that Cat Financial uses foreign currency forward, option and cross-currency contracts to reduce earnings volatility, and that Caterpillar’s insurance subsidiaries are regulated in Missouri and other jurisdictions, with Caterpillar Life Insurance Company assuming 100% of the risk on an accident and health stop loss policy for a VEBA trust covering select retirees and dependents.

sections.Item 1B: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) states that there were no unresolved staff comments from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) for the 2026 Form 10-K annual report. This Item 1B section is effectively empty, indicating no outstanding regulatory review issues or follow-up comments requiring disclosure.
sections.Preamble: Caterpillar Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report with the SEC for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, under Commission File No. 1-768, identifying its principal executive offices at 5205 N. O'Connor Boulevard, Suite 100, Irving, Texas 75039. The filing states that Caterpillar’s common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol CAT and its 5.3% Debentures due September 15, 2035 trade under CAT35, and notes that the company is a Delaware corporation, a well-known seasoned issuer, and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1D: Item 1D of Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K is the “Information about our Executive Officers” section, which identifies the company’s senior leadership team and provides a brief overview of their roles. sections.Item 2: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) states in Item 2 — Properties that its annual report includes a properties section, but the provided excerpt contains only the section heading and page reference (“Properties | 23”) with no substantive disclosure. No specific facilities, geographic locations, owned or leased properties, or related metrics are described in this excerpt.
sections.Item 1A: Caterpillar Inc. describes risk management for Cat Financial’s foreign currency exposure, using forward, option, and cross-currency contracts to reduce earnings volatility from balance-sheet remeasurement and future foreign-currency transactions. The section also details regulatory and solvency risks for Caterpillar Insurance Company and Caterpillar Life Insurance Company, both Missouri-domiciled insurers overseen primarily by the Missouri Department of Insurance and subject to state regulators, the National Association of Insurance Commissioners, and, for the Zurich branch, the Swiss Financial Market Supervisory Authority. Caterpillar Life Insurance Company also provides reinsurance to Caterpillar Insurance Company, assuming 100% of an Accident and Health Stop Loss policy covering a Caterpillar VEBA Trust for medical losses of select retirees and dependents.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, financial data, or discussion of operations. The section appears only as a placeholder in the annual report filed with the SEC on 2026-02-13.
sections.Item 3: Item 3, Legal Proceedings in Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K is a brief section indicating the company’s disclosure of legal matters and litigation exposure, with the excerpt provided showing only the section heading and page reference. This section would typically summarize material proceedings involving Caterpillar Inc. and any related regulatory or court actions, but no specific cases, claims, or named regulators are included in the excerpt.
sections.Item 1C: Caterpillar Inc. describes cybersecurity as integral to its enterprise risk management, digital efforts, and protection of corporate IT, operational technology, and customer-facing products, noting threats such as ransomware, denial-of-service attacks, and advanced adversaries that could also affect customers, suppliers, and other partners. The company says its program uses governance processes to identify, assess, mitigate, respond to, and report cyber risks, relies on NIST Cyber Security Framework and ISO 27001 standards, partners with third parties for maturity assessments, incident response, penetration testing, and consulting, and operates a 24x7 monitoring center to coordinate investigation and remediation. In incident response, Caterpillar evaluates safety, supply chain and manufacturing disruption, data and personal information loss, business interruption, cost, and reputational harm, with support from technical, legal, and law enforcement resources as needed.
sections.Item 7: Caterpillar Inc. says it expects to incur about $300 million to $350 million of restructuring costs in 2026, with prior restructuring actions adding roughly $40 million of incremental benefit to operating costs, mainly cost of goods sold and SG&A, versus 2025. The section also defines key non-GAAP metrics such as Adjusted Operating Profit Margin and Adjusted Profit Per Share, and describes major operating segments and functions including All Other Segment, Construction Industries, Machinery, Power & Energy, Financial Products, dealer development, parts distribution, logistics, automation, electronics, software, and digital investments.
sections.Item 4: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) provides Mine Safety Disclosures in Item 4 of its 2026 Form 10-K, but this section excerpt contains only the section heading and page reference (“Mine Safety Disclosures | 25”) with no substantive mine safety metrics, citations, or regulatory details. The excerpt does not identify any specific mines, violations, MSHA actions, or other named entities beyond Caterpillar Inc. and the Mine Safety Disclosures section itself.
sections.Item 9: Caterpillar Inc. sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K presents the company’s Financial Statements and Supplementary Data, the core audited financial reporting section of the annual report. This section contains Caterpillar’s consolidated financial statements and related supplementary disclosures for the fiscal year ended 2025, providing the primary accounting and performance data used by investors, analysts, and regulators.
sections.Item 9B: Item 9B, Other Information, in Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K contains no substantive disclosure in the excerpt provided, appearing only as a section heading with page reference “124.” This section does not identify any new events, regulatory matters, products, metrics, or other material information beyond the filing structure itself.
sections.Item 9A: Item 9A, Controls and Procedures, in Caterpillar Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K is a brief section indicating the company’s disclosure controls and internal control framework for financial reporting. sections.Item 7A: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) Item 7A, Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk, is a brief section in the 2026 Form 10-K that introduces the company’s discussion of exposure to market risks. The excerpt provided contains only the section heading and page reference, indicating this part of the filing is where Caterpillar addresses quantitative and qualitative risk disclosures related to financial market factors.
sections.Item 9C: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) states in Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” that the item is not applicable. The section appears at page 124 of the 2026 Form 10-K and transitions into Part III, with no foreign jurisdiction inspection issues disclosed.

sections.Item 12: Caterpillar Inc. (CAT) states that security ownership information for certain beneficial owners and management is incorporated by reference from the 2026 Proxy Statement, and provides equity compensation plan data as of December 31, 2025. Approved equity compensation plans cover 4,710,403 securities with a weighted-average exercise price of $220.52, and 39,336,515 securities remain available for future issuance; there were no securities under plans not approved by security holders.
sections.Item 13: Caterpillar Inc. states that the information required for Item 13, “Certain Relationships and Related Transactions, and Director Independence,” is incorporated by reference from its 2026 Proxy Statement. This section does not provide substantive details in the 10-K excerpt itself, but directs readers to the proxy statement for disclosures on related-party transactions and director independence.
sections.Item 14: Item 14 states that Caterpillar Inc.’s independent registered public accounting firm is PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP of Dallas, Texas (Auditor Firm ID: 238), and that the required information is incorporated by reference from the 2026 Proxy Statement. The section then transitions into Part IV, Item 15, listing exhibits and financial statement schedules, including Caterpillar’s restated certificate of incorporation, amended bylaws, and multiple indenture-related agreements and debenture forms involving The First National Bank of Chicago, Citibank, N.A., and U.S. Bank National Association.
sections.Item 16: Caterpillar Inc., originally organized as Caterpillar Tractor Co. in 1925 and reorganized in Delaware in 1986, reported 2025 sales and revenues of $67.589 billion and described itself as the world’s leading manufacturer of construction and mining equipment, off-highway diesel and natural gas engines, industrial gas turbines, and diesel-electric locomotives. The company highlighted its global dealer network, Cat Financial financing services, and five operating segments, including the reportable Power & Energy, Construction Industries, and Resource Industries businesses, while emphasizing its 2025 refreshed strategy centered on “Solving our customers’ toughest challenges,” commercial excellence, advanced technology, and operational excellence.
sections.Item 10: Caterpillar Inc.’s Item 10 describes its directors, executive officers, and corporate governance by incorporating much of the required disclosure from the 2026 Proxy Statement, including board nominees, audit committee matters, Section 16(a) compliance, and any legal proceedings involving officers or directors. The section states there are no family relationships between officers and directors and highlights Caterpillar’s Worldwide Code of Conduct, first published in 1974 and updated in 2019, which applies to all directors and employees, including the CEO, CFO, and principal accounting officer, and is posted at www.Caterpillar.com/code. It also notes Caterpillar’s policy to comply with applicable securities and state laws, including Board or committee approvals when the company transacts in its own securities, and references New York Stock Exchange and SEC disclosure rules for code amendments or waivers.

CRM_10K_2025-03-05_full.txt.document_context: Salesforce Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025-01-31. The company describes itself as a global CRM leader and says its AI-powered Salesforce Platform, including Agentforce, unites sales, service, marketing, commerce, collaboration, integration, analytics and automation on one platform to help customers deploy autonomous AI agents and improve productivity, lower costs and drive operational efficiencies.

sections.Item 1B: Salesforce Inc. (NYSE: CRM) reported no unresolved staff comments in Item 1B of its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on March 5, 2025. The section states “None,” indicating there were no outstanding comments from the SEC staff requiring resolution.
sections.Item 1A: Salesforce Inc. says its business is exposed to material risks that could adversely affect growth, reputation, operating and financial results, cash flows, liquidity, and the trading price of its common stock, and that the risk factors are not exhaustive. The section highlights operational and execution risks including security breaches affecting Salesforce, third-party data center hosting facilities, cloud computing platform providers, service partners, and internet infrastructure; service defects or outages; third-party interruptions; failure to realize acquisition benefits; scaling and personnel strain; customer attrition; and uncertainty around subscription renewals and upgrade rates.
sections.Preamble: Salesforce, Inc. (NYSE: CRM) filed its Form 10-K annual report under Sections 13/15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 for the fiscal year ended January 31, 2025, with Commission File Number 001-32224. The filing identifies Salesforce as a Delaware corporation headquartered at Salesforce Tower, 415 Mission Street, San Francisco, California 94105, with IRS Employer Identification No. 94-3320693 and telephone number (415) 901-7000, and notes its common stock is registered on the New York Stock Exchange. It also states that Salesforce is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, and that it has filed all required reports and submitted all required Interactive Data Files under Regulation S-T.
sections.Item 2: As of January 31, 2025, Salesforce Inc. (CRM) used approximately 0.9 million square feet of leased and owned property in San Francisco for executive and principal offices supporting sales, marketing, professional services, development, and administration, excluding about 2.1 million square feet in San Francisco that is leased to others or available for lease. The company also leases office space across the United States and in Europe, North America, Asia, South America, Africa, and Australia, and operates data centers in the U.S., Europe, and Asia under co-location lease arrangements, stating that its existing facilities are adequate for current needs and that additional space should be obtainable on commercially reasonable terms.
sections.Item 1: Salesforce, Inc. (CRM), founded in 1999, describes itself as a global leader in CRM technology and an AI-powered platform that unifies sales, service, marketing, commerce, collaboration, integration, analytics, automation and industry solutions to give customers a single source of truth across systems and devices. The company highlights Agentforce, its agentic AI layer for deploying autonomous digital labor across business functions, and says it sells primarily on a subscription basis worldwide through direct sales and partners while enabling third parties to build applications on its platform. Salesforce emphasizes core values of trust, customer success, innovation, equality and sustainability, with trust grounded in compliance with laws and regulations governing security, privacy, data protection, operational integrity and enterprise-grade availability.
sections.Item 3: Salesforce Inc. says it evaluates all claims and lawsuits based on merits, defenses, counterclaims, settlement or litigation potential, and expected business impact, and warns that its technologies could be subject to injunctions if found to infringe third-party rights. The company also notes that many subscription agreements require it to indemnify customers against third-party intellectual property infringement claims, which could increase the cost of adverse rulings and force changes to its technology or business practices, payment of damages, or entry into royalty or licensing agreements.
sections.Item 4: Salesforce Inc. (CRM) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on March 5, 2025. The section contains no mine safety operations, metrics, citations, or regulatory disclosures.
sections.Item 1C: Salesforce Inc. says its CRM and enterprise cloud services store and transmit customers’ and customers’ customers’ data, making the company a continuing target for cybersecurity threats, and it emphasizes “trust” and secure, compliant platform design as core to its business. Management runs day-to-day cybersecurity risk management while the Board’s Cybersecurity and Privacy Committee oversees risks; Salesforce reports no cybersecurity risks or incidents in the last fiscal year that materially affected operations, but says ongoing threats from increasingly sophisticated bad actors and service defects could materially affect the company. The company describes a company-wide cybersecurity program informed by NIST and other recognized frameworks, supported by experienced cybersecurity professionals, customer incident-prevention training, and efforts to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of its information systems and stored data.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Salesforce, Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is marked “RESERVED” and contains no substantive discussion, financial data, or operational disclosure in the section excerpt provided. The section appears only as a placeholder within the table of contents, with no named products, metrics, regulators, or other material facts disclosed.
sections.Item 4A: Salesforce Inc.’s Item 4A lists its executive officers as of February 28, 2025, including Marc Benioff (Chair of the Board, CEO and co-Founder), Parker Harris (Director and Chief Technology Officer, Slack), Miguel Milano (President and Chief Revenue Officer), Brian Millham, Sabastian Niles, Sundeep Reddy, David Schmaier, Srinivas Tallapragada, and Amy Weaver. The section emphasizes Benioff’s long tenure since 2001 and Salesforce’s position as the #1 global provider of CRM software, while also noting Harris’s role as a Salesforce co-founder and former CTO, plus Benioff’s ties to the World Economic Forum, Salesforce Foundation, and University of Southern California.
sections.Item 7: Salesforce Inc. (CRM) describes itself as a global leader in customer relationship management (CRM) technology, emphasizing its unified platform that connects sales, service, marketing, commerce and IT teams through customer data, AI, and trust to create a single source of customer truth. The section highlights fiscal 2025 and 2024 management discussion, forward-looking statements and risk factors, and notes the launch of Agentforce in Q3 fiscal 2025 as a new layer enabling autonomous AI agents across business functions, alongside continued investment in generative and agentic AI across products.
sections.Item 7A: Salesforce Inc. says it is exposed to market risk from foreign currency exchange rates, interest rates, and equity investments, with risk heightened by recent financial market movements and macroeconomic conditions. Its global operations across the United States, Europe, Canada, Latin America, Asia Pacific, and Japan create currency exposure in the Euro, British Pound Sterling, Japanese Yen, Canadian Dollar, Australian Dollar, and Brazilian Real, driven by multi-currency subscriptions, receivables, intercompany transactions, and international investments such as data center expansion and third-party infrastructure costs. To manage transaction risk, Salesforce uses short-term foreign currency forward contracts that are not designated as hedges and are marked to fair value through earnings under its consolidated financial statements.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Salesforce, Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K indexes the consolidated financial statements, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, stockholders’ equity, cash flows, and notes, with the independent auditor’s report on page 56. The report states that Salesforce’s consolidated financial statements for the years ended January 31, 2025 and 2024 were audited in accordance with U.S. GAAP and that the company’s internal control over financial reporting was also audited under PCAOB standards, with an unqualified opinion issued by the registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID: 42).

sections.Preamble: Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ: CSCO) filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended July 26, 2025, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 170 West Tasman Drive, San Jose, California 95134-1706. The filing states Cisco’s common stock is registered on The Nasdaq Stock Market LLC, that it has no securities registered under Section 12(g), and that it is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.
sections.Item 1A: Cisco Systems says its networking business spans switching, routing, wireless, and servers, combining hardware, software licenses, and SaaS to help customers modernize network infrastructure for hybrid and cloud-first environments. The section highlights campus and data center switching, including the Catalyst 9000 family with Cisco DNA software subscriptions, Meraki cloud-managed switches, and the newly launched Cisco Smart Switches Cisco 9350 and Cisco 9610, while warning that failure to anticipate technological trends and customer needs could hurt operating results and market share. sections.Item 1B: Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) states in Item 1B, “Unresolved Staff Comments,” that there were none as of the 2025 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. sections.Item 1: Cisco Systems Inc. designs and sells global technologies for networking, security, collaboration, and observability, increasingly embedding artificial intelligence across its product portfolios and integrating products more tightly to help customers power, secure, and derive insights from the Internet. The company serves businesses of all sizes, public institutions, governments, and service providers across the Americas, EMEA, and APJC, and also provides lifecycle services such as technical support and advanced services. Cisco was incorporated in California in 1984, reincorporated in Delaware in 2021, is headquartered in San Jose, California, and makes SEC filings available through its investor relations website and sec.gov.
sections.Item 3: Cisco Systems, Inc. states that its pending legal proceedings are described in Note 14, “Commitments and Contingencies - (f) Legal Proceedings,” in the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements included in Item 8 of this Form 10-K. This Item 3 section does not provide substantive case details itself, but incorporates the Item 8 note by reference as the source for Cisco’s legal proceedings disclosure.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Cisco Systems Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, with the section text showing only “[Reserved]” and a table of contents page reference. No financial metrics, business updates, products, or regulatory matters are discussed in this section.
sections.Item 2: Cisco Systems Inc. states that its corporate headquarters are at an owned site in San Jose, California, with regional headquarters in Amsterdam, Netherlands (EMEA) and Singapore (APJC). The company also owns additional U.S. facilities in the San Jose area, Research Triangle Park, North Carolina, and Richardson, Texas, while leasing office space across many U.S. locations and primarily leased sites abroad, including significant operations in Australia, Belgium, Canada, China, Germany, India, Israel, Norway, Poland, and the United Kingdom. Cisco says its owned and leased facilities are in good condition and suitable for conducting its business.
sections.Item 7: Cisco Systems Inc.’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis in its 2025 Form 10-K emphasizes that the report contains forward-looking statements protected by the safe harbors of the Securities Act of 1933 and the Exchange Act of 1934. It explains that statements about future financial performance, growth, trends, projections, targets, and plans are based on management’s current expectations, estimates, forecasts, and assumptions, and warns that these predictions are subject to risks, uncertainties, and assumptions that are difficult to predict.
sections.Item 1C: Cisco Systems Inc. says cybersecurity is a critical trust issue and describes a companywide risk management program led by its Security and Trust Organization (S&TO) under the Chief Security and Trust Officer, with processes to identify, assess, monitor, detect, investigate, respond to, and escalate internal and external threats and potentially material incidents to senior management and, when appropriate, the independent registered public accounting firm. Cisco also partners with Talos Threat Intelligence Group, governments, peer companies, consultants, and auditors to share threat intelligence, monitor vulnerabilities on the internet and dark web, and manage third-party supplier risk through audits, contractual requirements, and ongoing assessments. The company embeds security into products and services through the Cisco Secure Development Lifecycle (CSDL), provides mandatory employee cybersecurity training, and gives employees tools to report suspected incidents.
sections.Item 5: Cisco Systems Inc. states that its common stock trades on the Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol CSCO and that it had 30,790 registered stockholders as of August 28, 2025. During the quarter ended July 26, 2025, Cisco repurchased 19 million shares at an average price of $64.65 per share under its publicly announced stock repurchase program, leaving approximately $14.2 billion authorized for future repurchases with no termination date. The section also notes that Cisco’s stock performance graph compares five-year cumulative total shareholder return against the S&P 500 Index and the S&P Information Technology Index.

sections.Item 7A: Cisco Systems Inc. says its financial position is exposed to interest rate risk, equity price risk, and foreign currency exchange risk, with the main focus here on available-for-sale debt investments held for return of principal and risk management rather than trading. As of July 26, 2025, Cisco was not leveraged in this portfolio and estimated fair value sensitivity under parallel yield-curve shifts of plus or minus 50, 100, and 150 basis points, with fair value ranging from $7,307 million to $7,454 million versus $10,057 million to $9,672 million as of July 27, 2024. The company states it monitors credit exposures by rating category and issuer and believes the portfolio’s overall credit quality is strong.

sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Cisco Systems, Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, cash flows, stockholders’ equity, and notes covering revenue, acquisitions, goodwill and intangible assets, restructuring charges, leases, financing receivables, investments, fair value, borrowings, derivatives, contingencies, income taxes, segment information, and net income per share. The plan contemplates sales of approximately 49,067 gross shares, including shares underlying performance-based equity awards at target, related dividend-equivalent shares, and shares purchased through Cisco’s employee stock purchase plan, while excluding shares withheld for tax obligations, and is scheduled to terminate on December 31, 2025 unless earlier terminated under specified events.

sections.Preamble: Chevron Corporation’s 2024 Form 10-K, filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) under Commission File Number 001-00368, identifies Chevron as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 1400 Smith Street, Houston, Texas 77002-7327, with telephone number (832) 854-1000. The filing states that Chevron’s common stock (par value $0.75 per share) trades on the New York Stock Exchange under ticker CVX, and that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.

sections.Item 1: Chevron Corporation, a Delaware corporation, manages investments in subsidiaries and affiliates and provides administrative, financial, management, and technology support to U.S. and international businesses engaged in integrated energy and chemicals operations. Its upstream segment covers exploration, production, transportation, LNG processing/liquefaction/regasification, crude oil export pipelines, natural gas marketing and storage, carbon capture and storage, and a gas-to-liquids plant, while downstream operations include refining, marketing crude oil and petroleum products, renewable fuels, petrochemicals, plastics, and fuel and lubricant additives. The section also highlights that Chevron’s business is shaped by global supply and demand, OPEC, Russia and U.S. production, economic conditions, weather, energy-transition trends, taxation, and laws and policies on energy and the environment, with strong competition across petroleum and petrochemical markets.
sections.Item 1A: Chevron’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that, as a global energy company, its results of operations, financial condition, and liquidity are highly exposed to volatile commodity prices for crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids. The filing highlights drivers of price swings including economic growth, industry production and inventories, OPEC and other producer actions, weather and geopolitical risks, energy transition trends, customer preferences, substitutes, and government regulation of oil and gas development, greenhouse gas emissions, and climate change. Extended periods of low prices could materially reduce upstream earnings, cash flows, capital expenditures, production, and proved reserves, with related climate and environmental matters discussed elsewhere in the 2025 10-K, including environmental expenditures and remediation reserves.
sections.Item 2: Chevron Corporation (CVX) states that its crude oil and natural gas properties, along with its refining, marketing, transportation, and chemicals facilities, are described in the Properties section beginning on page 3 of the 2025 Form 10-K. sections.Item 1C: Chevron says its cybersecurity program protects business and proprietary information across IT and OT networks through a risk-based approach integrated into Enterprise Risk Management and aligned with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework. The company uses worldwide cybersecurity teams, automated threat intelligence, testing and risk assessments of technologies and third-party suppliers, and updated cybersecurity guardrails to mitigate vulnerabilities and support business resiliency. Chevron also notes expanding third-party supplier risk management beyond IT vendors, while its Chief Information Officer (CIO), who joined in 2024, oversees the broader IT and cybersecurity program and incident remediation/recovery efforts.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Chevron Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure. This section does not provide operational, financial, regulatory, or product-specific information for Chevron (CVX) or any other named entity.
sections.Item 5: Chevron Corporation’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker CVX, and as of February 7, 2025 it had approximately 95,000 stockholders of record. sections.Item 3: Chevron Corporation’s Item 3 Legal Proceedings discloses several environmental enforcement matters that could each result in $1.0 million or more in civil penalties, including a May 2024 New Mexico Environment Department Notice of Violation for alleged air-quality violations at Chevron facilities, a Renewable Fuel Standard issue at the El Segundo, California refinery involving overstated 2022 biofuel credits reported to the U.S. EPA, and two October 2024 Bay Area Air District NOVs at the Richmond, California refinery tied to low-NOx burner permit conditions. The section also notes that Chevron paid $1.5 million in stipulated penalties in August 2024 related to a 2015 Noble Energy consent decree with the U.S. Department of Justice, U.S. EPA, and State of Colorado, which was terminated by court-approved stipulation on December 20, 2024.

Wirth entered into a pre-arranged stock trading plan on November 6, 2024, during an open insider trading window. DE_10K_2025-12-18_full.txt.document_context: Deere & Company filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025, covering its business and operations as of the 2025 reporting period. The excerpt says Deere has manufactured agricultural equipment since 1837 and operates through four segments: Production & Precision Agriculture, Small Agriculture & Turf, Construction & Forestry, and Financial Services.

sections.Item 1B: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, of its 2025 Form 10-K that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding regulatory issues or pending comment resolution with the Securities and Exchange Commission at the time of filing.
sections.Preamble: Deere & Company (Delaware, IRS No. 36-2382580), headquartered at One John Deere Place in Moline, Illinois, filed this Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended November 2, 2025 under Commission File Number 1-4121. The filing states that Deere is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, has complied with Exchange Act reporting and EDGAR submission requirements, and lists its NYSE-registered securities as common stock (DE) and 6.55% Debentures Due 2028 (DE28).
sections.Item 1A: Deere & Company says its financial results are highly dependent on the cyclical agricultural market and broader macroeconomic conditions, which can weaken demand for equipment and services, restrict customer access to funding, and raise Deere’s own funding costs. The risk factors highlight farm income, commodity and protein prices, world grain stocks, acreage, crop yields, farmland values, debt levels, financing costs, international trade, and government farm programs and policies as key drivers of demand for Deere agricultural equipment. The section also notes that downturns in the agricultural industry and uneven competition between multinational and domestic companies can materially hurt Deere’s business and financial performance.
sections.Item 1: Deere & Company (John Deere), incorporated in Delaware in 1958 and manufacturing agricultural equipment since 1837, describes its business in Item 1 as organized into four segments: Production & Precision Agriculture (PPA), Small Agriculture & Turf (SAT), Construction & Forestry (CF), and Financial Services (John Deere Financial or FS). The section lists major products across these segments, including 4WD/track and row crop tractors, harvesters, cotton pickers, specialty and compact tractors, hay and forage equipment, utility vehicles, riding lawn and commercial mowing equipment, backhoe loaders, crawler dozers, skid steers, excavators, and related equipment. It also emphasizes that the 10-K contains forward-looking statements subject to risks and uncertainties, with amounts presented in millions of U.S. dollars and references to Item 1A Risk Factors and Item 7 MD&A for additional cautionary disclosures.
sections.Item 4: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on December 18, 2025. The section contains no mine safety operations, citations, or related disclosures, indicating Deere had no reportable mine safety matters under Part II of the filing.
sections.Item 1C: Deere & Company says cybersecurity is an integral part of its enterprise risk management program, using a risk-based, multi-layered strategy to identify, protect, detect, respond to, and recover from cyber threats while protecting the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of critical systems and information. Governance is led by a global Cybersecurity Team under the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), with oversight from the Digital Risk Governance Council (DRGC), the Audit Review Committee (ARC) of the Board of Directors, and the full Board, and the company reports quarterly trends, strategic initiatives, and metrics to the ARC. Deere also works with third parties to assess cybersecurity maturity and references industry best practices and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) framework in evaluating its program.
sections.Item 7A: Deere & Company (DE) states that it is exposed to market risks including interest rate risk and currency exchange rate risk and actively manages these exposures. The section directs readers to Management’s Discussion and Analysis, “Financial Instrument Market Risk Information,” and Note 26 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for additional detail.
sections.Item 2: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) says its global properties are primarily used for manufacturing, marketing, parts distribution, warehousing, research and development, and administration, and that all properties are in good condition with sufficient capacity to meet current and anticipated manufacturing needs. As of November 2, 2025, Deere’s equipment operations owned and operated 23 U.S./Canada factory locations and leased and operated 4 more for manufacturing, plus 12 distribution facilities, while outside North America it owned or leased and operated 45 factory locations and 13 distribution facilities across multiple countries. The section highlights major manufacturing sites and business segments including John Deere Augusta Works, Davenport Works, Des Moines Works, Dubuque Works, Harvester Works, Turf Care, John Deere Iberica, Kleemann GmbH, Vögele AG, and John Deere Pune Works, spanning SAT, PPA, and CF operations.
sections.Item 7: Item 7, Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations, introduces Deere & Company’s discussion of its financial condition and operating results in the 2025 Form 10-K. This section serves as the primary narrative overview for Deere (NYSE: DE) and directs readers to the detailed MD&A content that follows.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Deere & Company’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive discussion, financial data, or named business developments. The section is marked “[RESERVED]” only, so it provides no additional information on Deere, its products, operations, regulators, or performance metrics.
sections.Item 9: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K. The section reports no changes in accountants and no disagreements with auditors or other accounting and financial disclosure issues.
sections.Item 8: Deere & Company’s Item 8, Financial Statements and Supplementary Data section in its 2025 Form 10-K directs readers to the Consolidated Financial Statements, related notes, and supplementary data. This section serves as the gateway to Deere’s audited annual financial reporting and supporting disclosures for the fiscal year ended 2025.
sections.Item 5:
Deere & Company’s common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “DE,” and the company reported 15,503 holders of record as of November 28, 2025. Deere noted its long-standing quarterly cash dividend policy, but future dividends depend on earnings, capital needs, financial condition, and Board discretion.  

The company also described a December 2022 share repurchase authorization of up to $18.0 billion, with 17.1 million shares still available to repurchase and $7.9 billion remaining at the end of the fourth quarter of 2025; no repurchases were made during the quarter, aside from 4,000 shares withheld to cover payroll taxes on restricted stock vesting. Deere also disclosed an August 5, 2025 issuance of 13,656 shares under the Deere & Company Nonemployee Director Stock Ownership Plan (NEDSOP), exempt under Section 4(a)(2) of the Securities Act and
sections.Item 3: Deere & Company says the FTC and the Attorneys General of Illinois and Minnesota, later joined by Arizona, Michigan, and Wisconsin, sued it on January 15, 2025 in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Illinois Western Division, alleging monopolization and unfair competition under federal and state antitrust laws and seeking injunctive relief to give equipment owners and independent repair providers access to John Deere repair tools and resources. Deere filed a motion to dismiss that was denied, is in preliminary discussions with the FTC about a possible resolution, and says it cannot predict the outcome or impact of the case. The company also faces other unresolved legal actions and investigations, including product liability, asbestos-related liability, employment, patent, trademark, and class action antitrust matters, but currently believes the reasonably possible losses would not materially affect its financial statements.
sections.Item 9B: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) reported no “Other Information” for Item 9B in its 2025 10-K annual report filed on December 18, 2025. The section states that there were no director or executive officer trading arrangements disclosed.
sections.Item 9C: Deere & Company’s Item 9C disclosure states that the section is “Not applicable,” indicating the company is not subject to any foreign jurisdiction that prevents inspections for purposes of this 10-K. The filing is otherwise positioned within Part III of the annual report and contains no additional substantive facts, metrics, regulators, or named foreign jurisdictions in this section.
sections.Item 11: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that the information required by Item 11, Executive Compensation, will be provided in its proxy statement to be filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). This section contains no substantive compensation disclosure in the 10-K itself and instead cross-references the forthcoming proxy statement for executive pay details.
sections.Item 10: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that director information required by Item 10 will be provided in the definitive proxy statement for its 2026 annual meeting of stockholders, to be filed with the SEC, while executive officer information appears in Item 1 under “Information about our Executive Officers.” The company also discloses a code of ethics covering its executives, including the principal executive officer, principal financial officer, and principal accounting officer, and says its governance policies and the charters of the Audit Review, Corporate Governance, Compensation, and Finance committees are posted on Deere’s website and available free in print.
sections.Item 12: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that the information required by Item 12, “Security Ownership of Certain Beneficial Owners and Management and Related Stockholder Matters,” will be provided in the company’s proxy statement to be filed with the SEC. sections.Item 9A: Deere & Company (DE) reported that its principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of November 2, 2025 under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), and management likewise concluded internal control over financial reporting was effective using the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013). The company stated its controls are designed to provide reasonable assurance over U.S. GAAP financial reporting, noted inherent limitations in any internal control system, and said there were no material changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of 2025. An independent registered public accounting firm, registered with the PCAOB and auditing under PCAOB standards, issued an audit report on the effectiveness of Deere’s internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 13: Deere & Company (NYSE: DE) states that the information required by Item 13, covering certain relationships and related transactions as well as director independence, will be provided in its proxy statement to be filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). sections.Item 14: Deere & Company’s Item 14 states that the required disclosure on principal accountant fees and services, including aggregate fees billed by Deloitte & Touche LLP (PCAOB ID No. 34), is not included in the 10-K itself and will instead be provided in the company’s proxy statement filed with the SEC. This section is a brief Part IV placeholder directing readers to the forthcoming proxy statement for the detailed auditor fee information.
sections.Item 16: Deere & Company’s Form 10-K summary for the 2025 annual report (filed December 18, 2025) begins the filing’s Table of Contents and Part I, serving as the opening navigation section rather than substantive disclosure. sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Deere & Company’s 2025 Form 10-K lists the consolidated financial statements for fiscal years ended November 2, 2025, October 27, 2024, and October 29, 2023, including consolidated income, comprehensive income, balance sheets, cash flows, stockholders’ equity, and notes, with page references to the report’s financial statements section. It also identifies the exhibits section, notes that certain long-term borrowing instruments representing less than 10% of total assets are omitted under Item 601(b)(4)(iii)(A) of Regulation S-K subject to SEC request, and states that schedules I through V are omitted because the required conditions are absent.

DIS_10K_2022Q4_2022-11-29_full.txt.document_context: The Walt Disney Company filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2022-10-01. The company operates in two segments, Disney Media and Entertainment Distribution and Disney Parks, Experiences and Products, and says COVID-19 continued to affect operations through capacity restrictions, closures, and higher health and safety costs.

sections.Preamble: The Walt Disney Company’s Form 10-K is an annual report filed with the SEC under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 for the fiscal year ended October 1, 2022, with Commission File Number 001-38842 and principal executive offices at 500 South Buena Vista Street, Burbank, California 91521. Disney, a Delaware corporation with IRS Employer Identification Number 83-0940635, lists its common stock ($0.01 par value) under ticker DIS on the New York Stock Exchange, states that it has no Section 12(g) securities, and identifies itself as a well-known seasoned issuer and large accelerated filer that has filed all required reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1: The Walt Disney Company is a diversified worldwide entertainment company operating through two segments, Disney Media and Entertainment Distribution (DMED) and Disney Parks, Experiences and Products (DPEP), and its Item 1 business description emphasizes the company’s global operations across film, television, streaming, theme parks, resorts, cruises, guided tours, and consumer products. The section highlights the ongoing impact of COVID-19 and its variants on operations and financial results, including park and resort closures, suspended cruise sailings and tours, delayed or canceled theatrical releases, production disruptions, capacity restrictions, and added health and safety costs, while noting resumed operations since May 2020 and a focus on human capital objectives such as attracting, retaining, and developing talent through competitive pay, benefits, inclusion, and internal mobility.
sections.Item 1A: Disney says COVID-19 and future pandemics remain a major risk to The Walt Disney Company’s revenue and operations, with the most severe impacts on Disney Parks, Experiences and Products (DPEP) and Disney Media and Entertainment Distribution (DMED), including theme park and resort closures, suspended cruise sailings and guided tours, delayed or canceled theatrical releases, and disrupted film and television production. The filing also warns that ongoing health measures, capacity restrictions, international park and theater closures, and changing consumer behavior could accelerate the decline of Linear Networks, while mitigation actions such as additional indebtedness and project delays may themselves have negative effects.
sections.Item 3: The Walt Disney Company states that, as disclosed in Note 14 to the Consolidated Financial Statements, it is involved in certain legal matters, and that disclosure is incorporated by reference into Item 3. Disney, together with in some cases certain directors and officers, is a defendant in various legal actions involving copyright, breach of contract, and other claims arising from its businesses, but management does not expect any material liability from these proceedings.
sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 2 describes a global portfolio of owned and leased properties supporting Corporate, Disney Media and Entertainment Distribution (DMED), and Disney Parks, Experiences and Products (DPEP), with parks and resorts and media library assets referenced elsewhere in Item 1. Significant real estate includes major office, production, technical, warehouse, theater, transmitter, retail, and residential sites in Burbank, Los Angeles, New York, Bristol, Emeryville, and San Francisco, plus multiple sites across the U.S. and Canada and in Europe, Asia, Australia, and Latin America, with notable subleased space to third-party tenants in Burbank, New York, and San Francisco.
sections.Item 1B: The Walt Disney Company reported that it had received no written comments from the staff of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regarding its periodic or current reports that were issued 180 days or more before the end of fiscal 2022 and remained unresolved. This Item 1B disclosure indicates there were no outstanding SEC staff comment issues as of the 2022 annual report.
sections.Item 4: Disney states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in this 2022 Form 10-K. The section then identifies the Company’s executive officers as of November 20, 2022, including Robert A. Iger as Chief Executive Officer (appointed effective November 20, 2022), Robert A. Chapek as former CEO through November 20, 2022, Christine M. McCarthy as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, Horacio E. Gutierrez as Senior Executive Vice President and General Counsel, Paul J. Richardson as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Human Resources Officer, and Kristina K. Schake as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Communications Officer. It also notes Iger’s prior service as CEO and Executive Chairman, Chapek’s leadership of Disney Parks, Experiences and Products, and Gutierrez’s prior role as Chief Legal Officer at Spotify Technology S.A. (Spotify).
sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker “DIS,” and as of October 1, 2022 it had approximately 793,000 common shareholders of record. Disney paid a $1.6 billion dividend in fiscal 2020 related to second-half fiscal 2019 operations, but paid no dividend for fiscal 2020, fiscal 2021, or fiscal 2022 operations. During the quarter ended October 1, 2022, Disney purchased 75,841 shares on the open market at a weighted average price of $108.48 per share to provide shares for the Walt Disney Investment Plan, with no shares bought under a publicly announced repurchase program because the company no longer has a stock repurchase plan.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of The Walt Disney Company’s 2022 Form 10-K introduces the Financial Statements and Supplementary Data section and directs readers to the Index to Financial Statements and Supplemental Data on page 63. This section serves as the gateway to Disney’s audited financial statements and related supplemental disclosures for the fiscal year ended 2022.
sections.Item 9: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2022 Form 10-K. This section states “None,” indicating no disputes or related matters to disclose for the annual report filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 7: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 7 MD&A reports consolidated fiscal 2022 results versus 2021, with total revenues rising 23% to $82.722 billion as Services revenue increased to $74.200 billion and Products revenue to $8.522 billion. Operating performance improved despite higher costs and expenses, with income from continuing operations before taxes up to $5.285 billion, net income attributable to Disney up 58% to $3.145 billion, and diluted EPS from continuing operations increasing to $1.75, while restructuring and impairment charges fell to $237 million and other income turned to a $667 million expense.
sections.Item 9A: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that its disclosure controls and procedures under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 are designed to ensure required information is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time, and that it is communicated to certifying officers, senior management, and the Board of Directors. Based on an evaluation as of October 1, 2022, the principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded these controls were effective under Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), and Disney reported no material changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of fiscal 2022.
sections.Item 9B: Item 9B, Other Information, states “None” in The Walt Disney Company’s 2022 Form 10-K, indicating no additional material disclosures in this section. The filing also introduces Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” under Part III, but the excerpt contains no substantive discussion or named foreign jurisdictions, regulators, or related metrics.
sections.Item 7A: The Walt Disney Company says it is exposed to interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, commodity fluctuations, and changes in the market values of its investments, and it uses established policies and financial instruments to manage those risks. Disney primarily uses interest rate swaps to manage borrowings and targets fixed-rate debt within policy ranges, while using forward contracts, option strategies, and cross-currency swaps to hedge foreign exchange exposure in the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Chinese yuan, and Canadian dollar, generally for forecasted exposures of up to four years. The company states these hedges are intended to reduce volatility in earnings and cash flows and lower borrowing costs, though economic or political conditions in some countries may limit its ability to hedge or repatriate cash.
sections.Item 11: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 11 incorporates by reference the executive compensation disclosures in its 2023 Proxy Statement, including the “Director Compensation” and “Executive Compensation” sections. The filing expressly excludes the “Compensation Committee Report,” which is deemed furnished by reference, and the “Letter from the Compensation Committee” from this incorporation.
sections.Item 10: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 10 incorporates by reference governance disclosures from its 2023 proxy statement, including Section 16(a) compliance, the Audit Committee, the company’s code of ethics, director backgrounds, director nominations, governing documents, and the director selection and election process. It also notes that information on executive officers is provided in Part I of the 2022 Form 10-K, consistent with General Instruction G(3).
sections.Item 12: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 12 incorporates by reference the “Stock Ownership” and “Equity Compensation Plans” disclosures from its 2023 Proxy Statement, covering security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management. This section points readers to the proxy statement for details on Disney’s equity compensation arrangements and related stockholder matters.
sections.Item 14: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 14 in Part IV incorporates by reference the 2023 Proxy Statement sections titled “Auditor Fees and Services” and “Policy for Approval of Audit and Permitted Non-Audit Services.” This section indicates that Disney’s disclosure on principal accounting fees and services, including audit and permitted non-audit services, is contained in the proxy statement rather than repeated in the Form 10-K.
sections.Item 13: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 13 states that information on “Certain Relationships and Related Person Transactions” and “Director Independence” is incorporated by reference from the 2023 Proxy Statement. This section does not provide substantive transaction details in the 10-K itself, but directs readers to the proxy statement for related-party dealings and board independence disclosures.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of The Walt Disney Company’s 2022 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, with the section text showing only “[Reserved]” and a table of contents reference. This section does not provide additional financial, operational, or regulatory information beyond the placeholder entry.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 lists The Walt Disney Company’s exhibits and financial statement schedules for the 2022 Form 10-K, cross-referencing the Index to Financial Statements and Supplemental Data on page 63. It includes corporate governance documents such as the Restated Certificate of Incorporation, amended bylaws, and Certificate of Elimination of Series B Convertible Preferred Stock, plus debt-related agreements including the Senior Debt Securities Indenture with Wells Fargo Bank, N.A. and the 2019 Indenture with Citibank, N.A. as trustee. The section also notes that certain long-term borrowing instruments are omitted under Item 601(b)(4)(iii) of Regulation S-K, with Disney undertaking to furnish copies to the SEC upon request.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 is largely administrative, listing the Form 10-K Summary, Signatures, and Consolidated Financial Information for The Walt Disney Company (DIS), and it includes Disney’s cautionary note on forward-looking statements under the Securities Act of 1933 and Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The disclosure says future results may differ materially due to restructuring, strategic initiatives, capital investments, content and IP spending, pricing and cost decisions, competition, consumer acceptance, advertising demand on direct-to-consumer services and linear networks, health concerns, and broader economic, regulatory, political, military, and technological developments.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” states that The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) is not applicable to this disclosure in its 2022 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on November 29, 2022. The section appears as a brief transition into Part III of the annual report and contains no additional substantive information, metrics, or foreign jurisdiction inspection issues.

DIS_10K_2023Q3_2023-11-21_full.txt.document_context: The Walt Disney Company filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended September 30, 2023.
