sections.Preamble: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) filed its Form 10-K annual report with the SEC for the fiscal year ended September 30, 2023, under Commission File Number 001-38842. The filing identifies Disney as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 500 South Buena Vista Street, Burbank, California 91521, with common stock registered under Section 12(b) on the New York Stock Exchange and no securities registered under Section 12(g). Disney also indicates it is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, and that it has filed all required Exchange Act reports and submitted all required Interactive Data Files during the prior 12 months.
sections.Item 1: The Walt Disney Company is a diversified worldwide entertainment company operating in three segments—Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences—and its Item 1 discussion emphasizes human capital as a core business priority. As of September 30, 2023, Disney employed approximately 225,000 people globally (about 167,000 in the U.S. and 58,000 outside the U.S.), with roughly 77% full-time, 16% part-time, and 7% seasonal workers, many of whom are covered by collective bargaining agreements or represented by works councils, trade unions, or other employee associations. Disney says its HR programs focus on attracting, retaining, and developing talent through competitive pay and benefits, inclusive culture initiatives, internal mobility, and technology investments to support a diverse, high-performing workforce.
sections.Item 3: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it is engaged in certain legal matters, and refers readers to Note 14 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for the detailed disclosure. This Item 3 section does not describe specific lawsuits, regulators, products, or quantified exposures, but incorporates the Note 14 legal proceedings disclosure by reference.
sections.Item 1A: Disney’s Item 1A risk factors say that declines in U.S., global, or regional economic conditions, including recession and inflation, can reduce demand and raise costs across its Parks, Experiences and Products, advertising-supported media, cable programming, home entertainment, and consumer products businesses. The company warns that higher interest rates and weaker consumer spending could hurt attendance, MVPD payments, DTC subscription and ad revenue, borrowing costs, and the execution of plans to expand the Experiences segment, realign costs, and grow DTC ad-supported services.
sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company and its subsidiaries own and lease a global portfolio of properties supporting Corporate, Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences operations, with major sites in Burbank, Los Angeles, New York, Bristol, Emeryville, and San Francisco, plus multiple locations across the U.S., Canada, Europe, Asia, Australia, and Latin America. The section highlights significant office, production, technical, theater, warehouse, transmitter, retail, and residential facilities, including 201 acres and 4,694,000 square feet of owned property in Burbank, 117 acres and 1,174,000 square feet in Bristol for Sports, and leased space that includes portions rented to third-party tenants.
sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol “DIS,” and the company reports approximately 768,000 common shareholders of record as of September 30, 2023. This Item 5 section addresses the market for Disney’s common equity, related stockholder matters, and issuer purchases of equity securities.
sections.Item 7: Disney’s Item 7 MD&A presents consolidated results for fiscal 2023 versus 2022 and 2021, with total revenues rising 7% to $88.9 billion driven by Services revenue of $79.6 billion and Products revenue of $9.3 billion. Operating performance was pressured by higher restructuring and impairment charges of $3.9 billion, while net income attributable to The Walt Disney Company fell 25% to $2.354 billion and diluted EPS declined to $1.29 from $1.75, despite lower interest expense and improved other income.
sections.Item 1B: The Walt Disney Company reported that it had received no written comments from the staff of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on its periodic or current reports that were issued 180 days or more before the end of fiscal 2023 and remained unresolved. This Item 1B disclosure indicates there were no outstanding SEC staff comment issues as of the 2023 fiscal year-end.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of The Walt Disney Company’s 2023 Form 10-K introduces the Financial Statements and Supplementary Data section, directing readers to the Index to Financial Statements and Supplemental Data on page 77. This section serves as the gateway to Disney’s audited financial statements and related supplemental disclosures for the fiscal year ended 2023.
sections.Item 7A: The Walt Disney Company says it is exposed to interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, commodity price changes, and changes in the market values of its investments, and it uses established policies and financial instruments to manage those risks. Disney primarily uses interest rate swaps to manage borrowing costs and fixed-rate debt targets, and uses forward contracts, option strategies, and cross-currency swaps to hedge foreign exchange exposure on assets, liabilities, commitments, and forecasted revenues and expenses. The principal currencies hedged are the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Chinese yuan, and Canadian dollar, with hedge coverage generally maintained for forecasted exposures for periods not exceeding four years.
sections.Item 9: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2023 Form 10-K. The section states “None,” indicating no disputes, restatements, or related issues to disclose for the annual report filed with the SEC on November 21, 2023.
sections.Item 9B: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) reported in Item 9B that none of its directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement, as defined in Item 408(c) of Regulation S-K, during the quarterly period covered by the report. The section also references Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” and marks the transition to Part III of the 10-K filed on 2023-11-21.
sections.Item 9A: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it has established disclosure controls and procedures under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 to ensure information required in SEC reports is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time and communicated to certifying officers, senior management, and the Board of Directors. Based on evaluation as of September 30, 2023, the principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded the disclosure controls and procedures, as defined in Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), were effective, and Disney reported no material changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of fiscal 2023.
sections.Item 4: Item 4 states that Disney’s Mine Safety Disclosures are “not applicable,” indicating the company does not operate mine-related businesses subject to those reporting requirements. The section then identifies Disney’s executive officers as Robert A. Iger (Chief Executive Officer), Kevin A. Lansberry (Interim Chief Financial Officer), Horacio E. Gutierrez (Senior Executive Vice President, General Counsel and Chief Compliance Officer), Sonia L. Coleman (Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Human Resources Officer), and Kristina K. sections.Item 14: Item 14 of The Walt Disney Company’s 2023 Form 10-K incorporates by reference the 2024 Proxy Statement sections titled “Auditor Fees and Services” and “Policy for Approval of Audit and Permitted Non-Audit Services.” This section is part of Part IV and points readers to the proxy statement for details on principal accounting fees, audit fees, and permitted non-audit services related to Disney’s independent auditor.

The filing states that Disney’s common stock, $0.01 par value, is listed on the New York Stock Exchange, that it has no securities registered under Section 12(g), and that it is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.
sections.Item 1: The Walt Disney Company is a diversified worldwide entertainment company operating through three segments: Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences, with the excerpt focused on Entertainment’s global film and episodic content production and distribution businesses. Key Entertainment assets and brands include ABC Television Network, Disney, Freeform, FX, National Geographic (73% owned), Star, a 50% equity stake in A+E Television Networks, and direct-to-consumer services Disney+, Disney+ Hotstar, and Hulu, alongside content sales/licensing, theatrical distribution, home entertainment, Stage Plays, music distribution, and post-production services from Industrial Light & Magic and Skywalker Sound.
sections.Item 1A: The Walt Disney Company says that declines in U.S., global, or regional economic conditions, including recessions, slower growth, and inflation, could materially reduce demand for its products and services and raise operating costs across its businesses. The risk factor highlights potential pressure on guest spending at Parks and Resorts, advertising on DTC and linear platforms, MVPD payments for cable programming, subscription levels, licensing fees, and consumer products, while also noting elevated labor, borrowing, and financing costs that could affect Disney’s investment plans and business realignment.
sections.Item 3: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it is engaged in certain legal matters, and refers readers to Note 14 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for the detailed disclosure. This Item 3 legal proceedings section incorporates by reference the Note 14 discussion of the company’s pending or potential litigation and related legal contingencies.
sections.Item 1B: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it received no written comments from the staff of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regarding its periodic or current reports that were issued 180 days or more before the end of fiscal 2024 and remain unresolved. This Item 1B section indicates there were no outstanding SEC staff comment issues as of the 2024 annual report filed on 2024-11-14.
sections.Item 4: Item 4 states that Mine Safety Disclosures are not applicable for The Walt Disney Company, and the section instead provides information about the company’s executive officers. It identifies Robert A. Iger as Chief Executive Officer, Hugh F. Johnston as Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, Horacio E. Gutierrez as Chief Legal and Compliance Officer, Sonia L. Coleman as Chief Human Resources Officer, and Kristina K. Schake as Chief Communications Officer, with appointment dates ranging from 2022 to 2023.
sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 2 describes a global portfolio of owned and leased properties across the Corporate, Entertainment, Experiences, and Sports segments, with major concentrations in Burbank, Los Angeles, New York, Bristol, Emeryville, and San Francisco. Key holdings include 201 acres and 4,733,000 square feet of owned land/buildings in Burbank, 117 acres and 1,078,000 square feet in Bristol, and multiple leased office, production, technical, theater, warehouse, retail, and residential sites across the U.S., Canada, Europe, Asia, Australia, and Latin America, including space leased to third-party tenants.
sections.Item 1C: The Walt Disney Company says its cybersecurity risk management program is integrated into overall enterprise risk management and is informed by the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and other global standards, using layered defenses such as intrusion detection and prevention, multi-factor authentication, encryption, endpoint protection, automated monitoring, vulnerability scanning, patch management, and threat detection/response tools. Disney also conducts penetration tests, security audits, tabletop exercises, employee privacy and information security training, and third-party assessments and due diligence for service providers, with outside counsel and cybersecurity experts supporting regulatory compliance and risk management. The company maintains an incident response plan led by an internal incident response team, with escalation paths to senior management, law enforcement, and the Cybersecurity Incident Disclosure Subcommittee of the Risk Management Committee.
sections.Item 7A: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) says it is exposed to interest rate changes, foreign currency fluctuations, commodity price swings, and changes in the market value of its investments, and it uses established policies and financial instruments to manage those risks. Disney primarily uses interest rate swaps to manage borrowings and targets fixed-rate debt within policy limits, while using forward contracts, option strategies, and cross-currency swaps to hedge foreign exchange exposure in the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Chinese yuan, and Canadian dollar, generally for forecasted exposures of up to four years. The company says these hedges are intended to reduce volatility in earnings and cash flows and lower borrowing costs, though economic or political conditions in some countries may limit hedging or cash repatriation.
sections.Item 9: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) reports no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure for the 2024 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company’s common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under ticker DIS, and as of September 28, 2024 it had approximately 734,000 common shareholders of record. During the quarter ended September 28, 2024, Disney repurchased 5,011,000 shares at an average price of $92.49 per share under a share repurchase program effective February 7, 2024, which authorizes repurchases of up to 400 million shares with no expiration date and had 372 million shares remaining available.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of The Walt Disney Company’s 2024 Form 10-K introduces the Financial Statements and Supplementary Data section and directs readers to the Index to Financial Statements and Supplemental Data on page 67. sections.Item 7: Disney’s Item 7 MD&A for fiscal 2024 highlights consolidated results showing total revenues of $91.361 billion, up 3% from $88.898 billion in 2023, with services revenue of $81.841 billion and products revenue of $9.520 billion. Net income attributable to The Walt Disney Company rose to $4.972 billion from $2.354 billion, and diluted EPS increased to $2.72 from $1.29, driven by higher income before taxes despite $3.595 billion of restructuring and impairment charges, $1.260 billion of net interest expense, and $1.796 billion of income taxes.
sections.Item 9A: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that its disclosure controls and procedures are designed to ensure information required under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 is recorded, processed, summarized, and reported on time, and that it reaches certifying officers, senior management, and the Board of Directors for timely disclosure decisions. Based on an evaluation as of September 28, 2024, the principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded these controls were effective under Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), and Disney reported no material changes in internal control over financial reporting during the fourth quarter of fiscal 2024.
sections.Item 9B: The Walt Disney Company disclosed a Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement adopted on August 14, 2024 by Chief Executive Officer and Director Robert A. Iger under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The plan covers the potential exercise of vested stock options granted on December 18, 2014, which expire on December 18, 2024, and the sale of up to 372,412 shares of Disney common stock, excluding shares used for cashless exercise or tax withholding, with termination scheduled for December 17, 2024 subject to early termination.
sections.Item 10: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that governance disclosures on Section 16(a) compliance, the Audit Committee, the code of ethics, director backgrounds, and director nominations are incorporated by reference from its 2025 Proxy Statement, including the sections “Delinquent Section 16(a) Reports,” “The Board of Directors,” “Committees,” and “Corporate Governance Documents.” Disney also says it has adopted an insider trading compliance policy and program for directors, officers, employees, and the company itself, designed to promote compliance with insider trading laws, SEC-related rules and regulations, and New York Stock Exchange listing standards, with the full policy attached as Exhibit 19.

sections.Item 1: The Walt Disney Company is a diversified worldwide entertainment company operating in three segments: Entertainment, Sports and Experiences, with the Entertainment segment covering global film and episodic content production, distribution, and related businesses. Key Entertainment assets include ABC Television Network, Disney, Freeform, FX, National Geographic (73% owned), a 50% equity investment in A+E Global Media, and direct-to-consumer services Disney+ and Hulu, plus content sales/licensing, theatrical distribution, TV/VOD and home entertainment, Broadway stage plays, music distribution, and post-production services from Industrial Light & Magic and Skywalker Sound.
sections.Item 1B: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it received no written comments from the staff of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) on its periodic or current reports that were issued 180 days or more before the end of fiscal 2025 and remain unresolved. This Item 1B disclosure indicates there were no outstanding SEC staff comment issues as of the 2025 annual report.
sections.Item 1A: The Walt Disney Company says declines in U.S., global, or regional economic conditions, including recessions, slowdowns, and inflation, could reduce demand and raise costs across its businesses, hurting revenue, earnings, and financing flexibility. The risk is especially acute for Disney’s parks and experiences, cable programming, and direct-to-consumer (DTC) services, where lower attendance, weaker subscription levels, reduced advertising and licensing fees, and higher labor, borrowing, and operating costs could impair planned investments, pricing actions, and strategic initiatives.
sections.Item 3: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that it is engaged in certain legal matters, and refers readers to Note 14 to the Consolidated Financial Statements for the detailed disclosure. sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company and its subsidiaries own and lease a global portfolio of properties supporting Corporate, Entertainment, Experiences, and Sports operations, with major U.S. hubs in Burbank, Los Angeles, New York, Bristol, Emeryville, and San Francisco. The section highlights significant office, production, technical, warehouse, theater, transmitter, retail, and residential facilities, including 4,733,000 square feet of owned property in Burbank, 1,078,000 square feet in Bristol for Sports, and multiple leased sites across the USA, Canada, Europe, Asia, Australia, and Latin America.
sections.Item 1C: The Walt Disney Company says its cybersecurity risk management program is integrated into overall enterprise risk management and is informed by the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and other global standards, using layered defenses such as intrusion detection and prevention, multi-factor authentication, encryption, endpoint protection, automated monitoring, vulnerability scanning, patch management, network segmentation, and threat detection/response tools. Disney also conducts penetration tests, security audits, tabletop exercises, employee privacy and information security training, and third-party assessments, while using intelligence from law enforcement, government agencies, private-sector networks, cybersecurity firms, and outside counsel to manage regulatory compliance and third-party service provider risks. The company maintains a cybersecurity incident response plan (CIRP) that assigns an internal incident response team to assess, investigate, and remediate events.

During the quarter ended March 29, 2025, Disney repurchased 9,437,968 shares at an average price of $105.52 per share, including 2,856,000 shares in December 2024–January 2025, 2,047,300 shares in February 2025, and 4,534,668 shares in March 2025, leaving 355 million shares available under the program; amounts exclude the 1% excise tax on stock repurchases imposed by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.
sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company reported that none of its directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement or other trading plan during the quarter, as defined by Item 408(c) of Regulation S-K. Disney also announced an agreement with Miral LLC, a UAE company, to create a Disney-branded theme park and resort in Abu Dhabi that Miral will build and operate, with Disney licensing IP and providing development and management services in exchange for royalties tied to project revenues and service fees, while contributing no capital and awaiting final agreements.
sections.Item 4: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) reports that its disclosure controls and procedures under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 were evaluated as of March 29, 2025 and concluded to be effective by the principal executive officer and principal financial officer. Disney also states there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the second quarter of fiscal 2025 that materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, internal controls.
sections.Item 1A: Disney says its businesses are highly exposed to shifts in consumer tastes and preferences across entertainment, travel, and consumer products, including the performance of DTC platforms, broadcast, cable, theatrical releases, theme parks, resorts, cruise ships, hotels, and travel experiences. The company warns that demand for out-of-home entertainment, including moviegoing, has not returned to pre-pandemic levels, that many offerings depend on acceptance outside the U.S., and that it must make large upfront investments in content, sports and programming rights, attractions, and facilities before knowing whether consumers will respond.
sections.Item 6: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 6 includes its standard forward-looking statement cautionary note for the 2025 Form 10-Q, citing Sections 27A of the Securities Act of 1933 and 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Disney says its projections about financial results, business plans, new services and products, expenditures, impairments, seasonality, capital allocation, share repurchases, and dividends are subject to risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ materially, including restructuring and strategic initiatives, cost rationalization, content and IP investment decisions, and broader economic and competitive pressures.

sections.Preamble: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) filed its Form 10-Q for the quarterly period ended June 28, 2025, with the SEC under Commission File No. 001-38842, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 500 South Buena Vista Street, Burbank, California 91521. The filing states Disney is a large accelerated filer, not a shell company or emerging growth company, and reports 1,797,933,833 shares of common stock outstanding as of July 30, 2025.
sections.Item 1: The Walt Disney Company (DIS) states that, as disclosed in Note 13 to the Condensed Consolidated Financial Statements, it is engaged in certain legal matters and related proceedings. The Item 1 — Business section incorporates by reference the Note 13 disclosure concerning these legal matters, with no additional details provided in this excerpt.
sections.Item 4: The Walt Disney Company (NYSE: DIS) states that its disclosure controls and procedures under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 are designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and reporting of required SEC information, and that this information is communicated to senior management and the Board of Directors. Based on an evaluation as of June 28, 2025, Disney’s principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded these controls were effective under Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), and the company reported no material changes in internal control over financial reporting during the third quarter of fiscal 2025.
sections.Item 3: Item 3 is a brief cross-reference section stating that The Walt Disney Company’s market risk disclosures are discussed in Item 2, Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations, and in Note 15 to the Condensed Consolidated Financial Statements. It does not provide substantive legal proceedings or market risk detail in this excerpt, but directs readers to the company’s broader 10-Q financial statement and MD&A disclosures for those topics.
sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company reported share repurchases under its Section 12 registered equity securities program for the quarter ended June 28, 2025, buying 7,117,675 shares at an average price of $98.70 per share. Purchases were made in three monthly periods—March 30 to April 30, May 1 to May 31, and June 1 to June 28, 2025—and were all executed under Disney’s February 7, 2024 share repurchase program, which authorizes repurchases of up to 400 million common shares with no expiration date. After these repurchases, 348 million shares remained available for buyback, and the disclosed amounts exclude the 1% excise tax on stock repurchases imposed by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.
sections.Item 1A: Disney’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that The Walt Disney Company’s entertainment, travel, and consumer products businesses depend on rapidly changing consumer tastes, including demand for content distributed via DTC platforms, broadcast, cable, theaters, theme parks, resorts, cruise ships, hotels, and consumer products. The company warns that out-of-home entertainment demand, especially theater-going, has not returned to pre-pandemic levels, that many offerings rely on acceptance outside the U.S., and that evolving tourist preferences and competition from new content-delivery technologies could hurt profitability. Disney also highlights the risk of making large upfront investments in content production and acquisition, sports and programming rights, and park or resort assets before knowing whether consumers will embrace them.
sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company disclosed Rule 10b5-1 trading plans adopted by CEO and Director Robert A. Iger and Chief Human Resources Officer Sonia L. Coleman, covering sales of up to 272,331 and 18,955 gross shares of Disney common stock, respectively, with plan expirations on December 16, 2025 and July 31, 2026. Disney also reported that ESPN and the National Football League reached a non-binding agreement for ESPN to acquire the NFL Network, NFL RedZone Channel pay TV distribution, and NFL Fantasy in exchange for a 10% noncontrolling interest in ESPN, subject to definitive agreements, regulatory approvals, and other closing conditions. If completed, Disney would hold an effective 72% interest in ESPN, with Hearst at 18% and the NFL at 10%.
sections.Item 6: This Item 6 section of The Walt Disney Company’s Form 10-Q is a cautionary note on forward-looking statements under Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933 and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, warning that future financial and operating results may differ materially from expectations. It highlights risks tied to business plans, new products and services, capital expenditures, investments, transactions requiring regulatory or other approvals, impairments and amortization, competition, seasonality, capital allocation, and share repurchases and dividends, with outcomes affected by restructuring, strategic initiatives, pricing, cost structure, IP investments, and broader economic conditions.

sections.Item 2: The Walt Disney Company reported share repurchases under its Section 12-registered equity securities program for the quarter ended December 27, 2025, buying 18,463,192 shares at an average price of $112.06 per share. sections.Item 1A: The Walt Disney Company’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize exposure to renewal and renegotiation of long-term programming and distribution contracts, including agreements for sports and other programming rights and carriage deals with multichannel video programming distributors (MVPDs). Based on an evaluation as of December 27, 2025, the principal executive officer and principal financial officer concluded the controls were effective, and Disney reported no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the first quarter of fiscal 2026 that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, those controls.

sections.Item 5: The Walt Disney Company disclosed a Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement adopted on December 11, 2025 by Horacio E. Gutierrez, its Chief Legal and Public Affairs Officer, intended to satisfy the affirmative defense under Rule 10b5-1(c) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. sections.Item 11: Item 11, Executive Compensation, is marked “Not Applicable” in General Electric Company’s 2015 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on February 27, 2015. The section contains no additional compensation disclosure, metrics, named executives, or related governance details.

sections.Item 13: General Electric Company’s Item 13 states that certain relationships and related transactions, as well as director independence, are not applicable, and notes that GE conducts business from various facilities, most of which are leased. It also points readers to Item 1 for primary facility locations and Note 11 to the consolidated financial statements in Part II, Item 8, while confirming that GE’s common stock is owned entirely by General Electric Company and therefore has no trading market.
sections.Item 1A: GE’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that its future results may differ materially from expectations due to strategic, operational, financial, legal, and compliance risks, with particular focus on global macroeconomic and political conditions, competition, technology and product innovation, and public policy. The company says it operates in virtually every part of the world, serves customers in about 175 countries, and derived approximately 40% of 2014 revenue from outside the United States, making it vulnerable to inflation, recession, currency volatility and controls, sovereign debt defaults, and disruptive political changes. GE also highlights execution risk around acquisitions, joint ventures, dispositions, restructurings, and other strategic objectives, noting that hedging and insurance may be costly and not fully effective.
sections.Item 6: General Electric Company (GE) presents five years of selected financial data for 2010-2014, showing revenues declining from $49.163 billion to $42.725 billion while net earnings attributable to GECC rose to $7.234 billion in 2014 from $2.155 billion in 2010. The table also highlights GECC balance-sheet and leverage metrics, including shareowners’ equity of $87.499 billion, total assets of $500.216 billion, financing receivables net of $237.018 billion, short-term borrowings of $68.780 billion, long-term borrowings of $187.991 billion, and a debt-to-equity ratio of 3.99:1, with return on average shareowners’ equity at 8.60% and earnings-to-fixed-charges at 1.84.
sections.Item 1: General Electric Capital Corporation (GE Capital or GECC), wholly owned by General Electric Company (GE), is a diversified global financial services business offering commercial loans and leases, fleet management, financial programs, credit cards, personal loans, and strategic partnerships in aviation, energy, infrastructure, and healthcare. As of December 31, 2014, GECC employed approximately 47,000 people and operated from Norwalk, Connecticut, while its regulatory status as a savings and loan holding company made it subject to Federal Reserve Board supervision under Dodd-Frank and, since 2013, designation by the U.S. Financial Stability Oversight Council (FSOC) as a nonbank systemically important financial institution (nonbank SIFI). The section also highlights the FRB’s November 25, 2014 proposal for enhanced prudential standards, including capital and liquidity requirements, intercompany transaction rules, risk management and governance standards, and CCAR-style stress testing and capital planning.
sections.Item 7: GE’s 2014 MD&A says consolidated earnings fell to $7.0 billion from $8.0 billion in 2013, driven by dispositions, core declines and lower gains, partly offset by lower impairments and lower provisions for losses on financing receivables. GE Capital (GECC) also reduced ending net investment, excluding liquidity, to $363 billion at December 31, 2014, and the company describes itself as a smaller, more focused finance company with strong positions in commercial mid-market and consumer financing segments. The section also notes that GE uses non-GAAP financial measures with reconciliations in the Supplemental Information section and that acquisitions and dispositions had limited revenue effects but affected net earnings, including a $1.0 billion earnings decrease from dispositions in 2014.
sections.Item 7A: General Electric Company (GE) states that its quantitative and qualitative market risk disclosures are covered through its global risk management framework, with additional detail provided in Part II, Item 7 of the 2015 Form 10-K. The filing directs readers to the Risk Management and Financial Resources and Liquidity and Borrowings sections, including the Funding Plan discussion of exchange rate and interest rate risks, for information on GE’s exposure management.
sections.Item 3: GE’s Item 3 Legal Proceedings discloses 15 mortgage loan repurchase lawsuits involving WMC, its U.S. mortgage business sold in 2007, with adverse parties including securitization trustees such as Deutsche Bank National Trust Company, Law Debenture Trust Company of New York, and US Bank National Association. The cases generally allege breach of contract, indemnification, and declaratory judgment claims seeking loan repurchase or monetary damages, with reported exposures including about $4.3 billion of mortgage loans and more than $1.8 billion in damages in the Deutsche Bank matters and about $800 million of loans and over $425 million in alleged losses in the Law Debenture case. GE also notes prior settlements beginning in Q4 2013, district court rulings denying or partially denying dismissal motions, and a Minnesota state-court petition and proposed settlement for the US Bank cases, with bondholder objections and an evidentiary hearing scheduled for June 2015.
sections.Item 8: General Electric Company’s Item 8 financial statements section includes management’s annual report on internal control over financial reporting, stating that GE’s internal control was evaluated as of December 31, 2014 under the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) and concluded to be effective. The section also includes the independent registered public accounting firm’s audit report for General Electric Capital Corporation (GECC), covering the 2014 and 2013 statements of financial position and the related earnings, comprehensive income, equity, and cash flow statements for the three-year period ended December 31, 2014.
sections.Item 9A: General Electric Company (GE), under the direction of its Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, evaluated its disclosure controls and procedures and internal control over financial reporting and concluded that the disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of December 31, 2014. GE also reported that no change in internal control over financial reporting occurred during the quarter ended December 31, 2014 that materially affected, or was reasonably likely to materially affect, those controls, with the related management report and independent registered public accounting firm report included in Part II, Item 8 of the Form 10-K.
sections.Item 15: This section describes the independent audit of GE Capital Corporation (GECC)’s consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2014, with management responsible for the statements and control environment and the auditors responsible for expressing opinions on both. The audit was conducted under Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (United States) standards and used the COSO Internal Control – Integrated Framework (2013), including testing of financial statement amounts, disclosures, accounting estimates, and the design and operating effectiveness of internal controls to assess the risk of material misstatement or material weakness.
sections.Item 9B: General Electric Company (GE) disclosed an amended support agreement with GE Capital Corporation (GECC) under which GE makes payments to GECC to ensure GECC and its consolidated affiliates maintain an earnings-to-fixed-charges ratio of at least 1.10:1 for each GECC fiscal year, a requirement in place since fiscal 1991. Effective February 24, 2015, the amendment excludes non-cash goodwill and intangible asset charges from the ratio calculation because those charges are excluded from regulatory capital calculations, and the amended agreement is filed as Exhibit 10.
sections.Item 14: General Electric Company (GE) disclosed principal accounting fees and services paid to KPMG LLP for 2014 and 2013, totaling $33.9 million and $37.1 million, respectively. The table breaks out audit fees ($25.7 million in 2014 vs. $30.1 million in 2013), audit-related fees ($7.2 million vs. $5.7 million), and tax fees ($1.0 million vs. $1.3 million), and explains these categories under SEC rules, including Form 10-K and 10-Q audits, internal control over financial reporting under Sarbanes-Oxley Section 404, merger and acquisition due diligence, employee benefit plan audits, and tax compliance/advice/planning.

GS_10K_2025-02-27_full.txt.document_context: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024. The excerpt says the firm serves a large, diversified client base and operates through three business segments: Global Banking & Markets, Asset & Wealth Management, and Platform Solutions.

sections.Preamble: Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (NYSE: GS) filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 200 West Street, New York, NY 10282. The filing lists registered securities including common stock, multiple series of depositary shares tied to floating-rate preferred stock, capital securities of Goldman Sachs Capital II and III, and medium-term notes of GS Finance Corp., and notes that Goldman Sachs is not a well-known seasoned issuer but has filed all required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1B: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that it had no material unresolved written comments from the SEC staff received 180 days or more before fiscal year-end relating to its periodic or current reports under the Exchange Act. This Item 1B disclosure indicates there were no outstanding SEC staff comment issues requiring resolution as of the 2025 10-K filing.
sections.Item 1: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (Group Inc.) describes itself as a leading global financial institution serving corporations, financial institutions, governments, and individuals, with a “One Goldman Sachs” strategy aimed at delivering services more accessibly and efficiently across businesses and product areas. The firm is a Delaware bank holding company and financial holding company regulated by the Federal Reserve (FRB), with Goldman Sachs Bank USA as its New York State-chartered bank subsidiary, and it reports three segments: Global Banking & Markets, Asset & Wealth Management, and Platform Solutions. Global Banking & Markets generates revenue from investment banking fees, FICC and equities intermediation and financing, relationship lending and acquisition financing, while Asset & Wealth Management earns management, incentive, private banking, lending, and investment fees, and Platform Solutions earns from consumer platforms and transaction banking.
sections.Item 1C: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) directs readers to its “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations — Risk Management — Cybersecurity Risk Management” disclosure in Part II, Item 7 of the 2025 Form 10-K for additional information on cybersecurity. This Item 1C section itself contains no substantive cybersecurity details beyond cross-referencing the company’s broader risk management discussion.
sections.Item 3: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) says it is involved in numerous judicial, regulatory, and arbitration proceedings arising from its business activities, with many matters still in early stages and seeking indeterminate damages. The firm estimates the upper end of reasonably possible aggregate loss where possible and believes unresolved matters will not materially harm its financial condition, though they could materially affect operating results in a given period and keep litigation expenses elevated.
sections.Item 1A: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) says its businesses are exposed to substantial risks from global financial market and broader economic conditions, including declining asset values, market volatility, inflation, investor uncertainty, and poor performance or lower-fee preferences in its asset management and wealth management products. The firm also highlights liquidity and funding risks tied to disruptions in credit and debt capital markets, reduced access to credit, higher borrowing costs, and potential adverse effects from credit rating downgrades or wider credit spreads. As a holding company, The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. depends on payments and loans from subsidiaries that may be restricted by legal and regulatory limits on upstreaming funds.
sections.Item 4: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or regulatory disclosures involving the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) or related entities.
sections.Item 2: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. reports a global office footprint of approximately 11.0 million square feet across the Americas, Europe/Middle East/Africa, and Asia/Australia/New Zealand, with about 6.3 million square feet in the Americas, 1.8 million in EMEA, and 2.9 million in Asia-Pacific. Its principal executive offices at 200 West Street, New York, occupy about 2.1 million square feet on land leased from the Battery Park City Authority under a ground lease expiring in June 2069, with a $161 million lump-sum ground rent payment made in June 2007; other key sites include Plumtree Court in London (about 826,000 square feet, terminable in 2039) and approximately 1.9 million square feet in India, where most leases begin expiring in 2028.
sections.Item 5: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. common stock trades on the NYSE under the symbol “GS,” and as of February 7, 2025 the company had 5,251 holders of record. The section reports fourth-quarter 2024 repurchases of 3,531,667 shares at average prices of $523.45 in October, $582.31 in November, and $598.52 in December, leaving $16.2 billion of authorized repurchases remaining under the Board’s 2023 $30 billion share repurchase program. Repurchases are made primarily through open-market purchases, including Rule 10b5-1 plans and accelerated share repurchases, and are influenced by Goldman Sachs’ capital position, regulatory capital requirements, market conditions, and the trading price and volume of GS stock.
sections.Item 7: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (Group Inc.), founded in 1869 and headquartered in New York, describes itself as a leading global financial institution serving corporations, financial institutions, governments and individuals through three segments: Global Banking & Markets, Asset & Wealth Management, and Platform Solutions. The Item 7 MD&A also notes that Group Inc. is a Delaware bank holding company and financial holding company regulated by the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System (FRB), and that the discussion includes forward-looking statements under the U.S. Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995 that may differ materially from actual results, liquidity, capital actions, and financial condition.
sections.Item 9: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) states that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants regarding accounting or financial disclosure during the last two years. This Item 9 disclosure in the 2025 Form 10-K indicates continuity in the company’s external audit relationship and no reported accounting disputes.
sections.Item 7A: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that its quantitative and qualitative disclosures about market risk are provided in “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations — Risk Management” in Part II, Item 7 of the 2025 Form 10-K. This Item 7A section serves as a cross-reference rather than presenting standalone market risk metrics, directing readers to the company’s broader risk management discussion.
sections.Item 8: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that management is responsible for maintaining effective internal control over financial reporting, designed under the supervision of its principal executive and principal financial officers to provide reasonable assurance over the reliability of financial reporting and preparation of U.S. GAAP financial statements. Based on a COSO Internal Control – Integrated Framework (2013) assessment as of December 31, 2024, management concluded the controls were effective, and PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP (PCAOB ID 238) audited the controls and issued an unqualified opinion on their effectiveness.
sections.Item 9A: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. reports that management, with the participation of its Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, evaluated the effectiveness of disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(e) and concluded they were effective as of the end of the period covered by the 2024 annual report. The company also states that no change in internal control over financial reporting, as defined by Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(f), occurred during the fourth quarter of the year ended December 31, 2024 that materially affected, or is reasonably likely to materially affect, those controls, with the related management report and independent registered public accounting firm report included in Part II, Item 8.
sections.Item 9C: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) states in Item 9C that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is not applicable. The section serves as a brief Part III filing marker and contains no additional substantive disclosure, metrics, or named regulators beyond the SEC EDGAR annual report context.
sections.Item 9B: During the quarter ended December 2024, Goldman Sachs Group Inc. reported that no directors or executive officers entered into, modified, or terminated any contracts, instructions, or written plans for the sale or purchase of Group Inc.’s securities intended to satisfy Rule 10b5-1 affirmative defense conditions. The company also stated that there were no non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangements, as defined in Item 408 of Regulation S-K, involving its directors or executive officers during the period.
sections.Item 10: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) states that information on its executive officers is provided in Part I, Item 1, while details on directors, the audit committee, audit committee financial experts, and shareholder director-nomination procedures will be included in the 2025 Proxy Statement for the 2025 Annual Meeting of Shareholders and incorporated by reference. The section also references the Code of Business Conduct and Ethics for senior financial officers and describes an insider trading policy covering directors, officers, employees, other covered persons, and Group Inc. itself, designed to promote compliance with insider trading laws, regulations, and New York Stock Exchange listing standards, with the policy filed as Exhibit 19.1.
sections.Item 13: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that information on certain relationships and related transactions, as well as director independence, will be provided in the 2025 Proxy Statement and is incorporated into this Form 10-K by reference. This Item 13 section contains no additional substantive disclosure beyond directing readers to the proxy statement for details on related-party matters and board independence.
sections.Item 11: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that detailed executive officer and director compensation information, including matters relating to the Board’s compensation committee, will be provided in the 2025 Proxy Statement and is incorporated into this 2024 Form 10-K by reference. This Item 11 section contains no substantive compensation figures or policy discussion in the filing excerpt, and instead directs readers to the forthcoming proxy disclosure for Goldman Sachs and its Board of Directors.
sections.Item 12: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. states that security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management will be provided in the 2025 Proxy Statement and incorporated by reference into the 2024 Form 10-K. As of December 31, 2024, the company reported 18,300,116 shares issuable under outstanding restricted stock units (RSUs), with no outstanding options and no weighted average exercise price applicable, and 55,703,255 shares remaining available for future issuance under equity compensation plans approved by security holders. The section also explains that RSU shares are subject to vesting and tax withholding, and that forfeited, terminated, canceled, or withheld shares may return to availability under Goldman Sachs’ current stock incentive plan (SIP).
sections.Item 14: Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS) states that information regarding principal accountant fees and services will be provided in the 2025 Proxy Statement and is incorporated into this Form 10-K by reference. This Item 14 section is part of Part IV of the annual report and contains no additional fee amounts, auditor names, or service details in the excerpt.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 lists Goldman Sachs Group Inc.’s consolidated financial statements (included in Part II, Item 8) and a detailed exhibit index for the 2025 Form 10-K, including the Plan of Incorporation, Restated Certificate of Incorporation amended February 12, 2025, Amended and Restated By-Laws amended November 3, 2023, and the description of securities registered under Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. It also references multiple debt and senior debt indentures involving Goldman Sachs, GS Finance Corp., The Bank of New York, and The Bank of New York Mellon, with many exhibits incorporated by reference from prior Form S-1, Form 8-K, Form 10-Q, Form 8-A, and Form S-3 filings.

HD_10K_2025-03-21_full.txt.document_context: The Home Depot, Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for its fiscal year ended January 2025. The excerpt is from the Risk Factors section and says the company’s business, results of operations, cash flows, financial condition, and prospects are subject to numerous risks and uncertainties that could negatively affect the trading value of its securities.

sections.Item 1A: The Home Depot Inc. states that its business, results of operations, cash flows, financial condition, and prospects are subject to numerous risks and uncertainties, and that investors should consider these risk factors together with the company’s consolidated financial statements, related notes, and other SEC filings. The company warns that both known and unknown risks, including those it currently deems immaterial, could materially harm operations and reduce the trading value of its securities.
sections.Item 7: Home Depot’s Item 7 MD&A describes its two core customer segments—DIY consumers and professional customers (“Pros”)—and the company’s efforts to serve them through in-store associates, online resources, clinics and workshops, a dedicated Pro sales force, Pro Xtra loyalty, enhanced credit, and an extensive delivery network. It highlights fiscal 2024’s acquisition of SRS, which sells to specialty trade roofers, landscapers, and pool contractors, and notes ongoing investment to adapt to changing DIY preferences and expand growth with Pros across residential, multifamily, hospitality, and commercial end markets.
sections.Preamble: The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) files this Form 10-K annual report under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 for the fiscal year ended February 2, 2025, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation with IRS Employer Identification No. 95-3261426 and principal executive offices at 2455 Paces Ferry Road, Atlanta, Georgia 30339. The filing states that Home Depot’s common stock, $0.05 par value, trades on the New York Stock Exchange under symbol HD, that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and large accelerated filer, and that it has timely filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files with the SEC.
sections.Item 8: The Home Depot Inc. says its highly competitive, fragmented, and evolving home improvement market includes brick-and-mortar, multichannel, and online rivals such as other home improvement retailers, hardware stores, building materials suppliers, lumber yards, specialty design stores, mass digital retailers, warehouse clubs, and MRO distributors. The company emphasizes competition on customer experience, price, product availability and innovation, store location and merchandising, and delivery/pickup speed and cost, noting that internet-enabled price transparency and comparison shopping increase competitive pressure.
sections.Item 1B: The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) states in Item 1B, “Unresolved Staff Comments,” that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff for its fiscal 2024 Form 10-K. This section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no outstanding review issues or regulatory follow-up items were reported.
sections.Item 1: The Home Depot, Inc. is the world’s largest home improvement retailer by fiscal 2024 net sales, offering building materials, home improvement, lawn and garden, décor, and facilities MRO products through stores and e-commerce, plus installation and tool rental services. As of fiscal 2024 it operated 2,347 stores across the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, along with distribution and fulfillment centers and websites, and it completed the acquisition of SRS, a specialty trade distributor with over 780 branch locations serving roofing, landscape, and pool contractors. The company is a Delaware corporation headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia, and says its strategy in fiscal 2024 focused on executing amid weak home improvement demand, high interest rates, and macroeconomic uncertainty.
sections.Item 1C: Home Depot says its cybersecurity risk management is integrated into enterprise risk management and guided by the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and applicable Payment Card Industry Data Security Standards, with day-to-day oversight by its Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) and cybersecurity team. The company describes layered monitoring and mitigation controls including targeted audits, penetration tests, annual third-party maturity and risk assessments, a 24/7 security operations center, incident response procedures, data classification and retention policies, and cybersecurity insurance to reduce losses from significant security incidents.
sections.Item 4: The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) states in Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, that the disclosure is “Not applicable” in its Fiscal 2024 Form 10-K. This section indicates the company did not have mine safety reporting obligations under SEC mine safety rules for the period ended 2024.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, financial data, or operational discussion. This section provides no additional information on the company’s business, results, risk factors, or SEC-regulated metrics.
sections.Item 3: The Home Depot Inc. says it is involved in ordinary-course legal proceedings but does not believe any current matter will have a material adverse effect on its consolidated financial position, results of operations, or cash flows, and it uses a $1 million SEC disclosure threshold for environmental proceedings. The section highlights an April 2021 civil consent decree with the U.S. Department of Justice, EPA, and the states of Utah, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island covering lead-safe work practices in the installation services business, as well as EPA discussions in fiscal 2023 over stipulated penalties tied to third-party installer documentation violations. It also notes a Washington State Department of Ecology notice over sales of a refrigerant restricted in 2022, which led to an approximately $1.6 million civil penalty that Home Depot resolved and paid.
sections.Item 2: At the end of fiscal 2024, The Home Depot Inc. reported 356.5 million square feet in its Primary segment, with stores 89% owned and 11% leased, distribution and fulfillment centers and warehouses 97% leased, and offices/other facilities 73% leased; the company said it operated over 500 distribution and fulfillment centers and warehouses and over 780 SRS branch locations, most of them leased. The Store Support Center corporate headquarters is in Atlanta, GA, and the filing details a broad U.S. store footprint totaling 2,025 stores across all 50 states plus Puerto Rico, Guam, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and the District of Columbia, along with additional stores in Canada and Mexico.
sections.Item 5: The Home Depot Inc. common stock has been listed on the NYSE under the symbol “HD” since April 19, 1984, and the company has paid a cash dividend every quarter since its first dividend on June 22, 1987, with future dividends subject to Board approval based on earnings, capital needs, and financial condition. As of March 5, 2025, Home Depot had approximately 101,000 record holders and 5,678,000 additional street-name holders, and its five-year total shareholder return reached 203.78 versus 227.91 for the S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary Distribution & Retail Index and 202.40 for the S&P 500 Index. In fiscal Q4 2024, the company repurchased 8,166, 3,650, and 2,694 shares at average prices of $411.08, $422.79, and $395.74, respectively, with
sections.Item 9: The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) states in Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” that there were no such changes or disagreements for fiscal 2024. The section is marked “Not applicable” in the company’s 2025 Form 10-K filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 7A: Home Depot says it manages market risk primarily through interest rate swaps on its long-term debt portfolio, with $6.0 billion of floating-rate debt principal at February 2, 2025, about 12% of its senior notes portfolio, and swap agreements in a $795 million aggregate liability position; a 1 percentage point rise in floating-rate debt rates would increase annual interest expense by about $60 million. The company also faces foreign currency exchange risk from translating foreign operations and purchasing goods in local currencies, but its year-end fiscal 2024 hedging arrangements were not material, and it does not use derivatives for commodity price risk, instead relying on pricing actions amid inflation/deflation and competitive constraints.
sections.Item 9A: The Home Depot Inc. states that its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(e) were effective as of the end of the reporting period, with CEO and CFO oversight ensuring timely recording, processing, and communication of information required in SEC filings. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective as of February 2, 2025 under the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) and in accordance with GAAP, while excluding SRS, acquired June 18, 2024, from the assessment under SEC staff guidance; SRS represented about 7% of consolidated total assets excluding goodwill and intangible assets and about 4% of consolidated net sales for fiscal 2025.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C of The Home Depot, Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K states that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is not applicable. The section serves as a brief transition into Part III of the annual report and contains no additional substantive disclosure, metrics, or named regulators beyond the SEC filing context.
sections.Item 9B: During the fiscal quarter ended February 2, 2025, The Home Depot, Inc. reported that no director or executive officer adopted or terminated any “Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” or “non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement,” as defined in Item 408(a) of SEC Regulation S-K. This Item 9B disclosure indicates no changes to insider trading plans by the company’s directors or executive officers during the quarter.
sections.Item 11: The Home Depot, Inc. states that the Item 11 executive compensation disclosure in its 2025 Form 10-K is incorporated by reference to the Proxy Statement sections titled “Executive Compensation,” “Director Compensation,” and “Leadership Development and Compensation Committee Report.” The company expressly excludes the Proxy Statement’s “Executive Compensation – Pay Versus Performance” section from incorporation by reference.
sections.Item 12: The Home Depot, Inc. states that the Item 12 disclosure on security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management, as well as related stockholder matters, is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement. Specifically, the filing points readers to the sections titled “Beneficial Ownership of Common Stock” and “Executive Compensation – Equity Compensation Plan Information” for the required ownership and equity compensation plan information.
sections.Item 10: The Home Depot, Inc. (NYSE: HD) Item 10 describes directors, executive officers, and corporate governance, with much of the governance information incorporated by reference from the 2025 Proxy Statement sections on “Election of Directors,” “Corporate Governance,” “Executive Compensation,” “General,” and “Audit Committee Report.” It states that executive officers serve at the pleasure of the Board and profiles key leaders including William D. Bastek, Executive Vice President – Merchandising; Jordan Broggi, Executive Vice President – Customer Experience and President – Online; and Ann-Marie Campbell, Senior Executive Vice President, highlighting their tenure, prior roles, and long careers with The Home Depot and related businesses such as HD Supply.
sections.Item 14: The Home Depot Inc. (NYSE: HD) states that the disclosure required by Item 14 regarding principal accountant fees and services is incorporated by reference to the Proxy Statement section titled “Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm’s Fees.” This Item 14 section in Part IV of the 2025 Form 10-K does not provide fee amounts or additional details in the filing excerpt itself, instead directing readers to the company’s Proxy Statement for the relevant information.

It is signed by Edward P. Decker (Chair, President and Chief Executive Officer), Richard V. McPhail (Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer), Kimberly R. Scardino (Senior Vice President — Finance, Chief Accounting Officer and Controller), and the board of directors, including Gerard J. Arpey, Ari Bousbib, Jeffery H. Boyd, Gregory D. Brenneman, J. Frank Brown, Wayne M. Hewett, Manuel Kadre, Stephanie C. Linnartz, Paula A. Santilli, and Caryn Seidman-Becker.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 lists The Home Depot, Inc.’s incorporated financial statements and schedules for the 2025 Form 10-K, including KPMG LLP’s audit report, consolidated balance sheets as of February 2, 2025 and January 28, 2024, and consolidated earnings, comprehensive income, stockholders’ equity, and cash flows for fiscal 2024, 2023, and 2022. It states that all financial statement schedules are omitted as inapplicable or already covered in the financial statements and notes, and that exhibits are incorporated by reference to prior SEC filings under File No. 1-8207, with copies available from Investor Relations in Atlanta. The section also notes that Home Depot is not filing instruments evidencing indebtedness because no single instrument exceeds 10% of consolidated total assets, and it references exhibits including a March 27, 2024 merger agreement involving Star Acquisition Merger Sub Inc., Shingle Acquisition Holdings, Inc., and

This section does not provide substantive disclosure in the 10-K itself, but directs readers to the company’s proxy materials for details on related-party transactions and director independence.

IBM_10K_2025-02-25_full.txt.document_context: International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) filed a 10-K annual report for fiscal year 2024, as indicated by the excerpt from IBM’s 2024 Annual Report to Stockholders. The filing highlights IBM’s strategy to create value through hybrid cloud and AI, supported by software, consulting, and mission-critical infrastructure, and notes strategic partnerships with companies including Adobe, AWS, Microsoft, Oracle, Salesforce, Samsung Electronics, and SAP.

sections.Item 1B: International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. The section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating the company had no outstanding staff review issues in this 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed on 2025-02-25.
sections.Item 1A: IBM’s Item 1A risk factors highlight exposure to macroeconomic weakness and reduced client spending budgets, which could pressure revenue and profit if demand for IBM products and solutions declines. The company also emphasizes execution risk around innovation and commercialization in hybrid cloud, AI and generative AI, quantum, and other disruptive technologies, including the need to attract a strong developer ecosystem and scale new offerings in a highly competitive market. IBM further warns that brand damage from client disputes, product defects, internal control deficiencies, delivery failures, cybersecurity incidents, government investigations, or legal proceedings could hurt its ability to retain customers, talent, and partners, while heavy investment in growth areas carries uncertainty around adoption rates, economic models, and shifting customer preferences.
sections.Item 1: IBM, incorporated in New York in 1911 and renamed in 1924, describes itself as a globally integrated enterprise focused on helping clients drive digital transformation through hybrid cloud and artificial intelligence (AI), modernize applications, and improve operational efficiency. The company emphasizes its software, consulting services, and mission-critical infrastructure capabilities, supported by a leading research organization, and notes strategic partnerships with Adobe, Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft, Oracle, Palo Alto Networks, Salesforce, Samsung Electronics, and SAP. IBM also highlights intense global competition across its business segments, with hundreds of competitors and competition based on technology innovation, performance, price, quality, brand, breadth of offerings, talent, and client relationships.
sections.Preamble: IBM’s 2025 Form 10-K states that portions of its definitive Proxy Statement, to be filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission and delivered to stockholders for the Annual Meeting of Stockholders on April 29, 2025, are incorporated by reference into Part III of the report. The excerpt is a filing preamble and table-of-contents marker for International Business Machines Corp.’s annual report, highlighting SEC reporting linkage and the upcoming shareholder meeting date.
sections.Item 3: IBM’s Item 3, Legal Proceedings, does not describe new litigation in this excerpt but instead incorporates by reference Note Q, “Commitments & Contingencies,” from IBM’s 2024 Annual Report to Stockholders. The referenced disclosure on pages 95 to 97 is the primary source for IBM’s legal proceedings, commitments, and contingent liabilities.
sections.Item 2: IBM’s corporate headquarters are at an owned site in Armonk, New York, and as of December 31, 2024 the company owned or leased approximately 41 million square feet of facilities worldwide. Of that total, about 17 million square feet were in the U.S. (roughly 9 million owned and 8 million leased) and about 24 million square feet were outside the U.S. (roughly 3 million owned and 21 million leased), supporting sales and distribution, manufacturing, development, data processing services including cloud centers, research, and administrative functions across all business segments.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of International Business Machines Corp. (IBM)’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, financial data, or discussion of operations, risks, or metrics. The section is marked “[Reserved]” only, with no named entities, products, or regulatory matters beyond IBM and the SEC filing context.
sections.Item 1C: IBM says cybersecurity is integrated into its enterprise risk management framework and overseen by the Board of Directors and Audit Committee, with senior management responsible for ongoing assessment of cyber exposure. The company uses a multi-layered program based on the National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework, including policies, technical controls, a Security Operations Center, the IBM Security X-Force Exchange threat-intelligence platform, third-party security tools, and periodic external reviews and certifications. IBM also relies on its Computer Security Incident Response Team (CSIRT), workforce security awareness training, and a third-party supplier risk management program to manage threats across networks, devices, servers, applications, data, cloud solutions, and vendors.
sections.Item 4: International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 25, 2025. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or related metrics, and no additional regulators or entities are discussed.
sections.Item 5: IBM common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange and NYSE Chicago under the symbol “IBM,” with 348,544 common stockholders of record as of February 10, 2025. For the fourth quarter of 2024, IBM reported no share repurchases, and the remaining authorized capacity under its common stock repurchase program was $2,007,611,768. The Board authorized the $4.0 billion repurchase program on October 30, 2018, it has no expiration date, and IBM notes the program was suspended at the time of the Red Hat closing in 2019.
sections.Item 7: IBM’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations in this 2025 Form 10-K incorporates by reference pages 6 through 42 of IBM’s 2024 Annual Report to Stockholders. The section directs readers to IBM’s broader discussion of financial condition, operating results, and related performance trends contained in that annual report.
sections.Item 9: International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) states in Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” that there were no such changes or disagreements during the period. JNJ_10K_2023Q4_2024-02-16_full.txt.document_context: Johnson & Johnson filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2023-12-31. The company reported approximately 131,900 employees worldwide and said that, following the August 2023 separation of its Consumer Health business (Kenvue), it is now organized into two segments: Innovative Medicine and MedTech. The excerpt also highlights ongoing exposure to extensive government regulation and significant legal proceedings, including product liability, patent, antitrust, and consumer protection claims.

sections.Preamble: Johnson & Johnson filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023 with the SEC under Commission file number 1-3215, identifying itself as a New Jersey corporation headquartered at One Johnson & Johnson Plaza, New Brunswick, NJ 08933, with IRS Employer Identification No. 22-1024240 and telephone number (732) 524-0400. The filing lists JNJ common stock and several NYSE-listed debt securities, including 0.650% Notes due May 2024 (JNJ24C), 5.50% Notes due November 2024 (JNJ24BP), 1.150% Notes due November 2028 (JNJ28), and 1.650% Notes due May 2035 (JNJ35), and indicates the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.
sections.Item 7: Johnson & Johnson’s Item 7 MD&A describes the Innovative Medicine segment, which focuses on immunology, infectious diseases, neuroscience, oncology, cardiovascular and metabolism, and pulmonary hypertension, with medicines distributed to retailers, wholesalers, distributors, hospitals, and healthcare professionals. Key products include REMICADE, SIMPONI, SIMPONI ARIA, STELARA, TREMFYA, EDURANT, PREZISTA, PREZCOBIX/REZOLSTA, SYMTUZA, CONCERTA, INVEGA SUSTENNA/XEPLION, and INVEGA TRINZA/TREVICTA, covering major indications such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, HIV/AIDS, ADHD, schizophrenia, and schizoaffective disorder.
sections.Item 1A: Johnson & Johnson’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that investing in its common stock or debt securities involves significant business, industry, and operational uncertainties that could materially harm results of operations or financial condition. The company highlights intense competition across its two operating segments and all geographic markets, including competition on cost, innovation, intellectual property, pricing, reimbursement, and access to acquisitions, collaborations, and licensing, as well as risks from patent exclusivity loss for Innovative Medicine products and from integrating acquired businesses.
sections.Item 1B: Johnson & Johnson (NYSE: JNJ) states in Item 1B, “Unresolved Staff Comments,” that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. The section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no outstanding regulatory correspondence or disclosure issues for this annual report filed on 2024-02-16.
sections.Item 1: Johnson & Johnson (incorporated in New Jersey in 1887) is a global healthcare holding company with about 131,900 employees, operating in virtually all countries and focused on human health and well-being through research, manufacture, and sale of healthcare products. Following the August 2023 separation of Consumer Health into Kenvue, the company is organized into two segments—Innovative Medicine and MedTech—overseen by the Executive Committee and senior management at U.S. and international subsidiaries. The filing also highlights material regulatory and legal risks, including U.S. FDA approval and clearance requirements, supply-chain compliance pressures, and significant litigation involving product liability, patent disputes, antitrust, unfair trade practices, and consumer protection claims.
sections.Item 3: Item 3, Legal Proceedings, for Johnson & Johnson (NYSE: JNJ) incorporates by reference the disclosure in Note 19, Legal proceedings, of the Notes to Consolidated Financial Statements in Item 8 of the 2024 Form 10-K. The section itself contains no substantive litigation details and instead directs readers to the financial statement note for information on the company’s legal matters.
sections.Item 2: Johnson & Johnson’s subsidiaries operate 61 manufacturing facilities totaling approximately 9.8 million square feet, with 5,026 thousand square feet supporting Innovative Medicine and 4,782 thousand square feet supporting MedTech. By geography, the facilities are concentrated in the United States (23 facilities, 2,973 thousand square feet) and Europe (20 facilities, 4,900 thousand square feet), with additional sites in the Western Hemisphere excluding the U.S. and in Africa, Asia and Pacific. The company also maintains numerous office and warehouse facilities worldwide, generally prefers to own manufacturing properties rather than lease them, and uses some leased and contract-manufactured facilities.
sections.Item 1C: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) says it maintains documented cybersecurity policies and standards, uses security tools for protection, detection and response, operates a cybersecurity incident response plan, and manages third-party risks through a structured program covering data security and supply chain. The company also requires employee training on phishing, email security and data privacy, periodically uses third-party experts for maturity assessments, and states it is not aware of any cybersecurity incident that has had or is reasonably likely to have a material impact, though it warns of potential reputational, operational, financial and regulatory harm from evolving threats.
sections.Item 5: As of February 9, 2024, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) reported 118,772 record holders of common stock and incorporated related stockholder and equity compensation disclosures by reference to Note 16 and Item 12 of the 10-K. The company’s Board of Directors had previously authorized a $5.0 billion share repurchase program on September 14, 2022, which was completed in the first quarter of 2023. During the fiscal fourth quarter of 2023, J&J repurchased 1,390,000 shares in open-market transactions at average prices of $147.61 and $156.76 per share, primarily through a systematic plan to support compensation programs.

It lists key named executives including Chairman and CEO Joaquin Duato, CFO Joseph J. Wolk, General Counsel Elizabeth Forminard, Chief Human Resources Officer Peter M. Fasolo, and leaders of Innovative Medicine, MedTech, and Global Corporate Affairs, noting they are elected annually by the Board of Directors and that no family relationships or selection arrangements exist among them.
