sections.Item 1: Johnson & Johnson, incorporated in New Jersey in 1887, is a global healthcare holding company with about 138,100 employees engaged in research, development, manufacture, and sale of products focused on human health and well-being across virtually all countries. The company’s Chief Executive Officer serves as the Chief Operating Decision Maker, and its Executive Committee oversees strategy for its two operating segments, Innovative Medicine and MedTech, through U.S. and international operating companies. The section also highlights material regulatory and legal risk, including U.S. FDA approval and clearance requirements, changing government laws and directives, supply chain and manufacturing delays, and significant litigation involving product liability, patent disputes, antitrust, unfair trade practices, and consumer protection claims.
sections.Item 1A: Johnson & Johnson’s Item 1A emphasizes that investment in its common stock or debt securities carries significant business, operational, and financial risks that may be difficult or impossible to predict or eliminate, even with the Company’s risk management efforts. The filing highlights intense competition across its two operating segments and global markets, including competition on cost, technology, intellectual property, product performance, pricing, reimbursement, and access to acquisitions, collaborations, and licensing agreements, which could pressure earnings and increase investment and acquisition costs. It also warns that competitors’ superior products or patent positions, acquisition integration failures, and loss of patent exclusivity in Innovative Medicine could materially reduce sales and hinder new product development and commercialization.
sections.Item 7: Johnson & Johnson’s Item 7 MD&A describes the Innovative Medicine segment, which focuses on immunology, infectious diseases, neuroscience, oncology, cardiovascular and metabolism, and pulmonary hypertension, with products distributed to retailers, wholesalers, distributors, hospitals, and healthcare professionals. Key named products include REMICADE, SIMPONI, SIMPONI ARIA, STELARA, TREMFYA, EDURANT, PREZISTA, PREZCOBIX/REZOLSTA, SYMTUZA, CONCERTA, INVEGA SUSTENNA/XEPLION, and INVEGA TRINZA/TREVICTA, spanning treatments for rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, HIV/AIDS, ADHD, schizophrenia, and related conditions.
sections.Item 1C: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) says it maintains documented cybersecurity policies and standards, uses security tools for protection, detection and response, and operates a formal incident response plan, third-party risk management program, and mandatory employee training on phishing, email security, and data privacy. The company also periodically uses third-party experts to assess the maturity of its information security program and states it is not aware of any cybersecurity incident that has had, or is reasonably likely to have, a material impact on its business or operations. JNJ warns that evolving attack techniques and increasingly sophisticated threats could still cause reputational, operational, competitive, financial, or regulatory harm.

sections.Item 1: JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE: JPM), a Delaware financial holding company founded in 1968, is a leading U.S.-based global financial services firm with $4.4 trillion in assets and $362.4 billion in stockholders’ equity as of December 31, 2025. Through the J.P. Morgan and Chase brands, it serves consumers, small businesses, corporations, institutions, and governments via principal subsidiaries including JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A., J.P. Morgan Securities LLC, J.P. Morgan Securities plc, and J.P. Morgan SE, with operations across the U.S., U.K., Germany, and other international markets. sections.Item 2: JPMorgan Chase & Co. states that its headquarters is at 270 Park Avenue in New York City, a 60-story office building owned by the Firm and fully occupied as of December 31, 2025, including by senior management. The company owned or leased 74.2 million square feet of facilities worldwide, with 57.5 million in the United States, 5,083 branches in 48 states and Washington D.C., and major concentrations in New York City, Ohio, Chicago, Texas, Delaware, New Jersey, Arizona, London, India, and the Philippines. KO_10K_2022Q4_2023-02-21_full.txt.document_context: The Coca-Cola Company’s 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022 highlights risk factors that could materially affect future results, including inflation, recession or economic slowdown, commodity and energy price volatility, foreign currency exchange rates, and geopolitical instability. The excerpt says these conditions could reduce consumer demand for Coca-Cola’s beverages, pressure profitability, and disrupt operations through restrictions such as tariffs, import requirements, and limits on transferring earnings or capital across borders.

sections.Preamble: The Coca-Cola Company’s 2022 Form 10-K preamble identifies the registrant as COCA COLA CO, a Delaware corporation headquartered at One Coca-Cola Plaza, Atlanta, Georgia 30313, with Commission File Number 001-02217 and IRS Employer Identification No. 58-0628465. It lists the company’s securities registered on the New York Stock Exchange, including Common Stock (KO) and multiple outstanding notes maturing from 2024 through 2041, and states that Coca-Cola is a well-known seasoned issuer that has timely filed all required Exchange Act reports and electronic interactive data files.
sections.Item 1A: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 1A risk factors warn that unfavorable global economic and geopolitical conditions—such as inflation, recession, unemployment, commodity and energy price volatility, foreign currency exchange rates, trade policies, sovereign debt defaults, civil unrest, war, terrorism, and government changes—could materially hurt demand for its beverages, profitability, liquidity, and overall financial performance. The filing also highlights risks from consumer downtrading to lower-priced or private-label products, product boycotts tied to political activism, and restrictions on cross-border capital transfers, price controls, tariffs, and other government-imposed limits on business activities.
sections.Item 1: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes itself as a global total beverage company whose products have been sold since 1886 and are now available in more than 200 countries and territories through independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers, retailers, and consolidated bottling and distribution operations. Its portfolio includes Trademark Coca-Cola, sparkling flavors, water, sports, coffee and tea, juice, value-added dairy and plant-based beverages, and emerging beverages, with five of the world’s top six nonalcoholic sparkling soft drink brands: Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light. The company says its strategy is guided by “Loved Brands,” “Done Sustainably,” and “For A Better Shared Future,” and it reports operating segments including Europe, Middle East and Africa, Latin America, North America, Asia Pacific, Global Ventures, and Bottling Investments.
sections.Item 7: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 7 MD&A describes its global beverage portfolio and distribution model, highlighting owned brands such as Coca-Cola, Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, Fanta, Sprite, Dasani, smartwater, Powerade, Minute Maid, fairlife, and Topo Chico, as well as participation in Monster Beverage Corporation distribution arrangements. It emphasizes consumer demand-driven portfolio optimization and the Coca-Cola system’s reach through independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers, retailers, and consolidated operations in more than 200 countries and territories, serving about 2.2 billion servings per day. The excerpt also notes beverage volume reporting for the Coca-Cola system, with sales of 32.7 billion and 31.3 billion units referenced in the period.
sections.Item 1B: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) states that Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, is not applicable in its 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on February 21, 2023. This section indicates there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 3: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) says its business is exposed to numerous indirect taxes and fees worldwide, including import duties, tariffs, excise taxes, sales/value-added taxes, sweetened-beverage taxes, packaging taxes, carbon taxes, property taxes, and payroll taxes, and notes that U.S. lawmakers have previously considered a federal excise tax on sugar-, HFCS-, and other nutritive-sweetener beverages. The company warns that new or higher taxes could raise product costs, reduce affordability, and hurt net operating revenues and profitability, while changes to beverage-container and packaging laws could increase costs and reduce demand. Coca-Cola and its bottlers also face evolving regulations on deposits, recycling, recycling content, tethered bottle caps, ecotaxes, product stewardship, and restrictions on plastics and PFAS-containing packaging at federal, state, local, and international levels.
sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 2 — Properties describes its worldwide headquarters on a 35-acre complex in Atlanta, Georgia, plus owned and leased facilities used for administration, manufacturing, processing, packaging, storage, warehousing, distribution, and retail operations across its geographic operating segments, Global Ventures, Bottling Investments, and Corporate. As of December 31, 2022, Coca-Cola reported 32 principal concentrate and/or syrup plants, 93 principal beverage manufacturing/bottling plants, 116 principal distribution and storage facilities, and 1,636 principal retail stores, and management said these facilities are suitable, adequately utilized, and have sufficient capacity with additional output possible through added personnel, equipment, shifts, or expansion.
sections.Item 8: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 8 defines key product categories and trademarks used throughout the 2023 Form 10-K, including concentrates, syrups, fountain syrups, Company Trademark Beverages, and Trademark Coca-Cola beverages such as Coca-Cola, Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light, and Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, along with other brands like Fanta, Sprite, and Simply. It also explains that KO markets, manufactures, and sells both concentrate operations and finished product operations, with finished sparkling soft drinks and other beverages generally generating higher net operating revenues.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of The Coca-Cola Company’s 2023 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure. This section provides no additional financial, operational, regulatory, or product information beyond the placeholder designation.
sections.Item 4: Item 4 of The Coca-Cola Company’s 2023 Form 10-K states that Mine Safety Disclosures are “Not applicable,” indicating the company does not operate mining activities subject to those reporting requirements. The section then transitions to Item X, “Information About Our Executive Officers,” listing named executives and roles as of February 21, 2023, including Manuel Arroyo (Chief Marketing Officer), Henrique Braun (President, International Development), Lisa Chang (Senior Vice President and Chief People Officer), Monica Howard Douglas (Senior Vice President and General Counsel), and Nikolaos Koumettis (President, Europe).
sections.Item 5: The Coca-Cola Company’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker “KO,” and the company notes that future quarterly dividends remain discretionary and depend on earnings, financial condition, business needs, and regulatory considerations. As of February 17, 2023, Coca-Cola reported 187,325 shareowner accounts of record, incorporated its 2023 Proxy Statement equity compensation information by reference, and stated that no unregistered equity securities were sold in 2022. The section also details share repurchases under a publicly announced plan, including 96,440 shares bought in the quarter ended December 31, 2022 at an average price of $63.28 per share, with 139,705,526 shares remaining available under the 2019 authorization.
sections.Item 7A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) says it uses derivative financial instruments only to hedge, not trade, in order to reduce exposure to foreign currency exchange rates, interest rates, commodity prices, and other market risks, and it monitors these risks with sensitivity analysis and other measurement systems. In 2022, Coca-Cola generated $27.6 billion of net operating revenues outside the United States and used forward exchange contracts, foreign currency options, and collars—principally in euro, British pound sterling, and Japanese yen—to hedge forecasted cash flows, monetary assets and liabilities, and net investments in foreign operations. As of December 31, 2022, foreign currency derivatives had total notional value of $11.370 billion, compared with $13.691 billion in 2021, and hedge-accounting derivatives carried a net unrealized loss of $66 million.
sections.Item 9A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) reports that, under the supervision of management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, it evaluated the effectiveness of its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(e) as of December 31, 2022, and concluded they were effective. The section also references management’s report on internal control over financial reporting as of December 31, 2022 and the attestation report of the company’s independent registered public accounting firm.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C of The Coca-Cola Company’s 2023 Form 10-K states that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is “Not applicable.” This section appears at the end of Item 9C and immediately precedes Part III, with no additional substantive discussion of foreign inspection restrictions, regulators, or related operations.
sections.Item 9: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable in its 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. sections.Item 1: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes itself as a total beverage company whose trademarked products have been sold since 1886 and are now available in more than 200 countries and territories, with brands including Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light. sections.Item 1C: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes a multilayered cybersecurity risk management program integrated into its Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework to identify, assess, prioritize, mitigate, and recover from cyber threats such as unauthorized access, data theft, ransomware, malware, and system disruptions. The company says its controls cover computer systems, software, networks, and other technology assets, are benchmarked against the National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework, and include physical, administrative, and technical safeguards, incident triage and remediation processes, internal audit reviews, outside advisors, penetration testing, and tabletop exercises.
sections.Item 3: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 3 discusses legal and regulatory risks tied to indirect taxes and beverage-specific levies, including import duties, tariffs, excise taxes, sales/value-added taxes, packaging taxes, carbon taxes, and proposed U.S. federal excise taxes on sugar-, HFCS-, or other nutritive-sweetener beverages. sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company says its worldwide headquarters is a 35-acre complex in Atlanta, Georgia, with office, technical, engineering, and reception facilities used by Corporate and North America employees, and it also owns or leases facilities worldwide for administrative, manufacturing, processing, packaging, storage, warehousing, distribution, and retail operations. As of December 31, 2023, Coca-Cola reported principal facilities including 31 concentrate and/or syrup plants, 94 beverage manufacturing/bottling plants, 116 distribution and storage facilities, and 1,593 principal retail stores, with major concentrations in Bottling Investments and Global Ventures. Management states these production facilities are suitable and adequate, are being appropriately utilized, and generally have sufficient capacity for current needs, with seasonal demand affecting utilization and additional capacity available through added labor, equipment, shifts, or expansion except for certain dairy products requiring specialized equipment.

sections.Item 5:
The Coca-Cola Company’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “KO,” and as of February 16, 2024 it had 182,362 shareowner accounts of record, excluding many street-name beneficial holders. The company notes that quarterly dividends have historically been paid but future dividends remain at the discretion of the Board of Directors and depend on earnings, financial condition, business needs, and regulatory considerations.  

For the year ended December 31, 2023, Coca-Cola sold no unregistered equity securities and repurchased 18,969,052 shares of common stock at an average price of $57.46 per share, with 18,958,103 shares bought under its publicly announced repurchase plan and 102,852,072 shares remaining available under that plan at year-end; the section also incorporates by reference the “Equity Compensation Plan Information” in the company’s 2024 Proxy Statement filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 9A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) reports that, under the supervision of management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, it evaluated its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(e) and concluded they were effective as of December 31, 2023. sections.Item 9B: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) reported in Item 9B that none of its directors or officers, as defined in Rule 16a-1(f) under the Exchange Act, adopted or terminated any “Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” or “non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” during the fiscal quarter ended December 31, 2023. This disclosure references Item 408 of Regulation S-K and indicates no insider trading plan changes were made in the quarter.
sections.Item 7A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) says it uses derivative financial instruments only to hedge, not trade, in order to reduce exposure to foreign currency exchange rates, interest rates, commodity prices, and other market risks, using sensitivity analysis and other objective measurement systems. In 2023, Coca-Cola generated $29.2 billion of net operating revenues outside the United States and managed most foreign currency exposure on a consolidated basis, supplementing natural offsets with forward exchange contracts, foreign currency options, and collars, principally in euro, British pound, and Japanese yen. As of December 31, 2023, foreign currency derivatives had total notional values of $17,505 million versus $11,370 million in 2022, and hedge-accounting-qualified derivatives produced a net unrealized gain of $22 million.

KO_10K_2025-02-20_full.txt.document_context: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024. The excerpt highlights risks from unfavorable global economic and geopolitical conditions, including intensified inflation in certain markets throughout 2024 that may continue in 2025, which could reduce consumer demand and profitability.

sections.Preamble: The Coca-Cola Company (COCA COLA CO, NYSE: KO) filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, under Commission File Number 001-02217, with principal executive offices at One Coca-Cola Plaza, Atlanta, Georgia 30313. The filing identifies Delaware incorporation, IRS Employer Identification No. 58-0628465, and lists registered securities including common stock and multiple NYSE-listed notes maturing from 2026 through 2053, while indicating the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and has filed all required Exchange Act reports.
sections.Item 1A: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that global economic weakness, inflation, higher interest rates, credit market stress, unemployment, commodity and energy price volatility, trade policy shifts, foreign exchange movements, and recessionary conditions could materially hurt demand for its beverages, profitability, liquidity, and overall financial performance. The filing also highlights geopolitical instability, including international conflicts, civil unrest, terrorism, governmental changes, and changes in international relations, as well as health events and consumer trading down to lower-priced private-label brands, as key threats to Coca-Cola’s operations across its global markets.
sections.Item 8: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes its business on a consolidated basis and defines key product categories including concentrates, syrups, fountain syrups, Company Trademark Beverages, and Trademark Coca-Cola beverages such as Coca-Cola, Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light, and Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, along with trademarked brands like Fanta, Sprite, and Simply. The company states it operates in two lines of business—concentrate operations and finished product operations—and that concentrate operations generate net operating revenues by selling beverage concentrates, beverage bases, syrups, and certain finished beverages to authorized bottling operations and bottling partners.
sections.Item 1B: The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE: KO) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. This section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating the company had no outstanding staff review issues in its 2025 Form 10-K filed on February 20, 2025.
sections.Item 1: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes itself as a total beverage company whose trademarks have been sold in the United States since 1886 and are now distributed in more than 200 countries and territories through independent bottling partners, distributors, wholesalers, retailers, and consolidated bottling and distribution operations. Its portfolio includes major nonalcoholic brands such as Coca-Cola, Sprite, Coca-Cola Zero Sugar, Fanta, and Diet Coke/Coca-Cola Light across categories including sparkling flavors, water, sports, coffee and tea, juice, value-added dairy and plant-based beverages, and emerging beverages, with branded products accounting for 2.2 billion of an estimated 65 billion daily global beverage servings. The company, incorporated in Delaware in 1919 and guided by the purpose to “refresh the world and make a difference,” reports operating segments in Europe, Middle East and Africa, Latin America, North America, Asia Pacific, Global Ventures, and Bottling Investments.
sections.Item 7: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 7 MD&A describes its global beverage portfolio and how consumer demand drives optimization of the Coca-Cola system, including beverage volume measurement across the company and its bottling partners. It highlights major brands across sparkling soft drinks, water/sports/coffee/tea, and juice/value-added dairy/plant-based beverages, including Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Dasani, smartwater, Powerade, fairlife, and Minute Maid, as well as alcohol initiatives such as Jack Daniel’s & Coca-Cola, Lemon-Dou, and Topo Chico Hard Seltzer. The section also notes marketing and distribution relationships with third parties and Monster Energy, where certain Coca-Cola system bottlers distribute Monster brands under coordination and distribution agreements in the U.S., Canada, and other territories.
sections.Item 3: The Coca-Cola Company says its business is exposed to numerous indirect taxes and fees worldwide, including import duties, tariffs, excise taxes, sales and value-added taxes, beverage taxes, packaging taxes, carbon taxes, property taxes, and payroll taxes, and notes that U.S. lawmakers have previously considered a federal excise tax on sugar-, HFCS-, and other nutritive-sweetener beverages. The company also highlights growing regulatory risk around beverage containers and packaging—such as deposits, ecotaxes, recycling-content mandates, tethered bottle caps, extended producer responsibility laws, and restrictions on plastic products and PFAS-containing packaging—which could raise costs, reduce demand, and hurt net operating revenues and profitability.
sections.Item 1C: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) describes a multilayered cybersecurity risk management program integrated into its Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework to identify, assess, prioritize, mitigate, and recover from threats such as unauthorized access, data theft, ransomware, malware, system disruptions, and other intrusions. The company says its controls include physical, administrative, and technical safeguards benchmarked against the National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework, with internal audit oversight, outside advisors, penetration testing, tabletop exercises, and incident response processes for consolidated entities and acquired businesses.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of The Coca-Cola Company’s 2025 Form 10-K is marked “RESERVED” and contains no substantive disclosure. This section provides no additional financial, operational, regulatory, or product information beyond the placeholder designation.
sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company says its worldwide headquarters is a 35-acre complex in Atlanta, Georgia, with office, technical, engineering, and reception facilities used by Corporate and North America employees, and it also owns or leases facilities worldwide for administrative, manufacturing, processing, packaging, storage, warehousing, distribution, and retail operations. As of December 31, 2024, Coca-Cola reported principal facilities across operating segments including 30 owned and 1 leased concentrate/syrup plants, 74 owned and 7 leased beverage manufacturing/bottling plants, 63 owned distribution and storage facilities, and 1,526 principal retail stores, with Costa retail stores in Global Ventures and bottling/distribution facilities in Bottling Investments. Management states its production facilities are suitable and adequate, appropriately utilized, and generally have sufficient capacity to meet present needs, with seasonal demand affecting utilization and additional output possible through added personnel, shifts, capital equipment, or facility expansion.
sections.Item 4: Item 4 states that Mine Safety Disclosures are not applicable for The Coca-Cola Company (KO). The section then identifies KO’s executive officers as of February 20, 2025, including Manuel Arroyo (Executive Vice President, Global Chief Marketing Officer), Henrique Braun (Chief Operating Officer), Lisa Chang (Executive Vice President, Global Chief People Officer), Monica Howard Douglas (Executive Vice President, Global General Counsel), and Nikolaos Koumettis, with brief career histories spanning Coca-Cola operating units and prior roles at companies such as Deoleo, S.C. Johnson, AMB Group LLC, and Equifax.
sections.Item 5: The Coca-Cola Company’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “KO,” and the company notes that future quarterly dividends remain at the discretion of its Board of Directors and depend on earnings, financial condition, business needs, and regulatory considerations. As of February 18, 2025, Coca-Cola reported 176,283 shareowner accounts of record, excluding beneficial “street name” holders, and stated that no unregistered equity securities were sold during 2024. The company also disclosed repurchases of 8,505,629 shares in the quarter ended December 31, 2024 at an average price of $65.77 per share, with 8,494,118 shares bought under its publicly announced repurchase plan and 76,349,979 shares remaining authorized for purchase.
sections.Item 9A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) states that, under the supervision and participation of management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, it evaluated the effectiveness of its disclosure controls and procedures under Rule 13a-15(e) of the Exchange Act as of December 31, 2024. Based on that evaluation, management concluded the disclosure controls and procedures were effective, and the filing notes that the report of management on internal control over financial reporting and the attestation report of the independent registered public accounting firm are included in Part II.
sections.Item 9: The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE: KO) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on February 20, 2025. The section indicates there were no reported changes in accountants and no disagreements regarding accounting or financial disclosure matters.
sections.Item 7A: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) says it uses derivative financial instruments only to hedge, not trade, to reduce exposure to foreign currency exchange rates, interest rates, commodity prices, and other market risks, and it monitors these risks with sensitivity analysis and other measurement systems. In 2024, Coca-Cola generated $28.7 billion of net operating revenues outside the United States and used forward exchange contracts, foreign currency options, and collars—principally in euro, British pound, and Japanese yen—to hedge forecasted cash flows, monetary assets and liabilities, and net investments in foreign operations. As of December 31, 2024, foreign currency derivatives had total notional value of $18,442 million ($17,505 million in 2023), and hedge-accounting derivatives produced a net unrealized gain of $366 million.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C of The Coca-Cola Company’s 2025 Form 10-K states that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is “Not applicable.” This section appears immediately before Part III and contains no additional substantive information, metrics, or named regulators beyond the filing context for Coca-Cola (KO).
sections.Item 10: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 10, “Directors, Executive Officers and Corporate Governance,” is incorporated by reference to its 2025 Proxy Statement, which the company expects to file with the SEC within 120 days after the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024. sections.Item 9B: During the fiscal quarter ended December 31, 2024, The Coca-Cola Company (KO) reported that none of its directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement under Exchange Act Rule 16a-1(f) and Item 408 of Regulation S-K, except for Jennifer K. Mann, Executive Vice President and President of the North America operating unit, adopted a Rule 10b5-1 plan on November 22, 2024 during an open trading window for the potential exercise of vested stock options and sale of up to 80,820 shares of Coca-Cola common stock, with an expiration date of November 1, 2025 or earlier if completed.
sections.Item 11: Item 11, Executive Compensation, is not presented in the 2025 Form 10-K itself; The Coca-Cola Company (KO) incorporates the required disclosure by reference to its 2025 Proxy Statement, which is to be filed with the SEC within 120 days after the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024. KO_10Q_2022Q2_2022-04-28_full.txt.document_context: The Coca-Cola Company (KO) filed a 10-Q quarterly report for the fiscal quarter ended April 1, 2022. Net operating revenues rose to $10.491 billion from $9.020 billion a year earlier, while net income attributable to shareowners increased to $2.781 billion, or $0.64 per diluted share, versus $2.245 billion, or $0.52 per share.

sections.Preamble: The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE: KO), a Delaware corporation headquartered at One Coca-Cola Plaza in Atlanta, Georgia, filed this Form 10-Q quarterly report for the period ended April 1, 2022 under Section 13 or 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, with Commission File Number 001-02217 and IRS Employer Identification No. 58-0628465. The preamble lists Coca-Cola’s common stock and multiple NYSE-listed debt securities, including notes due from 2024 through 2041, and states that the company had filed all required reports and submitted all required Interactive Data Files under SEC Regulation S-T.
sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company said it suspended business in Russia on March 8, 2022 and that the Russia-Ukraine conflict disrupted operations in Ukraine, noting that these markets represented about 2% of 2021 unit case volume and roughly 1% of consolidated net operating revenues and 2% of operating income. As of April 1, 2022, assets tied to Russia and Ukraine were less than 0.5% of total assets, and the company held an approximate 21% stake in Coca-Cola HBC AG (CCH), its bottling and distribution partner in the region, which could affect equity income if CCH records an impairment. The section also says COVID-19, supply chain issues, labor shortages, and inflation continued to pressure results during the three months ended April 1, 2022.
sections.Item 3: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 3 Legal Proceedings focuses on its long-running U.S. federal income tax dispute with the IRS over transfer pricing for tax years 2007 through 2009, in which the IRS issued a September 17, 2015 Statutory Notice of Deficiency seeking approximately $3.3 billion in additional federal income tax and proposing to reallocate over $9 billion of income from foreign affiliates to the U.S. parent. The company states the dispute concerns a methodology previously memorialized in a 1996 Closing Agreement and repeatedly accepted in IRS audits for 1996 through 2006, while the IRS later retroactively rejected that approach and designated the matter for litigation on October 15, 2015.
sections.Item 1A: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 1A risk-factor section is accompanied here by first-quarter 2022 financial highlights showing net operating revenues of $10.491 billion, operating income of $3.405 billion, and net income attributable to shareowners of $2.781 billion, up from $2.245 billion in the prior-year quarter. The excerpt also reports diluted EPS of $0.64, comprehensive income of $3.916 billion, and notable other comprehensive income items including $1.009 billion of net foreign currency translation adjustments, with references to the Securities and Exchange Commission, the 2021 Form 10-K, and future SEC filings.

sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 2 discussion highlights operational disruptions from the Russia-Ukraine conflict, including the March 8, 2022 suspension of business in Russia and continued disruption in Ukraine, with the two markets representing about 2% of 2021 unit case volume and roughly 1% of consolidated net operating revenues and 2% of operating income. sections.Item 1A: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 1A risk-factor section in its 2022 Form 10-Q points readers to risks described in this report, the company’s 2021 Form 10-K, its prior 2022 Form 10-Q, and future Securities and Exchange Commission filings. sections.Preamble: The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE: KO), a Delaware corporation headquartered at One Coca-Cola Plaza in Atlanta, Georgia, filed this Form 10-Q quarterly report for the period ended September 30, 2022, under Commission File Number 001-02217. sections.Item 2: The Coca-Cola Company’s Item 2 discussion highlights operational disruptions from the Russia-Ukraine conflict, including the March 8, 2022 suspension of business in Russia and continued disruption in Ukraine, with the two markets contributing about 2% of 2021 unit case volume and roughly 1% of consolidated net operating revenues and 2% of operating income. sections.Item 1: Lockheed Martin Corporation describes itself as a global aerospace and defense technology company serving U.S. Government agencies and allied customers across defense, space, intelligence, homeland security, IT, and cybersecurity, organized into four business segments: Aeronautics, Missiles and Fire Control (MFC), Rotary and Mission Systems (RMS), and Space. The company says recent regional conflicts have increased demand for its products, prompting expansion of production capacity and investment in artificial intelligence, autonomy, open-architecture systems, and advanced networking to deliver integrated solutions at scale and strengthen deterrence. It also emphasizes a multi-year business transformation, strategic partnerships with commercial companies, workforce development, and potential acquisitions or divestitures to support evolving mission requirements.
sections.Item 1A: Lockheed Martin Corporation says its business is heavily dependent on U.S. Government contracts, with 72% of 2025 consolidated sales from the U.S. Government and 63% from the Department of War (DoW), also known as the Department of Defense, making it vulnerable to shifts in defense priorities, budget cuts, continuing resolutions, shutdowns, and debt-ceiling-related funding disruptions. The company warns that delayed appropriations, partial funding, contract starts restrictions, and multi-year funding uncertainty could lead to reduced orders, program cancellations, nonpayment or payment delays, and unreimbursed costs if spending is incurred before funds are obligated or contracts are definitized.
sections.Item 1C: Lockheed Martin Corporation says cybersecurity is central to its 21st Century Security vision and digital transformation, and that it faces threats ranging from ransomware and denial-of-service attacks to advanced nation-state adversaries targeting the defense industrial base and critical infrastructure. The company describes a corporate information security governance structure, a counterintelligence and insider-threat detection program, and extensive compliance obligations under DFARS and Department of War (DoW) requirements for safeguarding controlled unclassified information (CUI) and reporting incidents. It also highlights collaboration with the DoW and other defense industrial base members on the Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) program, noting that it submitted its initial CMMC certification in November 2025 and uses its proprietary Intelligence Driven Defense® model to improve risk management.
sections.Item 3: Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) says it is involved in ordinary-course litigation, environmental matters, and contingencies tied to previously owned businesses, with potential outcomes including fines, penalties, cost reimbursements, damages, and non-monetary relief. sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Lockheed Martin Corporation’s 2026 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive discussion, financial data, or named business developments. sections.Item 2: Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT) reported that as of December 31, 2025 it owned or leased building space at 333 locations, primarily in the U.S., and also managed or occupied 9 government-owned facilities under lease and other arrangements. Laurel, Owego, Syracuse, Manassas, Mielec, Huntsville, Sunnyvale, Denver, Cape Canaveral, Valley Forge, and Bethesda. The company reported $75.0 billion in 2025 sales, with 72% from the U.S. Government, including 63% from the Department of War (DoW)/Department of Defense and 28% from international customers including foreign military sales (FMS), and highlighted expanding production capacity and adoption of technologies such as artificial intelligence, autonomy, open-architecture systems, and advanced networking to meet rising demand.

sections.Item 7A: Lockheed Martin Corporation says its market risk exposure is concentrated in interest rates, foreign currency exchange rates, and market prices on certain equity securities, and that it actively manages counterparty exposure through relationships with U.S. and international financial institutions to preserve access to credit. As of December 31, 2025, the company reported $22.0 billion of estimated fair value for outstanding debt and $22.9 billion of principal debt outstanding, excluding $1.2 billion of unamortized discounts and issuance costs, and stated that a 10% interest rate change would not materially affect debt fair value. Lockheed Martin uses derivative instruments only for hedging, not speculation, including forward and option contracts and interest rate swaps designated as cash flow or fair value hedges, with major foreign currency exposures in the British pound sterling, euro, Canadian dollar, Australian dollar, Norwegian kroner, and Polish zloty.

MA_10K_2026-02-11_full.txt.document_context: Mastercard Inc. filed a 10-K annual report covering fiscal 2025. The company reported net revenue of $32.8 billion and net income of $15.0 billion, both up 16% year over year, with diluted EPS of $16.52; on a non-GAAP basis, adjusted net revenue was $32.8 billion and adjusted diluted EPS was $17.01. It also highlighted $10.6 trillion in gross dollar volume, 175.5 billion switched transactions, and $14.5 billion returned to stockholders, including $11.7 billion in share repurchases and $2.8 billion in dividends.

sections.Preamble: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) filed this Form 10-K annual report with the SEC, and this preamble identifies the document as the company’s annual filing. The excerpt is a brief table-of-contents header for Part I, indicating the start of the filing’s main sections without substantive business or financial disclosure.
sections.Item 1A: Mastercard Inc. says global payments-industry regulation and central bank oversight could materially hurt its business by restricting the products and services it can offer, the countries and customers it can serve, and how it structures and operates its network. The company highlights growing scrutiny of network fees, possible extension of debit rules to credit products, and designations in some jurisdictions as a “systemically important payment system,” “specified service provider,” or “critical infrastructure,” which bring requirements around risk management, collateral, participant default, timely switching, and capital and financial resources.
sections.Item 1B: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states in Item 1B, “Unresolved Staff Comments,” that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. The section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no outstanding issues or requests for clarification from the Securities and Exchange Commission in this annual report.
sections.Item 1: Mastercard Inc. describes itself as a global payments technology company that connects consumers, financial institutions, merchants, governments, digital partners, and businesses through its proprietary network, enabling secure electronic payments, authorization, clearing and settlement, ACH transactions, and services under the Mastercard®, Maestro®, and Cirrus® brands. The section highlights a franchise-based model and multi-layered security approach, along with 2025 performance metrics including $32.8 billion in net revenue, $15.0 billion in net income, diluted EPS of $16.52, $10.6 trillion in gross dollar volume, $17.6 billion in cash flows from operations, and 175.5 billion switched transactions.
sections.Item 2: Mastercard Inc. states that it owns its corporate headquarters in Purchase, New York, and its principal technology and operations center in O’Fallon, Missouri. As of December 31, 2025, Mastercard and its subsidiaries owned or leased commercial properties across the U.S. and other countries, including corporate and regional offices and operations centers, and the company says these facilities are suitable and adequate while it periodically reviews space needs to acquire, lease, consolidate, or dispose of space, including to address climate-related impacts.
sections.Item 1C: Mastercard Inc. says cybersecurity risk management is an integral part of its enterprise risk management program, reflecting its role as a global payments technology company entrusted with sensitive personal information. The company describes a multi-layered cybersecurity, privacy, data protection, and information security program that includes regular testing, third-party assessments, certifications and attestations, insurance coverage, and processes to evaluate third-party providers and respond to cyber and physical threats. Mastercard also works with customers, governments, policymakers, public-private partnerships, consultants, and peer companies to develop secure transaction standards and privacy-centric data practices.
sections.Item 4: Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, states that mine safety disclosures are not applicable to Mastercard Inc. The section also includes the company’s executive officers as of February 11, 2026, naming Ling Hai, Jon M. Huntsman, Jr., Linda Kirkpatrick, Jill Kramer, Jorn Lambert, and Edward McLaughlin, along with their current roles and prior experience at Mastercard, Accenture PLC, Bank of America, Booz Allen Hamilton, Ford Motor Company, and various U.S. government posts.
sections.Item 3: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states that its Item 3 Legal Proceedings discussion is incorporated by reference to Note 19, “Legal and Regulatory Proceedings,” in the consolidated financial statements included in Part II of the 2026 Form 10-K filed on February 11, 2026. This section indicates that the company’s litigation and regulatory matters are addressed in the financial statement note rather than detailed in the Item 3 text itself.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Mastercard Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, financial data, or discussion of operations, risks, or metrics. The section is marked “[Reserved]” and appears as a placeholder within Part II of the annual report.
sections.Item 7A: Mastercard Inc. states that market risk arises from adverse changes in foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates, but its exposure to these risks is limited. Management continuously monitors these exposures and oversees policies for funding, investments, and the use of derivative financial instruments to manage them, with further discussion referenced in Note 21, “Derivative and Hedging Instruments,” to the consolidated financial statements.
sections.Item 5: Mastercard Inc. states that its Class A common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “MA,” with 71 record holders as of February 6, 2026, while its non-voting Class B common stock has no established public trading market and had approximately 207 record holders representing about 0.7% of total outstanding equity. The section also compares five-year total shareholder return for Mastercard versus the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Financials, showing Mastercard’s indexed return rising to 164.50 by December 31, 2025, and discloses quarterly cash dividends of $0.87 per share declared on December 9, 2025 and February 10, 2026, payable in 2026 subject to legally available funds.
sections.Item 9: Mastercard Inc. (MA) reports that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable in its 2026 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The section indicates there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements regarding accounting or financial disclosure to disclose.
sections.Item 7: Mastercard Incorporated and its subsidiary Mastercard International Incorporated describe Mastercard as a global payments technology company that connects consumers, financial institutions, merchants, governments, digital partners, and businesses through secure electronic payments. The company operates a proprietary network that authorizes, clears, and settles transactions, and also offers ACH capabilities, consumer and commercial payment products, and services such as security solutions, digital and authentication, processing and gateway, consumer acquisition and engagement, and business and market insights. Key brands and products highlighted include Mastercard®, Maestro®, and Cirrus®, with the discussion emphasizing the company’s franchise model, secure data use, and interdependent payments capabilities.
sections.Item 8: Mastercard Inc. said 2025 was shaped by new regulatory developments affecting payment networks, including the EU’s revised systemic importance regulation for systemically important firms, the Central Bank of Brazil’s new rules for Payment Scheme Operators such as Mastercard and Visa, and New Zealand Commerce Commission-approved caps on cross-border interchange effective May 2026. The company also highlighted U.S. litigation over the Federal Reserve’s Regulation II interchange cap, with conflicting federal district court rulings leaving the cap in effect while appeals continue, and noted that its revenue recognition for customer rebates and incentives depends on volume thresholds, card issuance and conversion, expected payments, and historical experience.
sections.Item 9A: Mastercard Incorporated (NYSE: MA) states that its disclosure controls and procedures, as defined in Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), are designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and reporting of information required in SEC filings and to communicate such information to management, including the President and Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer. Based on management’s review as of December 31, 2025, Mastercard concluded that its disclosure controls and procedures were effective, and it also assessed the effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting, with the related management report included elsewhere in Part II of the Form 10-K.
sections.Item 9C: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states in Item 9C of its 2025 Form 10-K that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is “Not applicable.” This section contains no additional substantive information, metrics, or regulator-specific findings beyond that compliance status.
sections.Item 9B: Mastercard Inc. (MA) disclosed Item 9B other information on Rule 10b5-1 and non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangements adopted or terminated during the three months ended December 31, 2025, covering sales of Class A common stock by officers and directors. Named executives included President and CEO Michael Miebach, President & Chief Technology Officer Ed McLaughlin, President for Asia Pacific, Europe, Middle East & Africa Ling Hai, and Chief Services Officer Craig Vosburg, with plans tied to up to 26,400 shares from options, 16,000 vested but unsettled performance stock units, 6,228 shares, 34,060 shares, 12,952 shares, and 7,443 shares, respectively, and expirations ranging from November 4, 2026 to February 26, 2027.
sections.Item 10: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states that information about its executive officers is provided in Part I under “Information about our executive officers,” while the remaining Item 10 disclosures on directors, executive officers, and corporate governance will be included in the company’s definitive proxy statement for its 2026 annual meeting of stockholders. sections.Item 12: Mastercard Inc. states that the information required by Item 12, “Security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management and related stockholder matters,” is not presented in the Form 10-K itself but will appear in the company’s Proxy Statement and is incorporated by reference into the report. This section therefore serves as a cross-reference rather than providing ownership data, named holders, or stockholder metrics directly in the filing.
sections.Item 13: Mastercard Inc. states that the information required for Item 13, covering certain relationships and related transactions and director independence, is not presented in the Form 10-K itself but will appear in the company’s Proxy Statement and is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report. This section therefore serves as a cross-reference to the Proxy Statement for details on related-party transactions and board independence matters.
sections.Item 11: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states that the information required by Item 11, Executive Compensation, is not included in the Form 10-K itself and will instead appear in the company’s Proxy Statement, which is incorporated by reference into this Annual Report. This section therefore provides no compensation figures, named executives, or pay-related metrics in the filing excerpt, only a cross-reference to the Proxy Statement for the SEC-required executive compensation disclosure.
sections.Item 14: Mastercard Inc. This section is largely a cross-reference within Part IV of the filing and does not provide fee amounts, auditor names, or other substantive disclosures in the excerpt.
sections.Item 15: Mastercard Inc. (NYSE: MA) states in Item 15 that its 10-K includes the Consolidated Financial Statements referenced in Part II, Item 8, but no Consolidated Financial Statement Schedules are filed. The section directs readers to the Exhibit Index for the exhibits filed with the report or incorporated by reference from prior filings.
sections.Item 16: Mastercard Incorporated’s 2025 Form 10-K summary states that “Mastercard,” “we,” and “our” refer to Mastercard Incorporated, its consolidated subsidiaries, and operating subsidiary Mastercard International Incorporated, and it emphasizes that the report contains forward-looking statements under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995. The section highlights key risk factors affecting the global payments business, including regulation of interchange rates and surcharging, privacy/data/AI/information security, anti-money laundering and sanctions compliance, tax law changes, litigation, competition, technological change, real-time account-based payments, and information security incidents or service disruptions.

sections.Preamble: McDonald’s Corporation (NYSE: MCD) filed a Form 10-Q quarterly report with the SEC for the period ended March 31, 2023, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 110 North Carpenter Street, Chicago, Illinois 60607, with Commission File Number 1-5231 and IRS Employer Identification No. 36-2361282. sections.Item 3: McDonald’s Corporation (NYSE: MCD) states that there were no material changes to the quantitative and qualitative disclosures about market risk from those included in its Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2022. sections.Item 2: McDonald’s Corporation operates and franchises restaurants in more than 100 countries, with 40,535 restaurants worldwide at March 31, 2023, of which 95% were franchised. The company’s key segments are the U.S., International Operated Markets, and International Developmental Licensed Markets & Corporate, and it emphasizes franchising under conventional franchise, developmental license, and affiliate structures as central to brand protection, profitability, and locally relevant customer experiences. McDonald’s says company-operated restaurants remain important for training, testing innovations, and refining operating standards, while the franchise relationship is governed by McDonald’s Global Brand Standards and shaped by local legal and regulatory conditions.
sections.Item 1: McDonald’s Corporation’s unaudited condensed consolidated balance sheet for March 31, 2023 shows total assets of $52.0 billion, up from $50.4 billion at December 31, 2022, driven by higher cash and equivalents ($3.7 billion), prepaid expenses, and modest growth in property and equipment and other assets. Key liabilities include $36.6 billion of long-term debt, $12.1 billion of long-term lease liabilities, and $4.6 billion of current liabilities, while shareholders’ equity remained negative at $(5.8) billion due largely to $72.2 billion of treasury stock and accumulated deficit-like equity structure.
sections.Item 1A: McDonald’s Corporation (MCD) states that the risk factors affecting its business are discussed in Part I, Item 2 of the quarterly report rather than repeated in Item 1A. sections.Item 4: McDonald’s Corporation (MCD) states that, as of March 31, 2023, management including the CEO and CFO evaluated disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) and concluded they were effective, providing reasonable assurance that required SEC reporting information is recorded, processed, summarized, and communicated on time. The company also says it is implementing a multi-year finance and technology transformation to migrate its general ledger, financial close, and consolidation processes to new financial systems, not because of any identified internal control weakness, and that it has modified certain controls while continuing to monitor their effectiveness. Management further reports no material change in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended March 31, 2023.

MSFT_10K_2022Q2_2022-07-28_full.txt.document_context: Microsoft Corporation filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2022. sections.Item 1B: Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K Item 1B, “Unresolved Staff Comments,” contains no substantive disclosure beyond the section heading and page reference, indicating there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff to report. This section is effectively a placeholder in the annual report and does not identify any specific issues, regulators, products, or financial metrics.
sections.Item 1A: Microsoft says its operations, financial results, cash flows, and common stock price are exposed to broad risk and uncertainty, with intense competition across all markets potentially reducing revenue and operating margins. The filing highlights rapid technology change, low barriers to entry, and the need to sustain innovation and scale in platform-based ecosystems, while noting competition from vertically integrated rivals in personal computers, tablets, phones, gaming consoles, wearables, and other endpoint devices that bundle hardware, software, apps, and content.
sections.Item 1: Microsoft Corporation, founded in 1975, describes itself as a technology company whose mission is to empower every person and organization on the planet to achieve more by delivering software, services, devices, and solutions. In Item 1, Microsoft emphasizes its cloud-based solutions, distributed computing fabric across cloud and edge, AI capabilities, metaverse experiences, solution support and consulting services, and online advertising, while highlighting customer needs around hybrid work, security, management, and mission-critical workloads.
sections.Preamble: Microsoft Corporation’s Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2022 was filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) under Commission File Number 001-37845. The filing identifies Microsoft, headquartered at One Microsoft Way, Redmond, Washington, as a large accelerated filer and well-known seasoned issuer, with common stock (MSFT) and 3.125% Notes due 2028 and 2.625% Notes due 2033 registered on Nasdaq. It also confirms compliance with Exchange Act reporting and electronic Interactive Data File submission requirements under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Regulation S-T.
sections.Item 3: Item 3, Legal Proceedings in Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K provides a brief reference to the company’s legal matters and litigation disclosures, with the section appearing on page 37 of the annual report. sections.Item 4: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures section in this 2022 Form 10-K contains no substantive mine safety information and appears only as a placeholder in Part II of the annual report. sections.Item 5: Item 5 of Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K covers the market for MSFT common equity, related stockholder matters, and issuer purchases of equity securities. This section identifies the annual report location for information on Microsoft’s stock trading, shareholder matters, and any repurchases of equity securities.
sections.Item 2: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 2 describes a global real estate footprint centered at its Redmond, Washington headquarters, with about 15 million square feet in King County used for engineering, sales, marketing, operations, and other administrative functions, including roughly 10 million square feet owned on about 520 acres and 5 million square feet leased. As of June 30, 2022, Microsoft reported 73 million square feet of office, datacenter, and other facilities worldwide, split between 33 million square feet owned and 40 million square feet leased, with major owned properties in China, India, Ireland, the Netherlands, and Singapore and major leased properties in Australia, Canada, China, France, Germany, India, Ireland, Israel, Japan, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure, with the section text showing only “[Reserved]” and a page reference to 39. This section does not provide additional financial, operational, regulatory, or product information.
sections.Item 7: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis section in its 2022 Form 10-K provides management’s narrative on financial condition, results of operations, and key business drivers for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2022. sections.Item 9: Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) states in Item 9 of its 2022 Form 10-K that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure. sections.Item 9B: Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) states in Item 9B, Other Information, of its 2022 Form 10-K that there is no additional information to report. The section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no other disclosures, events, or regulatory matters were included under this item.
sections.Item 7A: Item 7A of Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K is the “Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk” section, which addresses the company’s exposure to market risks such as changes in interest rates, foreign currency exchange rates, and other financial market variables. This section is part of Microsoft’s annual risk disclosures filed with the SEC on July 28, 2022, and is intended to provide quantitative and qualitative context on how market volatility could affect the company’s financial position and results of operations.
sections.Item 8: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 8 financial statements show fiscal 2022 revenue of $198.3 billion, up from $168.1 billion in 2021 and $143.0 billion in 2020, driven by $125.5 billion of service and other revenue and $72.7 billion of product revenue. Net income rose to $72.7 billion in 2022, with diluted EPS of $9.65, operating income of $83.4 billion, and research and development expense of $24.5 billion; the comprehensive income statement also shows a $6.5 billion other comprehensive loss, mainly from investment and translation adjustments.
sections.Item 9C: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 9C disclosure states that the section is “Not applicable,” indicating no reportable issues under the SEC’s disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections. The filing simply transitions from the “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections” heading into Part III without additional substantive information.
sections.Item 9A: Microsoft’s Item 9A states that, under the supervision of the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, the company evaluated its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(b) and concluded they were effective as of the end of the reporting period. Management also reports that internal control over financial reporting, evaluated using the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013), was effective as of June 30, 2022, with no material changes during the quarter ended June 30, 2022. The section emphasizes the purpose and inherent limitations of internal controls for reliable U.S. GAAP financial reporting and notes that Deloitte & Touche LLP audited Microsoft’s internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 11: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 11 incorporates by reference the executive compensation disclosures from its Proxy Statement, including “Director Compensation,” “Named Executive Officer Compensation,” and the “Compensation Committee Report.” The section also references “Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation” if required, indicating that detailed pay and governance information is contained in the proxy materials rather than repeated in the 10-K.
sections.Item 10: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 10 incorporates by reference the executive officer biographies in Part I, Item 1 and the director and Audit Committee information in the company’s Proxy Statement for the Annual Meeting of Shareholders to be held December 13, 2022. sections.Item 12: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 12 cross-references the Proxy Statement for details on “Stock Ownership Information,” “Principal Shareholders,” and “Equity Compensation Plan Information.” This section does not provide standalone ownership data in the 10-K excerpt, but directs readers to the company’s proxy materials for beneficial ownership and equity compensation disclosures.
sections.Item 13: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 13 states that the disclosure on certain relationships and related transactions, as well as director independence, is incorporated by reference from the company’s Proxy Statement. sections.Item 14: Microsoft Corporation states that information on principal accountant fees and services is incorporated by reference from the Proxy Statement, specifically the sections “Fees Billed by Deloitte & Touche” and “Policy on Audit Committee Pre-Approval of Audit and Permissible Non-Audit Services of Independent Auditor.” The company identifies its principal accountant as Deloitte & Touche LLP, PCAOB ID No. 34, and notes that the relevant disclosure concerns audit fees, non-audit services, and audit committee pre-approval policies.
sections.Item 15: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 15 states that the financial statements are included in Part II, Item 8 of the 2022 Form 10-K, with financial statement schedules omitted because they are not required, not applicable, or already included elsewhere. The exhibit listing identifies key corporate governance and debt instruments, including the amended and restated Articles of Incorporation, Bylaws, and multiple indentures with The Bank of New York Mellon Trust Company, N.A. as trustee for Microsoft’s notes, such as the Base Indenture and supplemental indentures for various maturities and coupon rates.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 is largely administrative, noting that Microsoft Corporation’s 2022 Form 10-K contains no Form 10-K Summary and then presenting the required signatures under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Jolla, Corporate Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer, and by Satya Nadella, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, along with directors Reid Hoffman, Hugh F. Johnston, Teri L. List, Sandra E. Peterson, Penny S. Pritzker, Carlos A. Rodriguez, Charles W. Scharf, and John W. Stanton.

sections.Item 1A: Microsoft says its operations, financial condition, results of operations, liquidity, and common stock price could be harmed by intense competition across its technology markets, where low barriers to entry, rapid product cycles, and disruptive technologies require continual innovation in products, devices, and services. sections.Item 3: Microsoft Corporation says it is exposed to foreign exchange, interest rate, credit, and equity price risk and uses derivative instruments to manage these exposures, though they can still affect consolidated financial statements. The company monitors foreign currency positions daily, with principal exposures in the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Canadian dollar, and Australian dollar, and manages its fixed-income portfolio and investment-grade credit profile relative to global indices. Its December 31, 2024 sensitivity analysis estimates a 10% foreign exchange decline would reduce earnings by $9.903 billion, while a 100 basis point rise in U.S. Treasury rates, a 100 basis point widening in credit spreads, and a 10% equity market decline would reduce fair value or earnings by $1.097 billion, $315 million, and $1.098 billion, respectively.
sections.Item 1A: Microsoft Corporation says its operations, financial condition, results of operations, liquidity, and common stock price are exposed to broad risk factors, led by intense competition across technology markets and the need to keep innovating amid low barriers to entry, rapid product cycles, and shifting user needs. The company highlights competition from diversified global firms, specialized rivals, and competing platform-based ecosystems, including vertically integrated hardware-software models in PCs, tablets, smartphones, gaming consoles, wearables, and other endpoint devices that may claim security and performance advantages. Microsoft warns that failure to maintain scale, attract users and developers, and deliver appealing products and services could reduce growth, increase cost of revenue, compress operating margins, and hurt its business and results of operations.
sections.Item 5: Microsoft Corporation reported that none of its officers or directors, as defined in Rule 16a-1(f) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, adopted, modified, or terminated any “Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” or “non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement” under Item 408 of Regulation S-K during the three months ended December 31, 2024. This Item 5 market-related disclosure in the company’s Form 10-Q, filed January 29, 2025, highlights the absence of insider trading arrangement changes for the quarter.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 lists Microsoft Corporation’s 10-Q exhibits, including the unaudited interim financial information letter (Exhibit 15.1), Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 302 certifications from the CEO and CFO (31.1, 31.2), and Section 906 certifications furnished, not filed (32.1, 32.2). It also includes the Inline XBRL instance document and taxonomy extension schema (101.INS, 101.SCH) and the Inline XBRL cover page (104), followed by the signature of Alice L. Jolla, Corporate Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer, dated January 29, 2025.

MSFT_10Q_2025Q1_2025-04-30_full.txt.document_context: Microsoft Corporation filed a 10-Q quarterly report for the fiscal period ended March 31, 2025. For the three months ended March 31, 2025, revenue was $70.066 billion and net income was $25.824 billion, up from $61.858 billion and $21.939 billion a year earlier; for the nine months, revenue reached $205.283 billion and net income $74.599 billion.

sections.Item 1: Microsoft Corporation’s Item 1 financial statements for the quarter and nine months ended March 31, 2025 show total revenue of $70.1 billion for the quarter and $205.3 billion year-to-date, driven by $54.7 billion of service and other revenue versus $15.3 billion of product revenue. Operating income rose to $32.0 billion in the quarter and $94.2 billion year-to-date, with net income of $25.8 billion and diluted EPS of $3.46 for the quarter, alongside higher research and development spending and lower product revenue compared with the prior-year periods.
sections.Item 2: Microsoft Corporation’s MD&A in this Form 10-Q emphasizes forward-looking statements, risk disclosures, and the need to read the quarter’s results alongside the June 30, 2024 Form 10-K, the December 3, 2024 Form 8-K, and the consolidated financial statements. The company describes itself as a technology leader focused on making digital technology and artificial intelligence broadly and responsibly available, using AI-powered platforms and tools to help every person and organization achieve more.

The filing lists MSFT common stock on Nasdaq along with 3.125% Notes due 2028 and 2.625% Notes due 2033, confirms timely SEC Exchange Act reporting and Interactive Data File submissions, and reports 7,432,543,865 shares of common stock outstanding as of April 24, 2025.
sections.Item 4: Microsoft Corporation reported that, under the supervision of management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, it evaluated its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rule 13a-15(b) as of the end of the period covered by the 10-Q and concluded they were effective. The company also stated that there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended March 31, 2025 that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 3: Microsoft Corporation says it is exposed to market risk from foreign exchange rates, interest rates, credit risk, and equity prices, and uses derivative instruments to manage these exposures. The company identifies principal currency exposures as the euro, Japanese yen, British pound, Canadian dollar, and Australian dollar, and manages its fixed-income portfolio and credit exposure relative to global and broad-based indices. As of March 31, 2025, its sensitivity analysis estimated a 10% foreign exchange decline could reduce revenue by $10.372 billion and investments by $21 million, a 100 basis point U.S. Treasury rate increase could cut fair value by $1.045 billion, a 100 basis point credit-spread widening could cut fair value by $333 million, and a 10% equity market decline could reduce earnings by $1.247 billion.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 lists Microsoft Corporation’s 10-Q exhibits, including the unaudited interim financial information letter (Exhibit 15.1), Sarbanes-Oxley Act Section 302 certifications from the CEO and CFO (31.1, 31.2), and Section 906 certifications (32.1, 32.2), along with Inline XBRL exhibits 101.INS, 101.SCH, and 104. Jolla, Corporate Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer, on April 30, 2025.
sections.Item 1A: Microsoft says its operations, financial condition, liquidity, results of operations, and common stock price could be adversely affected by intense competition across its technology markets, where low barriers to entry, rapid product cycles, and disruptive technologies require continual innovation. The company highlights competition in platform-based ecosystems and vertically integrated hardware-software models, noting rivals in PCs, tablets, smartphones, gaming consoles, wearables, and other endpoint devices, and warning that shifting toward a vertically integrated model could raise cost of revenue and reduce operating margins. Microsoft also emphasizes its dependence on Windows operating system licensing on PCs and the need to maintain scale to preserve network effects and attractive margins.
sections.Item 5: Microsoft Corporation disclosed insider trading arrangements under Exchange Act Rule 10b5-1 in Item 5 of its 2025 Form 10-Q, noting that Section 16 officers and directors may use such plans to satisfy the affirmative defense conditions of Rule 10b5-1(c). During the quarter ended March 31, 2025, CEO and Chairman Satya Nadella adopted a new written trading plan on March 7, 2025, with a maximum duration through September 5, 2025 and no first trade before September 2, 2025, under which he will sell 80% of the net vested shares from an August 31, 2025 Performance Stock Award vesting, subject to performance metrics and a relative total shareholder return modifier. Microsoft stated that no other officers or directors adopted, modified, or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement during the period.
