sections.Item 2: PepsiCo Inc. discusses critical accounting policies in its 2023 Form 10-Q, focusing on “total marketplace spending,” which includes sales incentives, customer discounts, advertising, and other marketing activities that are generally recorded as reductions of revenue or as incurred. The company explains that accruals for programs such as bottler funding and customer volume rebates are based on annual targets, contract terms, historical experience, and management judgment, with interim-period allocations tied to forecasted full-year incentives and each period’s gross revenue or volume.
sections.Item 3: PepsiCo Inc. and its subsidiaries report that they are party to a variety of litigation, claims, legal or regulatory proceedings, inquiries, and investigations, with further detail referenced in the company’s 2022 Form 10-K. Management states it cannot predict the outcomes with certainty but believes the final resolution of these matters will not have a material adverse effect on PepsiCo’s financial condition, results of operations, or cash flows.
sections.Item 1: PepsiCo, Inc. reported unaudited condensed consolidated results for the 12 weeks ended March 25, 2023, with net revenue of $17.846 billion, gross profit of $9.858 billion, operating profit of $2.629 billion, and net income attributable to PepsiCo of $1.932 billion, or $1.40 per diluted share, versus $4.261 billion and $3.06 per diluted share in the prior-year period. The year-over-year decline reflects the absence of the 2022 Juice Transaction gain associated with the sale of Tropicana, Naked and other juice brands to PAI Partners, while comprehensive income was $1.645 billion and included currency translation and cash flow hedge losses.
sections.Item 4: PepsiCo Inc. reported that, as of the end of the period covered by its 2023 Q1 10-Q, management including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer concluded its disclosure controls and procedures were effective under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and reporting of required SEC information. The company also disclosed ongoing migration of financial processing systems to an Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) platform as part of its global business transformation initiative and 2019 multi-year productivity plan, noting these implementations materially affected internal control over financial reporting during the 12 weeks ended March 25, 2023 but were not expected to have an adverse or material effect on those controls.
sections.Item 7: PepsiCo’s Item 7 MD&A emphasizes that physical/unit volume is a key internal metric for evaluating demand, operating plans, and business performance, with adjustments for acquisitions, divestitures, and 53rd-week effects, and notes that international operations are reported on a monthly calendar basis. For the 12 weeks ended March 25, 2023, consolidated net revenue rose 10% to $17.846 billion versus $16.200 billion, while operating profit fell 50% to $2.629 billion and operating margin declined to 14.7% from 32.5%, driven mainly by the prior-year Juice Transaction gain, higher operating and commodity costs, and other cost pressures.
sections.Item 1A:
PepsiCo Inc. says it continued to face significantly higher operating costs in the 12 weeks ended March 25, 2023, driven by transportation, labor, energy and other commodity costs, and expects these pressures to persist through the remainder of 2023. The company highlights exposure to open-market commodity prices and supply chain disruptions from the war in Ukraine, inflation, adverse weather, raw material shortages and labor shortages, while using fixed-price contracts, pricing agreements and derivative instruments such as swaps and futures to manage risk.  

The risk factors also emphasize climate change-related regulation, including greenhouse gas emissions rules and potential carbon pricing programs in jurisdictions where PepsiCo manufactures, distributes or sells products, which could require significant additional investments in facilities and equipment and increase costs. PepsiCo notes that it cannot predict whether it can fully pass higher costs on to customers without hurting volume, revenue, margins or operating results, and refers investors to Note 9 of its condensed consolidated financial statements and 2022

sections.Item 1A: Pfizer Inc. says its revenues and outlook are exposed to loss, expiration, or invalidation of intellectual property rights, patent litigation outcomes, and the end of co-promotion and licensing rights, with certain products already facing patent expirations or loss of regulatory exclusivity and increased generic competition. The company expects a moderate revenue impact from patent expiries during 2023-2025 and a more significant impact in 2026-2030 as more in-line products lose protection, while it continues to defend patent rights and support global patent protections. This section contains no additional market risk metrics or new disclosures in the 2025-11-04 Form 10-Q excerpt, instead directing readers to the prior annual report for the relevant risk information.

PG_10K_2025-08-04_full.txt.document_context: The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) filed a 10-K annual report covering its fiscal year ended 2025. The company says its products are sold in about 180 countries and territories, and it describes a business model focused on sustainable value creation through balanced top- and bottom-line growth, supported by R&D, consumer insights, marketing, and distribution.

sections.Item 1B: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states in Item 1B of its 2025 Form 10-K that there are no unresolved staff comments. The section indicates that the company had no outstanding comments from the SEC staff requiring resolution as of the filing date.
sections.Item 1: Procter & Gamble Company (P&G) is a world-leading multinational consumer goods company incorporated in Ohio in 1905, with roots in Cincinnati in 1837, selling trusted branded products in about 180 countries and territories. Its business model emphasizes sustainable value creation through balanced top- and bottom-line growth by creating, manufacturing, marketing, and distributing daily-use products, supported by research and development, consumer insights, advertising, promotions, and endorsements. P&G notes that segment information is in MD&A and Note 2 to the Consolidated Financial Statements, and that its SEC filings are available via the SEC and pginvestor.com, with copies also obtainable from EQ Shareowner Services.
sections.Preamble: Procter & Gamble Company (NYSE: PG), headquartered at One Procter & Gamble Plaza in Cincinnati, Ohio, filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025, under Commission File No. 1-434. The preamble identifies PG as a well-known seasoned issuer and lists its registered securities, including common stock and multiple NYSE-listed debt notes denominated in U.S. dollars, euros, and pounds sterling with maturities from 2026 through 2041.
sections.Item 1A: Procter & Gamble says its forward-looking statements in the 2025 Form 10-K are inherently uncertain and that its risk factors are not exhaustive, with investors directed to Management’s Discussion and Analysis and the Consolidated Financial Statements for context. The company highlights macroeconomic and financial risks from its global footprint—operations in about 70 countries, products sold in about 180 countries and territories, and more than 50% of annual net sales generated outside the U.S.—including foreign currency fluctuations, exchange-rate and pricing controls, higher U.S.-dollar supply costs, and exposure from significant debt, floating-rate and foreign-currency borrowings, and derivatives.
sections.Item 1C: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) says its cybersecurity risk management uses a multi-functional enterprise security team, senior management oversight from the CIO and CISO, and integration with the broader Enterprise Risk Management program to identify, assess, and respond to cyber threats. The company also relies on employee training, global and targeted phishing simulations, tabletop exercises, third-party consultants, and Third-Party Risk Management controls for vendors handling personal or high-risk business data, including data processing agreements and security assessments. As of the 2025 Form 10-K, P&G states it has not identified any cybersecurity threats that have materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, its business strategy, results of operations, or financial condition, and that the Board of Directors and Audit Committee oversee cybersecurity governance.
sections.Item 2: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) reports a global manufacturing and office footprint, owning and operating 24 U.S. manufacturing sites across 18 states and 75 manufacturing sites in 32 other countries, with many facilities producing for multiple businesses. The company’s product categories are supported by 21 Beauty sites, 17 Grooming sites, 20 Health Care sites, 34 Fabric & Home Care sites, and 36 Baby, Feminine & Family Care sites, and it owns its corporate headquarters in Cincinnati, Ohio. PG also owns or leases principal regional general offices in Switzerland, Panama, Singapore, China, and the United Arab Emirates, plus shared service centers in Costa Rica, the United Kingdom, and the Philippines, and states that its properties and equipment are adequate and well maintained.
sections.Item 3: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) says it is routinely subject to legal proceedings and claims involving antitrust and trade regulation, product liability, advertising, contracts, environmental issues, patent and trademark matters, labor and employment, and tax, and notes SEC disclosure requirements for certain environmental proceedings exceeding $1 million. The only specific matter disclosed is a U.K. Environment Agency action against wholly owned subsidiary Procter & Gamble UK over an inadvertent failure to secure a required permit for its London manufacturing site under the EU and U.K. Emission Trading Systems, with the site registered since March 2021 and the agency indicating in July 2025 that it intends to impose a civil penalty of less than $2 million.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Procter & Gamble Company’s 2025 Form 10-K is intentionally omitted and contains no substantive disclosure. The section provides no additional financial, operational, regulatory, product, or metric information beyond the placeholder notation.
sections.Item 4: Procter & Gamble Company’s Item 4 Mine Safety Disclosures states that the topic is “not applicable,” indicating no mine safety reporting obligations or related incidents for PG in this 2025 Form 10-K. The section then lists the company’s executive officers as of August 4, 2025, including Jon R. Moeller (Chairman, President and CEO), Shailesh Jejurikar (COO), Andre Schulten (CFO), and other leaders across Grooming, Health Care, Baby/Feminine/Family Care, Beauty, Fabric and Home Care, Research, Development and Innovation, Brand, Human Resources, and Legal.
sections.Item 5: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) reported open-market share repurchases of 4,317,165 shares in May 2025 at an average price of $162.14 per share, with no purchases in April or June, and noted that the repurchases were made under a Board-authorized program using operating cash flow and debt financing. The company said its fiscal 2025 share repurchase plan, announced July 30, 2024 and reaffirmed April 24, 2025, targeted $6 to $7 billion of direct repurchases, ended June 30, 2025, and totaled $6.5 billion. P&G also highlighted its dividend record, including 135 consecutive years of dividends since 1890, 69 consecutive years of dividend increases since 1956, and a 5% ten-year compound annual growth rate.
sections.Item 7A: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that the Item 7A Quantitative and Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk are incorporated by reference to the “Other Information” section in Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A) and Note 9 to the Consolidated Financial Statements. This section contains no standalone market risk discussion in the excerpt, instead directing readers to those referenced disclosures in the 2025 Form 10-K.
sections.Item 7: Procter & Gamble’s Item 7 MD&A opens with an extensive forward-looking statements disclaimer under the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995 and the Securities Act and Exchange Act, noting that projections, business plans, and expected operating results may differ materially from actual outcomes. It highlights key risks affecting PG’s financial condition and results of operations, including foreign currency fluctuations, global interest rates, tariffs, economic volatility, geopolitical and trade disruptions, credit market and banking partner stress, and the company’s ability to sustain share repurchases and dividend payments.
sections.Item 9: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable for its 2025 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The section contains no reported changes in auditors, no accounting or financial disclosure disagreements, and no related metrics or named counterparties beyond Procter & Gamble and the SEC filing context.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Procter & Gamble Company’s 2025 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including earnings, comprehensive income, balance sheets, shareholders’ equity, cash flows, and notes covering accounting policies, segment information, goodwill and intangible assets, income taxes, EPS, share-based compensation, pensions and postretirement benefits, fair value/risk management, debt, leases, contingencies, and supplier finance programs. The excerpt also includes management’s report on internal control over financial reporting, stating that P&G’s controls are designed to provide reasonable assurance under U.S. GAAP and are supported by the Worldwide Business Conduct Manual, written policies, segregation of duties, Global Leadership Council oversight, the Disclosure Committee, the Board of Directors, the Audit Committee, and Global Internal Audit.
sections.Item 9A: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) reports that Chairman, President and CEO Jon R. Moeller and CFO Andre Schulten evaluated disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) as of the end of the reporting period and concluded they were effective for timely recording, processing, summarizing, and communicating required SEC disclosure. The Item 9A discussion incorporates by reference the Item 8 “Management’s Report on Internal Control over Financial Reporting” and “Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm,” and states that no changes in internal control over financial reporting occurred in the fourth fiscal quarter that materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 9B: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) reported that during the fiscal year ended June 30, 2025, none of its directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement, as defined in Item 408 of Regulation S-K. The company also stated that it maintains insider trading policies and procedures governing transactions in PG securities by directors, officers, employees, and the company itself, and believes these controls are reasonably designed to promote compliance with insider trading laws, regulations, and applicable listing standards.
sections.Item 9C: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states in Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” that the disclosure is not applicable. This Part III section of the 2025 10-K contains no additional foreign jurisdiction, inspection, regulator, or subsidiary-related information beyond that statement.
sections.Item 11: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that Item 11 Executive Compensation is incorporated by reference to its 2025 Proxy Statement filed under Regulation 14A, to be filed within 120 days after June 30, 2025. The referenced proxy sections include Corporate Governance subsections on Board Meetings and Committees of the Board, Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation, and Risk Oversight - Compensation-Related Risk, plus Director Compensation through but not including Pay Versus Performance.
sections.Item 10: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that its Audit Committee members Brett Biggs, Christine McCarthy (Chair), and Patricia Woertz are independent and qualify as audit committee financial experts under SEC rules. The Item 10 disclosure is largely incorporated by reference to the 2025 Proxy Statement to be filed under Regulation 14A within 120 days after June 30, 2025, including sections on the Code of Ethics, Election of Directors, Board Meetings and Committees of the Board, and Shareholder Recommendations or Nominations of Director Candidates, while executive officers are reported in Part I pursuant to Item 401 of Regulation S-K.
sections.Item 13: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that the Item 13 disclosure on certain relationships, related transactions, and director independence is incorporated by reference from its 2025 Proxy Statement filed under Regulation 14A, which will be filed no later than 120 days after June 30, 2025. The referenced proxy statement sections are the Corporate Governance subsections titled Director Independence and Review and Approval of Transactions with Related Persons.
sections.Item 14: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) states that the Item 14 disclosure on principal accountant fees and services is incorporated by reference to the 2025 Proxy Statement filed under Regulation 14A, which will be filed no later than 120 days after June 30, 2025. The referenced material is the Audit Committee report, ending with the subsection entitled “Services Provided by Deloitte,” identifying Deloitte as the company’s principal accountant for this disclosure.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Procter & Gamble Company’s 2025 Form 10-K lists the consolidated financial statements and related reports incorporated by reference into Part II, Item 8, including management’s report on internal control over financial reporting, PCAOB Firm ID 34, and the independent registered public accounting firm’s reports on the consolidated financial statements and internal control. It also states that financial statement schedules are omitted because they are not required or the information is already included in the financial statements or notes, and begins the exhibit index with charter documents, bylaws/regulations, debt indenture materials, common stock description, and notes such as the company’s 1.200% Notes due 2025.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 of Procter & Gamble Company’s 2025 Form 10-K is the signatures section, filed August 4, 2025 in Cincinnati, Ohio, and states that the Form 10-K Summary is “not applicable.” It includes the required certifications under Sections 13 and 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, signed by Chairman, President and CEO Jon R. Moeller, CFO Andre Schulten, and Senior Vice President–Chief Accounting Officer Matthew W. Janzaruk, along with the board of directors including B. Marc Allen, Craig Arnold, Brett Biggs, Sheila Bonini, Amy L. Chang, Joseph Jimenez, Christopher J. Kempczinski, Debra L. Lee, Terry J. Lundgren, Christine M. McCarthy, Ashley McEvoy, Robert J. Portman, Rajesh Subramaniam, and Patricia A. Woertz.
sections.Item 12: Procter & Gamble Company (PG) discloses its equity compensation plans as of June 30, 2025, including the 1992, 2001, 2003, 2009, 2014, and 2019 Stock and Incentive Compensation Plans, with 103,310,926 total securities issuable under outstanding awards. The table shows 96,633,659 stock options/stock appreciation rights at a weighted average exercise price of $120.9524 and 6,677,267 RSUs/PSUs, and states that only the 2019 Plan permits future grants, with a 187 million-share cap and 58 million shares remaining available for issuance. The section also incorporates by reference the “Security Ownership of Management and Certain Beneficial Owners” subsection from PG’s 2025 Proxy Statement filed under Regulation 14A.

RTX_10K_2026-02-06_full.txt.document_context: RTX Corporation filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025, describing itself as an aerospace and defense company serving commercial, military, and government customers worldwide. The excerpt highlights that RTX operates through three principal segments—Collins Aerospace, Pratt & Whitney, and Raytheon—and that Collins provides a broad range of aftermarket services and advanced aircraft and space systems, including power generation, flight controls, avionics, landing systems, and cabin interiors.

sections.Preamble: RTX Corporation filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, with the SEC under Commission file number 001-00812, listing its principal executive offices at 1000 Wilson Boulevard, Arlington, Virginia 22209 and telephone number (781) 522-3000. The filing identifies RTX common stock ($1 par value) trading on the New York Stock Exchange under symbol RTX and 2.150% Notes due 2030 under symbol RTX 30, and states that RTX is a Delaware corporation, a well-known seasoned issuer, and a large accelerated filer that has filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.
sections.Item 1: RTX Corporation is an aerospace and defense company serving commercial, military, and government customers worldwide across original equipment, aftermarket parts and services, and defense programs as a prime contractor or subcontractor. Its three principal business segments are Collins Aerospace, Pratt & Whitney, and Raytheon; Collins Aerospace provides advanced aerospace and defense products and aftermarket services, including spare parts, overhaul and repair, engineering and technical support, fleet management, and systems such as avionics, landing gear, environmental control, propulsion components, cabin interiors, and space exploration support.
sections.Item 1B: RTX Corporation’s Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, states that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff as of the 2026-02-06 Form 10-K filing. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding regulatory review issues requiring further response or disclosure.
sections.Item 1A: RTX Corporation says its business, operating results, financial condition, and liquidity are highly exposed to U.S. government defense spending, since U.S. government sales make up a significant portion of consolidated sales and most revenues come from defense-related contracts with the U.S. Department of War (DoW), formerly the U.S. Department of Defense, plus other federal agencies. The company warns that shifts in policy, budget priorities, appropriations, shutdowns, continuing resolutions, stop-work orders, delayed contract awards, new program starts, and late payments could disrupt programs and materially hurt results, especially given the unpredictability of federal budgeting and broader geopolitical and macroeconomic conditions.
sections.Item 4: RTX Corporation’s Item 4 Mine Safety Disclosures in this 2026 Form 10-K states that the section is not applicable. The filing provides no mine safety incidents, citations, or related metrics for RTX Corporation in this disclosure.
sections.Item 2: RTX Corporation reports significant properties in approximately 25 countries, totaling about 450 properties and roughly 75 million square feet of productive space, with about 70% owned and 30% leased. As of December 31, 2025, approximately 70% of RTX’s significant property square footage was located in the United States, and its fixed assets included manufacturing and non-manufacturing facilities, warehouses, laboratories, office space, and substantial machinery and equipment that were described as in good operating condition and well-maintained.
sections.Item 5: RTX Corporation’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker “RTX,” and the company reported 35,327 registered shareowners as of December 31, 2025. The section compares RTX’s five-year cumulative total return, assuming reinvested dividends, against the S&P 500 Index and the S&P 500 Aerospace & Defense Index, showing RTX’s indexed value rising from $100.00 at December 31, 2020 to $287.64 at December 31, 2025 versus $196.16 and $230.45 for the two benchmarks. It also states that no equity securities were repurchased during the quarter ended December 31, 2025, while a Board-authorized share repurchase program remained in place with approximately $615 million available for future purchases.
sections.Item 3: RTX Corporation says it is subject to numerous lawsuits, investigations, and claims, but generally does not expect any unresolved matters to have a material adverse effect on its competitive position, results of operations, financial condition, or liquidity; it also notes a $1 million disclosure threshold for environmental proceedings under SEC rules. The section highlights a Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) enforcement action against Raytheon Company over alleged water discharge permit violations at a former Boulder, Colorado facility, resolved in October 2025 via a Compliance Order on Consent requiring a $458,211 civil penalty and remediation work, and it references Collins Aerospace’s involvement in 737 MAX aircraft litigation tied to Boeing and the Lion Air Flight 610 and Ethiopian Airlines Flight 302 accidents.
sections.Item 7: RTX Corporation’s MD&A explains how its consolidated financial statements, accounting policies, critical estimates, and forward-looking business outlook should be read alongside Item 8 and Item 1A risk factors, with emphasis on changes in key financial items and the factors driving them. RTX describes itself as a global systems provider to the aerospace and defense industries operating through Collins Aerospace, Pratt & Whitney, and Raytheon, serving OEM, aftermarket, and government defense customers across domestic and international markets. The section highlights exposure to industrial, economic, political, and regulatory conditions, including legislation affecting greenhouse gas emissions, carbon pricing, and energy taxes, as well as the differing cycle lengths of commercial aerospace spares, maintenance, and defense development contracts.
sections.Item 7A: RTX Corporation says its primary market risk exposures are foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates affecting cash, debt, and derivative instruments, and it uses sensitivity analysis to measure those risks. The company hedges foreign currency exposure with forward contracts and net investment hedges for foreign operations, including intercompany loans and deposits, with outstanding foreign currency hedge notional principal of $26 billion at December 31, 2025 and $17 billion at December 31, 2024. RTX states that a 10% unfavorable exchange-rate move would have increased unrealized losses on its foreign currency contract portfolio by $0.9 billion in 2025 and $1.0 billion in 2024, but that these losses are offset by underlying hedged transactions and do not create material market risk.
sections.Item 8: RTX Corporation’s Item 8 financial statements section includes management’s report on internal control over financial reporting, stating that RTX assessed its controls as of December 31, 2025 using the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) and concluded they were effective. The section also notes that PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP audited RTX’s internal control over financial reporting and the consolidated financial statements for the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, with signed officers including Chairman and CEO Christopher T. Calio, CFO Neil G. Mitchill, Jr., and Controller Amy L. Johnson.
sections.Item 9: RTX Corporation states in Item 9 of its 2026 Form 10-K that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its independent accountants on accounting principles, financial statement disclosure, or auditing scope or procedure. The section is a brief “None” disclosure, indicating no reportable issues with accounting or financial disclosure for the annual report filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 9A: RTX Corporation states that, under Rule 13a-15 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, management—including the Chairman and CEO, Executive Vice President and CFO, and Senior Vice President and Controller—evaluated disclosure controls and procedures and concluded they were effective to provide reasonable assurance that required information is recorded, processed, summarized, reported on time, and communicated for timely disclosure decisions. The company also reports that its internal control over financial reporting, assessed as of December 31, 2025 using the COSO 2013 Internal Control—Integrated Framework, was effective, and that this conclusion was audited by PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP in Item 8 of the Form 10-K.
sections.Item 11: RTX Corporation states that the Item 11 executive compensation disclosure is incorporated by reference to its 2026 Annual Meeting Proxy Statement. The referenced proxy sections include “Compensation Discussion & Analysis,” “Compensation of Directors,” “Equity Award Granting Policy,” and the “Report of the Human Capital & Compensation Committee,” indicating that detailed pay, director compensation, equity grant practices, and committee oversight are contained in the proxy materials rather than the 10-K itself.
sections.Item 9C: RTX Corporation’s Item 9C disclosure states that the company is “Not applicable” with respect to foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections. This section appears in Part III of the 2026 RTX 10-K filed with the SEC on 2026-02-06 and contains no additional substantive disclosure beyond the applicability statement.
sections.Item 13: RTX Corporation states that the Item 13 disclosure on certain relationships and related transactions is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement for the 2026 Annual Meeting of Shareowners. The referenced proxy sections are “Corporate Governance” under “Director Independence” and “Other Important Information” under “Transactions with Related Persons,” indicating that director independence and related-person transactions are addressed outside the 10-K text.
sections.Item 9B: During the quarter ended December 31, 2025, RTX Corporation reported that no director or officer, as defined in Rule 16a-1(f), adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement. The disclosure references Item 408(a) of Regulation S-K and indicates no insider trading plan changes occurred in the period.
sections.Item 10: Item 10 states that RTX Corporation’s director, audit committee, audit committee financial expert, and shareholder nominee procedures are incorporated by reference to its 2026 Proxy Statement sections “Election of Directors” and “Corporate Governance.” It also identifies RTX’s executive officers as of February 6, 2026, including Chairman/CEO/President Christopher T. Calio, CFO Neil G. Mitchill, Jr., General Counsel Ramsaran Maharajh, Jr., CHRO Dantaya M. Williams, Treasurer Kevin G. DaSilva, Controller Amy L. Johnson, and business-unit leaders Troy Brunk (Collins Aerospace), Shane G. Eddy (Pratt & Whitney), and Philip J. Jasper (Raytheon).
sections.Item 1C: RTX Corporation says its aerospace and defense businesses face advanced, persistent cyber-attacks targeting IT systems, sensitive unclassified and classified information, and products and services integrated with third-party systems across commercial, government, and military customers. The company describes a robust cybersecurity program covering both enterprise systems and products/services, aligned with National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) standards, with controls for new IT deployments, vulnerability management, regular testing, annual employee and contractor training, and simulated phishing exercises.
sections.Item 12: RTX Corporation’s Item 12 cross-references the “Share Ownership” section of its 2026 Proxy Statement for beneficial ownership and management holdings, and provides equity compensation plan information as of December 31, 2025. The company reported 22,606,067 securities issuable under shareowner-approved plans at a weighted-average exercise price of $89.57, with 101,179,202 shares remaining available for future issuance and no plans not approved by shareowners. The outstanding awards include nonqualified stock options, stock appreciation rights (SARs), restricted stock units (RSUs), performance share units (PSUs), and deferred stock units under the RTX Corporation Board of Directors Deferred Stock Unit Plan, with SAR valuation based on the NYSE closing price of $183.40 on the last trading day of 2025.
sections.Item 14: RTX Corporation states that the Item 14 principal accountant fees and services disclosure is incorporated by reference to its 2026 Proxy Statement section titled “Appointment of PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP to Serve as Independent Auditor for 2026.” The referenced proxy materials provide the fee breakdown for PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, including Audit Fees, Audit-Related Fees, Tax Fees, and All Other Fees.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of RTX Corporation’s 2026 Form 10-K lists the included audited financial statements and notes for 2025, 2024, and 2023, along with the report of independent registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID 238), and states that all financial statement schedules were omitted because they were not required, not applicable, or already included elsewhere. It also provides the exhibits index, including the Separation and Distribution Agreement among United Technologies Corporation, Otis Worldwide Corporation, and Carrier Global Corporation, RTX’s amended and restated certificate of incorporation and bylaws, and an amended and restated indenture with The Bank of New York as trustee, with many items incorporated by reference to prior SEC filings.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains RTX Corporation’s 10-K signature page and states “FORM 10-K SUMMARY Not applicable.” It records the February 6, 2026 execution of the annual report by Christopher T. Calio (Chairman and Chief Executive Officer), Neil G. Mitchill, Jr. (Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer), and Amy L. Johnson (Senior Vice President and Controller), along with the signatures of directors including Tracy A. Atkinson, Leanne G. Caret, Bernard A. Harris, Jr., George R. Oliver, Ellen M. Pawlikowski, Denise L. Ramos, Fredric G. Reynolds, Brian C. Rogers, James A. Winnefeld, Jr., and Robert O. Work, and notes Ramsaran Maharajh, Jr. as Executive Vice President and General Counsel.

SBUX_10K_2025-11-14_full.txt.document_context: Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) filed a 10-K annual report, but the excerpt does not specify the fiscal period covered. The excerpt only shows “Unresolved Staff Comments: None,” indicating there were no outstanding staff comments disclosed in this section.

sections.Item 1B: Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding regulatory comment issues requiring disclosure in its 2025 Form 10-K filed on 2025-11-14.
sections.Preamble: Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended September 28, 2025, identifying its principal executive office at 2401 Utah Avenue South, Seattle, Washington 98134 and its IRS Employer ID 91-1325671. The filing states that Starbucks common stock, par value $0.001 per share, is registered on the Nasdaq Global Select Market, and that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files.
sections.Item 1C: Starbucks Corporation (SBUX) describes a cybersecurity program aligned with the National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework and integrated into its Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) governance, with oversight by management, the Board, and internal audit. The company says it uses policies, monitoring tools, phishing simulations, penetration tests, and annual employee awareness training to identify, detect, and protect against threats while making ongoing investments to address evolving risks and compliance requirements. Starbucks also maintains and periodically tests an incident response plan with cross-functional teams, escalation protocols, and processes to inform senior management and the Board and comply with legal obligations.
sections.Item 6: This section is a brief transition into Starbucks Corporation’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis, stating that the company’s financial condition and results of operations should be read with the consolidated financial statements and notes in the 2025 Form 10-K. It emphasizes that the discussion contains forward-looking statements subject to risks and uncertainties identified in Item 1A Risk Factors, notes that Starbucks’ fiscal year ends on the Sunday closest to September 30, and says fiscal years 2025, 2024, and 2023 each included 52 weeks, with store-count data reported net of closures.
sections.Item 7: Starbucks Corporation’s MD&A describes three reportable operating segments—North America, International, and Channel Development—and emphasizes the company’s core growth drivers of new store openings, comparable store sales, and operating margin management, with unallocated corporate expenses reported in Corporate and Other. Fiscal 2025 results reflected progress on “Back to Starbucks” initiatives, including the Green Apron Service model, added staffing and hours, and Leadership Experience 2025, while consolidated net revenues rose 3% to $37.2 billion from $36.2 billion in fiscal 2024. The discussion also notes North America and U.S. revenue increased 1% on 4% net new company-operated store growth, or 441 stores, before 584 North America restructuring closures late in the fourth quarter, alongside contributions from the Global Coffee Alliance and the acquisition of 23.5 Degrees Topco Limited.
sections.Item 8: Starbucks Corporation’s Item 8 financial statements show total net revenues of $37.2 billion for fiscal 2025, up from $36.2 billion in 2024, driven primarily by company-operated stores ($30.7 billion) and other revenue ($2.1 billion), while licensed store revenue declined to $4.35 billion. Operating income fell sharply to $2.94 billion from $5.41 billion as store operating expenses rose to $17.1 billion and restructuring and impairments reached $892.0 million, reducing net earnings attributable to Starbucks to $1.86 billion and diluted EPS to $1.63.

TGT_10K_2025-03-12_full.txt.document_context: Target Corporation filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2025-02-01. The company said it operates as a single segment and uses stores, digital channels, fulfillment services, and its loyalty ecosystem to drive traffic, sales, and market share growth. Target also highlighted its strategy to expand owned brands and signature partnerships, open new stores, update existing stores, and grow capabilities such as Roundel advertising and the Target Plus marketplace.

sections.Item 1B: Target Corporation’s 2024 Form 10-K states that Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, is not applicable. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding unresolved comments from the SEC staff in connection with the filing.
sections.Preamble: Target Corporation’s Form 10-K is the annual report for the fiscal year ended February 1, 2025, filed under Commission File Number 1-6049 with the SEC under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. It identifies Target as a Minnesota corporation headquartered at 1000 Nicollet Mall, Minneapolis, Minnesota 55403, with common stock trading on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol TGT and par value of $0.0833 per share. The filing also states that Target is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, has filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files for the prior 12 months, and has no securities registered under Section 12(g).
sections.Item 1C: Target Corporation says its cybersecurity, information security, and data privacy program is designed to safely receive, protect, store, use, and share guest, team member, vendor, and third-party information, while detecting, containing, and responding to security incidents. Oversight is shared by the Board of Directors, the Audit & Risk Committee, and management, including the Chief Information and Product Officer, Chief Information Security Officer, Chief Legal & Compliance Officer, and Chief Corporate Affairs Officer, with regular reporting on cyber threats, controls, and enterprise risk management.
sections.Item 1A: Target Corporation says its results could be materially harmed if it cannot differentiate its guest experience through price, merchandise assortment, store environment, convenience, guest service, loyalty programs, advertising, and marketing, especially as consumers increasingly shop through digital channels and compare prices and convenience online. The risk factors emphasize competition, reputational sensitivity, and the cost and complexity of expanding digital channels, fulfillment options, and new technology, including artificial intelligence and generative AI, while maintaining in-stock levels, responsible sourcing, store safety and cleanliness, and personalized guest experiences.
sections.Item 4: Target Corporation (NYSE: TGT) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on March 12, 2025. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or related disclosures, and simply transitions into Part II of the annual report.
sections.Item 2: Target Corporation’s Item 2 — Properties discloses that, as of February 1, 2025, it operated 1,978 stores across all 50 U.S. states and the District of Columbia, totaling 248,278 thousand retail square feet, with the largest footprints in Texas (157 stores), California (318), Florida (132), and New York (107). The company also reported 66 supply chain facilities, including distribution centers and sortation centers, totaling 68.5 million square feet, and noted that its stores and facilities are generally owned, leased, or on owned buildings on leased land, with corporate headquarters and office space in the Minneapolis, Minnesota area and elsewhere in the U.S.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 is reserved and contains no substantive discussion in Target Corporation’s 2024 Form 10-K. The section only shows the filing navigation text, including “Management’s Discussion and Analysis,” “Executive Overview & Financial Summary,” and the “Index to Financial Statements,” with no additional financial metrics, business updates, or named regulatory matters.
sections.Item 3: Target Corporation’s Item 3 discloses ongoing federal securities litigation: a prior class action in the U.S. District Court for the District of Minnesota was dismissed on November 15, 2024, while two new purported class actions filed on January 31, 2025 and February 20, 2025 in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Florida name Target and certain Board members as defendants. The complaints allege violations of Exchange Act Sections 10(b), 14(a), and 20(a) and Rules 10b-5 and 14a-9, claiming Target misled investors about risks tied to ESG initiatives, including diversity, equity, and inclusion, oversight of those risks, and its 2023 Pride Month merchandise collection, with proposed shareholder classes spanning March 9, 2022 to August 16, 2023 and August 26, 2022 to November 19, 2024.
sections.Item 7: Target Corporation said its 2024 strategy focused on strengthening core retail offerings, expanding differentiated and exclusive merchandise, and creating new growth channels through owned brands, Target Plus, Target Circle, Roundel, and AI-enabled operations. The company highlighted 2,000 new wellness products, exclusive collaborations tied to Taylor Swift, Wicked, Diane von Furstenberg, and celebrities including Dwayne “The Rock” Johnson and Tom Holland, 11 owned brands above $1 billion in annual sales, 23 new store openings, and more than 65% of digital sales fulfilled through same-day options, while reporting GAAP and adjusted diluted EPS of $8.86 for fiscal 2024.
sections.Item 8: Target Corporation’s Item 8 presents the 2024 consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including the statements of operations, comprehensive income, financial position, cash flows, shareholders’ investment, and notes covering accounting policies, net sales, inventory, leases, income taxes, debt, derivatives, share repurchases, pensions, and segment reporting. The section also includes management’s report asserting that the financial statements were prepared in accordance with U.S. GAAP and that Target’s internal control system provides reasonable assurance over asset safeguarding and transaction execution, with Board oversight through the Audit & Risk Committee.
sections.Item 7A: Target Corporation says its primary market risk as of February 1, 2025 was interest rate exposure on debt obligations, floating-rate short-term investments, and derivative hedges, with floating-rate investments exceeding floating-rate debt by about $1.7 billion. A 1 percentage point rise in floating rates would increase earnings before income taxes by $17 million, while a 1 percentage point decrease in the weighted average pension discount rate would raise annual expense by $33 million; Target also notes it had hedged 70% of the interest rate exposure of its qualified defined benefit pension plan liabilities. The company further discloses net present value liabilities for general liability and workers’ compensation, and offsetting investments in life insurance contracts and prepaid forward contracts on Target common stock for nonqualified deferred compensation plans, with no other material changes in market risk management.
sections.Item 9B: On December 5, 2024, Christina Hennington, Target Corporation’s Executive Vice President and Chief Strategy and Growth Officer, adopted a written stock trading plan intended to satisfy the affirmative defense conditions of Rule 10b5-1(c) under the Exchange Act. The plan covers 13,514 shares of Target common stock in total, including sales of 11,965 shares and a gift of 1,549 shares, and is scheduled to expire on April 2, 2026.
sections.Item 5: Target Corporation’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “TGT,” with authorization to issue up to 6.0 billion shares of common stock and 5.0 million shares of preferred stock; as of March 5, 2025, Target had 12,240 shareholders of record. The Board’s August 11, 2021 $15 billion share repurchase program has no expiration, and through February 1, 2025 Target had repurchased 31.0 million shares for $6.3 billion, including 3,697,484 shares during the three months ended February 1, 2025 at an average price of $136.80 per share, leaving $8.67 billion available for future buybacks.
sections.Item 1: Target Corporation, incorporated in Minnesota in 1902, operates as a single segment retailer serving “guests” with fashionable, differentiated merchandise and everyday essentials at discounted prices through stores and digital channels, and it has donated 5% of profit to communities since 1946. Its strategy emphasizes curated multi-category assortment, owned brands, national brands, Target Circle loyalty, new and remodeled stores, digital experience, supply chain transformation, and discovery-driven channels including the Target app, website, social platforms, Roundel advertising, and the Target Plus third-party marketplace. The company says these efforts are intended to drive traffic, sales, and market share growth through team engagement, consumer-centricity, technology including artificial intelligence, efficiency, and sustainability.
sections.Item 9C: Item 9C states that disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is “Not applicable” for Target Corporation’s 2024 Form 10-K. The section then notes that most Part III governance and proxy-related information is incorporated by reference from Target’s definitive Proxy Statement for the Annual Meeting of Shareholders on June 11, 2025, including Item 10 directors and executive officers, Item 11 executive compensation, Item 12 security ownership and equity compensation plan information, and Item 13 related-person transactions and director independence.
sections.Item 9A: Target Corporation reported that during the most recently completed fiscal quarter there were no changes in internal control over financial reporting that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, its internal control. Management, including the chief executive officer and chief financial officer, evaluated disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15 and 15d-15 and concluded they were effective at a reasonable assurance level, with disclosure controls designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and reporting of information required in SEC filings.
sections.Item 9: Target Corporation (NYSE: TGT) reports that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable for the 2025 10-K filed on March 12, 2025. The filing discloses no changes in independent accountants and no disagreements regarding accounting principles, financial statement disclosure, or auditing matters.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Target Corporation’s 2025 Form 10-K lists the consolidated financial statements for fiscal years ended February 1, 2025, February 3, 2024, and January 28, 2023, including statements of operations, comprehensive income, financial position, cash flows, shareholders’ investment, notes, and the independent registered public accounting firm’s report (PCAOB ID: 42). It also states that no financial statement schedules are included and provides an exhibit index covering Target’s amended and restated articles of incorporation, bylaws, indenture agreements with Bank One Trust Company, N.A. and The Bank of New York Trust Company, N.A., a description of securities, and executive compensation plans such as the Executive Officer Cash Incentive Plan and Long-Term Incentive Plan.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains no Form 10-K summary and consists primarily of Target Corporation’s signatures and authorization statements for its 2024 Annual Report filed March 12, 2025. The filing is signed on behalf of the registrant by Brian C. Cornell (Chair of the Board and Chief Executive Officer), Jim Lee (Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer and Attorney-in-fact), and Matthew A. Liegel (Senior Vice President, Chief Accounting Officer and Controller), with the board majority listed including David P. Abney, Douglas M. Baker, Jr., George S. Barrett, Gail K. Boudreaux, Robert L. Edwards, Donald R. Knauss, Christine A. Leahy, Monica C. Lozano, Grace Puma, Erica W. Rice, and Dmitri L. Stockton.
sections.Item 14: Target Corporation’s Item 14 incorporates by reference the Proxy Statement section on principal accountant fees and services, specifically the ratification of Ernst & Young LLP as the company’s independent registered public accounting firm. It also references the audit and non-audit fees disclosure for Ernst & Young LLP, as included in the “Item two” proxy matter.

TMO_10K_2025-02-20_full.txt.document_context: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024-12-31. The company says it is the world leader in serving science and operates four segments—Life Sciences Solutions, Analytical Instruments, Specialty Diagnostics, and Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services—serving pharmaceutical, biotech, hospital, diagnostic, university, government, and industrial customers. It highlights growth through organic investment in R&D, capacity and capabilities, and acquisitions, with brands including Thermo Scientific, Applied Biosystems, Invitrogen, Fisher Scientific, Unity Lab Services, Patheon, and PPD.

sections.Preamble: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 168 Third Avenue, Waltham, Massachusetts 02451, with SEC file number 1-8002 and employer identification number 04-2209186. The filing lists Thermo Fisher’s common stock (NYSE: TMO) and multiple NYSE-listed debt securities, including notes due from 2025 through 2049, and states that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and has timely filed all required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. describes itself as the world leader in serving science, with a mission to help customers make the world healthier, cleaner and safer through brands including Thermo Scientific, Applied Biosystems, Invitrogen, Fisher Scientific, Unity Lab Services, Patheon and PPD. The company serves pharmaceutical and biotech companies, hospitals, clinical diagnostic labs, universities, research institutions, government agencies, and industrial/environmental customers, and says it grows through R&D, capacity investments and acquisitions to improve productivity and accelerate innovation. Thermo Fisher reports four business segments—Life Sciences Solutions, Analytical Instruments, Specialty Diagnostics, and Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services—with Life Sciences Solutions providing reagents, instruments and consumables for biological and medical research, drug and vaccine discovery/production, and infection and disease diagnosis through its Biosciences, Genetic Sciences, and BioProduction businesses.
sections.Item 1A: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. says its Item 1A Risk Factors include forward-looking statements and material risks to investors, with a specific focus on cybersecurity governance and oversight. The Board of Directors delegates cybersecurity oversight to the Audit Committee, while the company’s senior vice president and chief information officer and vice president and chief information security officer (CISO) lead the cybersecurity program, supported by the corporate IT security team and quarterly Audit Committee updates. Thermo Fisher maintains ISO/IEC 27001:2013 certification through annual internal and external audits covering cybersecurity governance, operations, product security, investigations, architecture and engineering, and security awareness and training, and integrates cybersecurity into quarterly business reviews, annual budget planning, and risk-based engagements.
sections.Item 1B: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE: TMO) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there are no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. This section indicates the company had no outstanding regulatory issues or pending correspondence with the Securities and Exchange Commission requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 3: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) states that it is subject to various lawsuits and claims involving product liability, intellectual property, employment, and commercial issues. The company refers readers to Note 5 to its Consolidated Financial Statements, “Commitments and Contingencies,” for additional information on these legal proceedings.
sections.Item 2: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) states that it owns and leases office, engineering, laboratory, production, and warehouse space worldwide. This Item 2 — Properties section provides a broad overview of the company’s global real estate footprint supporting its operations across research, manufacturing, and distribution functions.
sections.Item 1C: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. says cybersecurity is embedded in its enterprise risk management through a risk-based “defense in depth” program designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of company data and systems while supporting business objectives and customer expectations. The company uses layered technologies, processes, and controls, includes third-party technology providers in its IT procurement security assessments, and trains employees through security awareness programs, periodic cyber-event simulations, and annual attestation to its Technology Acceptable Use Policy. Thermo Fisher states it does not reasonably believe any current cybersecurity incidents have materially affected, or are reasonably likely to materially affect, its business strategy, results of operations, or financial condition.
sections.Item 4: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on 2025-02-20. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or regulatory disclosures.
sections.Item 5: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE: TMO) reported that, as of February 1, 2025, it had 2,173 holders of record of its common stock, excluding street or nominee holdings. In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company repurchased 1,888,612 shares at an average price of $529.49 per share, all under its publicly announced repurchase program. On November 15, 2024, the Board replaced the prior authorization with a new $4.0 billion share repurchase program; by February 20, 2025, Thermo Fisher had already repurchased $2.0 billion (3.6 million shares) in early Q1 2025, leaving $1.0 billion available.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and states “Not applicable,” indicating that no selected financial data or related disclosure is provided in this section.
sections.Item 7: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s Item 7 MD&A explains its business as a provider of life sciences research, analytical solutions, diagnostics, and biopharma services across four segments: Life Sciences Solutions, Analytical Instruments, Specialty Diagnostics, and Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services. For 2024, the company reported revenues of $42.879 billion versus $42.857 billion in 2023, GAAP operating income of $7.337 billion, adjusted operating income of $9.707 billion, GAAP diluted EPS of $16.53, and adjusted EPS of $21.86, while organic revenue growth, acquisitions, and currency translation were all 0%. The section also emphasizes use of non-GAAP measures reconciled to GAAP and references the SEC-filed 2023 Form 10-K and Note 11 to the consolidated financial statements.
sections.Item 9A: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s Item 9A discusses the audit framework for its consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting, with the independent public accounting firm stating it is registered with the PCAOB and independent under SEC and U.S. federal securities laws. The section explains that the audits were conducted under PCAOB standards to obtain reasonable assurance against material misstatement due to error or fraud, and that procedures included risk assessment, testing of evidence, evaluation of accounting principles and management estimates, and testing the design and operating effectiveness of internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 7A: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) says its market risk exposure is primarily to interest rate and foreign currency exchange rate movements, which it manages through normal operating and financing activities, fixed-rate debt hedging with interest rate swaps, and short-term forward and option contracts. The currency hedges cover balance sheet and operational exposures from purchases, sales, cash, and intercompany loans denominated in euro, Canadian dollars, British pounds sterling, Swedish krona, Singapore dollars, Hong Kong dollars, and Swiss franc, and the company states it does not use speculative derivatives. As of December 31, 2024, Thermo Fisher’s debt portfolio was mainly fixed-rate borrowings with an estimated fair value of $28.53 billion; a 100 basis point decrease in rates would increase debt fair value by about $1.98 billion, while a 100 basis point increase would reduce it by about $1.76 billion, and cross-currency interest rate swaps would change by
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data for the years ended December 31, 2024, 2023, and 2022, including balance sheets, income, comprehensive income, cash flows, and redeemable noncontrolling interest and equity, along with notes covering debt, fair value, taxes, derivatives, segments, acquisitions, leases, pensions, and stock-based compensation. The section also includes the Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm (PCAOB ID 238), which states that Thermo Fisher’s consolidated financial statements fairly present its financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in all material respects and that internal control over financial reporting was audited under COSO’s Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013).
sections.Item 10: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. states that the Item 10 disclosures on directors, corporate governance, and insider trading arrangements are not fully included in the Form 10-K but will be provided in the company’s Proxy Statement filed with the SEC within 120 days after fiscal year-end and incorporated by reference. The filing also notes that executive officer information is contained in Item 1 of Part I, and that additional required governance information will appear in the Proxy Statement under “Corporate governance” and “Executive compensation.”
sections.Item 9B: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. disclosed that its Board of Directors amended and restated the company’s By-Laws on February 19, 2025, effective immediately, as part of a periodic corporate governance review that referenced recent Delaware case law; the changes primarily updated advance notice and proxy access provisions with clarifying and procedural revisions. The section also reports Rule 10b5-1(c) trading plans under the Exchange Act adopted by senior vice president and general counsel Michael A. Boxer on December 5, 2024 and executive vice president Michael D. Shafer on November 20, 2024, covering vested stock option exercises and sales of up to 7,450 shares and up to 2,509 shares plus 10,725 shares, respectively, through June 11, 2025 and December 12/15, 2025. It further states that Item 9C, Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections, is not applicable
sections.Item 11: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE: TMO) states that the information required by Item 11, including executive compensation details, is not presented in the 10-K itself but is incorporated by reference from the company’s Proxy Statement. The referenced proxy materials are expected to contain the relevant “Corporate governance” and “Executive compensation” disclosures for the annual report filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 12: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. states that the information required by Item 12 on security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management, and related stockholder matters, is not presented in the Form 10-K itself but is incorporated by reference from the company’s Proxy Statement. The referenced proxy disclosure includes “Information about stock ownership” and is intended to provide the ownership details for management and other beneficial owners.
sections.Item 13: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. states that the information required by Item 13, “Certain Relationships and Related Transactions, and Director Independence,” is not presented in the Form 10-K itself but is incorporated by reference from the company’s Proxy Statement. The referenced Proxy Statement is expected to include the relevant “Corporate governance” disclosures for Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO).
sections.Item 14: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) states that the information required by Item 14, including principal accountant fees and services, is contained in its Proxy Statement under “Audit matters” and is incorporated into the Form 10-K by reference. This Item 14 section is part of Part IV of the company’s 2025 annual report filed with the SEC.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 lists Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s consolidated financial statements and notes that all financial statement schedules are omitted because they are not applicable, not required, or already included elsewhere in the filing. It then catalogs the company’s incorporated-by-reference exhibits, including governance documents such as the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation, amended and restated By-Laws effective February 19, 2025, and debt instruments and supplemental indentures involving The Bank of New York Mellon Trust Company, N.A. under Regulation S-K Item 601(b)(4)(iii)(A).
sections.Item 9: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosure,” is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The section indicates there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements regarding accounting or financial disclosure to report.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.’s Form 10-K signatures page for the year ended February 20, 2025, confirming the report was duly signed under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. It names Marc N. Casper as Chairman, President and Chief Executive Officer (Principal Executive Officer), Stephen Williamson as Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Officer (Principal Financial Officer), and Joseph R. Holmes as Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer (Principal Accounting Officer), along with directors Jennifer M. Johnson, R. Alexandra Keith, James C. Mullen, Nelson J. Chai, Debora L. Spar, Ruby R. Chandy, Scott M. Sperling, C. Martin Harris, Dion J. Weisler, and Tyler E. Jacks.

TSLA_10K_2022Q4_2023-01-31_full.txt.document_context: Tesla Inc filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2022-12-31. The company designs, manufactures, sells and leases electric vehicles and energy generation/storage systems, operates two reportable segments (automotive and energy generation and storage), and in December 2022 began early production and deliveries of the Tesla Semi.

sections.Preamble: Tesla, Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022, under Commission File Number 001-34756. The filing identifies Tesla as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 1 Tesla Road, Austin, Texas 78725, with common stock trading on The Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol TSLA, and states that Tesla is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer.
sections.Item 1B: Tesla Inc. states that there were no unresolved staff comments from the SEC staff in Item 1B of its 2023 Form 10-K annual report filed on January 31, 2023. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding comments or issues requiring further resolution with the Securities and Exchange Commission.
sections.Item 1A: Tesla says its business, financial condition, and operating results could be materially harmed by macroeconomic conditions tied to the global COVID-19 pandemic, including persistent changes in trade and consumer behavior, port congestion, supplier shutdowns, semiconductor shortages, labor shortages, and higher labor costs. The company highlights risks to production ramp-ups at Gigafactory Berlin-Brandenburg and Gigafactory Texas, dependence on supplier solvency and government cooperation for travel and visas, and notes that 2022 COVID-19 spikes in Shanghai temporarily shut Gigafactory Shanghai and parts of Tesla’s supply chain, affecting vehicle deliveries.
sections.Item 1: Tesla Inc. designs, manufactures, sells and leases high-performance fully electric vehicles and energy generation and storage systems, primarily through direct sales supported by a global network of service centers, Mobile Service, body shops, Supercharger stations and Destination Chargers. The company’s mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy, and it emphasizes performance, safety, lower cost of ownership, vertically integrated operations, and ongoing development of full self-driving technology. Tesla reports two segments—automotive and energy generation and storage—and its automotive lineup includes Model 3, Model Y, Model S and Model X, with early production and deliveries of the Tesla Semi beginning in December 2022.
sections.Item 2: Tesla Inc. is headquartered in Austin, Texas and maintains a global property footprint across North America, Europe, and Asia for manufacturing and assembly, warehousing, engineering, retail and service locations, Supercharger sites, and administrative and sales offices. The company says these facilities support both reporting segments and are suitable and adequate for its business, with most locations leased except certain manufacturing sites, including owned Gigafactory Texas, Fremont Factory, Gigafactory Nevada, and Gigafactory Berlin-Brandenburg, plus Gigafactory Shanghai where Tesla owns the building and 50-year land use rights, and leased Gigafactory New York and Megafactory in Lathrop, California.
sections.Item 4: Tesla Inc. states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2022 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on 2023-01-31. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or related metrics, and no additional regulators or entities are discussed.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Tesla, Inc.’s 2022 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive discussion or disclosed information in this section. The section appears only as a placeholder in the SEC EDGAR filing, with no additional financial, operational, regulatory, or product details provided.
sections.Item 5: Tesla, Inc.’s common stock has traded on The NASDAQ Global Select Market under the symbol “TSLA” since June 29, 2010, following its initial public offering priced at approximately $1.13 per share as adjusted for the 2020 five-for-one stock split and the 2022 stock split. As of January 25, 2023, Tesla reported 8,686 holders of record, stated that it has never paid cash dividends and does not expect to do so in the foreseeable future, and disclosed no unregistered sales of equity securities or issuer/affiliated repurchases. The section also describes a stock performance graph comparing Tesla’s cumulative total return from January 1, 2018 through December 31, 2022 against the NASDAQ Composite Index and SIC code 3711 motor vehicle peers, with the graph expressly not filed under the Exchange Act or Securities Act.
sections.Item 3: Tesla Inc. discloses material legal proceedings under Item 103 of Regulation S-K and refers readers to Note 15, Commitments and Contingencies, noting that environmental-regulation matters and aggregate civil penalties could exceed $1 million and that material proceedings may involve penalties far above that threshold. The section highlights a German Umweltbundesamt enforcement action against Tesla’s Germany subsidiary over market participation notifications and end-of-life battery take-back obligations, where a 12 million euro fine was reduced to 1.45 million euro and then settled at 600,000 euro with no appeal, as well as an investigation by district attorneys in certain California counties into Tesla’s waste segregation practices under Cal. Health & Saf. Code section 25100 et seq. and Cal. Civil Code § 1798.80, which Tesla says is not currently expected to materially adversely affect the business.
sections.Item 9: Tesla Inc. reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in its 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The Item 9 section states simply: “None.”
sections.Item 7A: Tesla Inc. says its global operations create foreign currency risk across revenue, cost of revenue, operating expenses, and localized subsidiary debt denominated mainly in Chinese yuan, euro, pound sterling, and Norwegian krone, and it generally does not hedge this exposure. The company notes that exchange-rate movements affect U.S.-dollar reported results and can cause gains or losses on intercompany and cash balances, with a reasonably possible 10% adverse currency move estimated to have changed pre-tax income by $473 million at December 31, 2022 and $277 million at December 31, 2021.
sections.Item 7: Tesla Inc. says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy through high-performance fully electric vehicles, solar energy generation systems, energy storage products, and related services, while increasingly emphasizing artificial intelligence, robotics, automation, and Full Self-Driving (FSD) capabilities. In 2022, Tesla produced 1,369,611 consumer vehicles and delivered 1,313,851, deployed 6.5 GWh of energy storage and 348 MW of solar systems, and generated $81.46 billion in total revenues, up $27.64 billion year over year, despite supply chain, logistics, and factory shutdown challenges. Net income attributable to common stockholders rose to $12.56 billion, cash and cash equivalents plus investments ended at $22.19 billion, and management highlighted ongoing expansion of manufacturing capacity, global infrastructure, battery technology, Solar Roof installation, and profitability through operational efficiencies.
sections.Item 9A: Tesla’s Item 9A section describes the independent auditor’s opinion on the company’s consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting, emphasizing audits conducted under PCAOB standards and in compliance with U.S. federal securities laws, SEC rules, and PCAOB independence requirements. The excerpt highlights audit procedures for assessing risks of material misstatement due to error or fraud, testing amounts and disclosures, evaluating accounting principles and management estimates, and examining the design and operating effectiveness of internal controls to support reasonable assurance.
sections.Item 9C: Tesla Inc. states in Item 9C of its 2023 Form 10-K that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is not applicable. This section contains no additional substantive information beyond the transition to Part III of the annual report.
sections.Item 9B: Tesla Inc. (TSLA) states in Item 9B — Other Information of its 2023 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on January 31, 2023, that there was no other information to disclose. The section contains no additional material events, regulatory matters, or company-specific updates beyond the single word “None.”
sections.Item 10: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 10 of Form 10-K regarding directors, executive officers, and corporate governance is not included in the annual report itself but will be provided in the company’s 2023 Proxy Statement. That proxy statement will be filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) in connection with the solicitation of proxies for Tesla’s 2023 Annual Meeting of Stockholders and is incorporated by reference into the 10-K, with filing due within 120 days after fiscal year-end.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 presents Tesla, Inc.’s consolidated financial statements and supplementary data for the years ended December 31, 2022 and 2021, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, redeemable noncontrolling interests and equity, cash flows, and notes. The independent registered public accounting firm, identified with PCAOB ID 238, audited both the financial statements and Tesla’s internal control over financial reporting under COSO’s Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013) and concluded they fairly present Tesla’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP. The section also notes a 2021 change in Tesla’s accounting for convertible debt.
sections.Item 11: Tesla Inc. states that the executive compensation information required by Item 11 of Form 10-K is not presented in the annual report itself but will be included in the company’s 2023 Proxy Statement. The section incorporates that proxy statement by reference, indicating that details on compensation for Tesla’s named executive officers are deferred to the proxy filing rather than disclosed here.
sections.Item 12: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 12 of Form 10-K, covering security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management and related stockholder matters, will be provided in its 2023 Proxy Statement and is incorporated into the annual report by reference. This section contains no ownership tables or specific holdings in the filing excerpt, only a cross-reference to the forthcoming proxy materials.
sections.Item 13: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 13 of Form 10-K, covering certain relationships and related transactions and director independence, is not presented in the annual report itself but will be included in the company’s 2023 Proxy Statement. The filing incorporates that proxy statement by reference, indicating that the relevant disclosures for Tesla’s related-party transactions and board independence are deferred to the proxy materials.
sections.Item 14: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 14 of Form 10-K, covering principal accountant fees and services, is not presented in the annual report itself but will be included in the company’s 2023 Proxy Statement and incorporated by reference. This section is a brief cross-reference within Part IV of the 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC, with no fee amounts, auditor names, or service details disclosed in the excerpt.
sections.Item 15: Tesla Inc.’s Item 15 states that no financial statement schedules were included because they were not applicable, not required in sufficient amounts, or the information was already covered in the consolidated financial statements and notes. The section then provides an index of exhibits filed with or incorporated by reference into the 10-K, including corporate governance documents such as the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation, Amended and Restated Bylaws, specimen common stock certificate, and multiple amendments to the Fifth Amended and Restated Investors’ Rights Agreement, including references to Toyota Motor Corporation and SEC forms 10-K, 8-K, S-1, and S-1/A.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains Tesla, Inc.’s signatures page for its 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, confirming the report was duly authorized and signed on January 30, 2023. Signatories include Elon Musk as Chief Executive Officer and Director, Zachary J. Kirkhorn as Chief Financial Officer, Vaibhav Taneja as Chief Accounting Officer, and directors Robyn Denholm, Ira Ehrenpreis, Joseph Gebbia, Hiromichi Mizuno, James Murdoch, Kimbal Musk, and Kathleen Wilson-Thompson.

TSLA_10K_2023Q4_2024-01-29_full.txt.document_context: Tesla Inc filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2023-12-31.
