The company designs, manufactures, sells and leases electric vehicles and energy generation/storage systems, operates two reportable segments (automotive and energy generation and storage), and in November 2023 began first deliveries of the Cybertruck, its full-size electric pickup truck.

sections.Preamble: Tesla, Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2023 with the SEC under Commission File Number 001-34756, identifying its principal executive offices at 1 Tesla Road, Austin, Texas 78725 and its IRS Employer Identification No. 91-2197729. The filing states that Tesla’s common stock trades on The Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol TSLA, that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, and that it has filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files for the preceding 12 months.
sections.Item 1: Tesla Inc. designs, manufactures, sells and leases high-performance fully electric vehicles and energy generation and storage systems, with a direct-to-customer model supported by showrooms, service centers, Mobile Service, body shops, Supercharger stations and Destination Chargers. The company says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy, and it emphasizes vertical integration, lower cost of ownership, and development of full self-driving technology for safety and user experience. Tesla reports two segments—automotive and energy generation and storage—and its automotive lineup includes Model 3, Model Y, Model S, Model X and the Cybertruck, which began first deliveries in November 2023.
sections.Item 1B: Tesla Inc. states that there were no unresolved staff comments from the SEC staff in Item 1B of its 2024 Form 10-K annual report filed on 2024-01-29. The section indicates that the company had no outstanding comment issues requiring resolution with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission.
sections.Item 1A: Tesla’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that delays in launching and ramping new products, features, and manufacturing processes could materially hurt business, financial condition, and operating results, citing prior supplier and automation issues during the Model X and Model 3 ramps. The company says its growth depends on scaling mass-market vehicle production and efficient, cost-effective manufacturing and supply chains across facilities in California, Nevada, Texas, China, Germany, and potential future sites such as Mexico, while hiring and training skilled employees. Tesla also warns of similar ramp risks for energy storage products, Solar Roof, new vehicle variants, and future AI-based features and services, with potential impacts on cost and profitability targets.
sections.Item 2: Tesla Inc. is headquartered in Austin, Texas, and operates a broad global property footprint across North America, Europe, and Asia for manufacturing and assembly, warehousing, engineering, retail and service locations, Supercharger sites, and administrative and sales offices. Its principal manufacturing facilities include owned Gigafactory Texas in Austin, Fremont Factory in California, Gigafactory Nevada in Sparks, Gigafactory Berlin-Brandenburg in Grunheide, Germany, and Gigafactory New York in Buffalo, plus leased Megafactory in Lathrop, California; Gigafactory Shanghai is noted as building-owned with 50-year land use rights treated as operating lease right-of-use assets.
sections.Item 1C: Tesla says cybersecurity risk management is integrated into its overall risk management systems and covers operational risk, intellectual property theft, fraud, extortion, employee/customer harm, and data privacy or security law violations. The company uses third-party assessments, internal IT audit, IT security, governance/risk/compliance reviews, penetration testing, a bug bounty program, employee training, and monitoring of data-protection laws to defend, detect, and respond to incidents through a four-stage process: preparation, detection and analysis, containment/eradication/recovery, and post-incident analysis. Incident response is overseen by Information Security, Product Security, Compliance, and Legal leaders, and Tesla states its Information Security Management System was certified to ISO/IEC 27001:2013 as of 2023.
sections.Item 3: Tesla Inc. states that its material pending legal proceedings are described in Note 15, Commitments and Contingencies, and discloses additional matters under Item 103 of Regulation S-K because they involve environmental regulations and aggregate civil penalties that could exceed $1 million. The excerpt highlights an investigation by district attorneys in certain California counties into Tesla’s waste segregation practices under Cal. Health & Saf. Code § 25100 et seq. and Cal. Civil Code § 1798.80, with Tesla implementing remedial measures such as training, audits, and site waste management program enhancements while settlement discussions continue and no material adverse impact is currently expected.
sections.Item 4: Tesla Inc. states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on January 29, 2024. The section contains no mine safety operations, citations, or related metrics, and simply transitions to Part II of the filing.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Tesla Inc.’s 2024 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure in the filing excerpt, indicating no additional selected financial data or related discussion is provided in this section. The section is marked “[RESERVED]32” and does not name any products, metrics, regulators, or other material business information.
sections.Item 7A: Tesla Inc. says its global operations create foreign currency risk because revenue, costs of revenue, and operating expenses are denominated in currencies other than the U.S. dollar, primarily the Chinese yuan and euro, and it generally does not hedge this exposure. The company notes that exchange-rate movements affect operating results and net income through remeasurement and settlement gains or losses on monetary assets and liabilities, and estimates that a 10% adverse move across currencies could have caused a $1.01 billion impact on net income before taxes at December 31, 2023, versus $473 million at December 31, 2022.
sections.Item 7: Tesla Inc. says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy through high-performance fully electric vehicles, solar energy generation systems, energy storage products, and related services, while increasingly emphasizing artificial intelligence, robotics, automation, and Full Self-Driving (FSD). In 2023, Tesla produced 1,845,985 consumer vehicles and delivered 1,808,581, deployed 14.72 GWh of energy storage and 223 MW of solar systems, and generated $96.77 billion in total revenues, up $15.31 billion year over year. The company also reported net income attributable to common stockholders of $15.00 billion, up $2.44 billion, and said it is focused on expanding manufacturing capacity, lowering costs, improving battery and vehicle technology, and growing its global service and charging infrastructure.
sections.Item 5: Tesla Inc.’s common stock has traded on The NASDAQ Global Select Market under the symbol “TSLA” since June 29, 2010, following its June 28, 2010 IPO priced at about $1.13 per share on a split-adjusted basis, reflecting the August 2020 five-for-one stock split and August 2022 three-for-one stock split. As of January 22, 2024, Tesla had 9,300 holders of record, has never paid cash dividends and does not expect to do so in the foreseeable future, and reported no unregistered sales of equity securities or issuer repurchases; its stock performance graph compares TSLA’s cumulative total return from 2019 to 2023 against the NASDAQ Composite Index and SIC 3711 Motor Vehicles and Passenger Car Bodies peer group.
sections.Item 9: Tesla Inc. states that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure matters for the 2024 Form 10-K. This Item 9 disclosure is marked “None,” indicating no reportable issues involving Tesla’s independent auditors or SEC-related accounting disputes.
sections.Item 9A: This section describes the independent auditor’s responsibilities and audit approach for Tesla Inc.’s consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting, emphasizing compliance with PCAOB standards, SEC rules, and U.S. federal securities laws. It explains that the audit was designed to provide reasonable assurance against material misstatement due to error or fraud, and included testing of financial statement evidence, accounting principles, management estimates, and the design and operating effectiveness of internal controls. It also defines internal control over financial reporting as a process intended to ensure reliable financial reporting and proper preparation of GAAP financial statements for external reporting purposes.
sections.Item 9B: Tesla reported that no directors or officers adopted, modified, or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangements during the fiscal quarter ended December 31, 2023, except for two new Rule 10b5-1 plans disclosed under Item 408(a) of Regulation S-K. Robyn Denholm, a Tesla director, adopted a plan on October 23, 2023 to potentially sell up to 281,116 shares of common stock tied to stock options expiring in August 2024, with the arrangement expiring August 16, 2024, and Andrew Baglino, Senior Vice President, Powertrain and Energy Engineering, adopted a plan on November 13, 2023 to potentially sell up to 115,500 shares, expiring December 31, 2024.
sections.Item 11: Tesla Inc. states that the executive compensation information required by Item 11 of Form 10-K will be provided in its 2024 Proxy Statement and is incorporated into the annual report by reference. This section contains no substantive compensation details in the filing excerpt itself, only a cross-reference to the forthcoming proxy disclosure.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Tesla, Inc.’s 2024 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, redeemable noncontrolling interests and equity, cash flows, and notes for the years ended December 31, 2023 and 2022. The Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm (PCAOB ID: 238) states that Tesla’s financial statements fairly present its financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP and that internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 31, 2023 under COSO 2013 criteria. It also notes a change in Tesla’s accounting for convertible debt in 2021.
sections.Item 13: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 13 of Form 10-K, covering certain relationships and related transactions and director independence, is not presented in the annual report itself but will be included in the company’s 2024 Proxy Statement. The filing incorporates that proxy statement information by reference, indicating that details on related-party matters and board independence are deferred to Tesla’s proxy materials.
sections.Item 14: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 14 of Form 10-K, covering principal accountant fees and services, is not presented in the annual report itself but will be included in the company’s 2024 Proxy Statement and incorporated by reference. This section is a brief cross-reference within Part IV of the 2024 annual report filed with the SEC, with no fee amounts, auditor names, or service details disclosed here.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Tesla Inc.’s 2024 Form 10-K states that the consolidated financial statements are included in Part II, Item 8, and that all financial statement schedules were omitted because they were not applicable, not present in sufficient amounts, or already covered by the financial statements and notes. The section then provides an Index to Exhibits listing key corporate governance and securities documents, including the Amended and Restated Certificate of Incorporation, Certificate of Amendment, Amended and Restated Bylaws, specimen common stock certificate, and multiple Fifth Amended and Restated Investors’ Rights Agreement amendments, with references to prior SEC filings such as 10-K, 8-K, S-1, and S-1/A, including an amendment involving Toyota Motor Corporation.
sections.Item 9C: Tesla Inc. states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in its 2024 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The section appears at the end of Item 9C and immediately transitions to Part III, with no foreign jurisdiction inspection issues disclosed.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains Tesla, Inc.’s formal signatures for its 2024 Form 10-K filed under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, certifying the report on behalf of the registrant. The filing is signed by Elon Musk as Chief Executive Officer and Principal Executive Officer, Vaibhav Taneja as Chief Financial Officer, Principal Financial Officer, and Principal Accounting Officer, and directors Robyn Denholm, Ira Ehrenpreis, Joseph Gebbia, James Murdoch, Kimbal Musk, JB Straubel, and Kathleen Wilson-Thompson, all dated January 26, 2024.
sections.Item 10: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 10 of Form 10-K, covering directors, executive officers, and corporate governance, will be provided in its 2024 Proxy Statement rather than in the annual report itself. The Proxy Statement will be filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission in connection with the solicitation of proxies for Tesla’s 2024 Annual Meeting of Stockholders and is incorporated by reference into the 10-K, with filing due within 120 days after fiscal year-end.
sections.Item 12: Tesla Inc. states that the information required by Item 12 of Form 10-K, covering security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management and related stockholder matters, is not presented in the annual report itself. Instead, Tesla incorporates that information by reference to its 2024 Proxy Statement, which will contain the relevant ownership and stockholder details.

sections.Preamble: Tesla, Inc. filed a Form 10-K annual report with the SEC for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, under Commission File Number 001-34756, identifying its principal executive offices at 1 Tesla Road, Austin, Texas 78725 and its telephone number as (512) 516-8177. The filing states that Tesla’s common stock trades on The Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol TSLA, that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer, and that it had filed all required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files during the preceding 12 months.
sections.Item 1A: Tesla says its growth depends on successfully launching and ramping new vehicles, energy storage products, Solar Roof, and AI-based features while controlling manufacturing costs across facilities in California, Nevada, Texas, China, Germany, and potential future sites such as Mexico. The company warns that supplier issues, bottlenecks, design and production challenges, and the need to hire and train skilled employees could delay product ramps, prevent it from meeting design tolerances, output rates, and profitability targets, and materially harm business, financial condition, and operating results.

sections.Item 2: Tesla Inc. is headquartered in Austin, Texas and operates a broad global real estate footprint across North America, Europe, and Asia, including manufacturing and assembly plants, warehousing, engineering, retail and service locations, Supercharger sites, and administrative and sales offices supporting both reporting segments. Its principal manufacturing facilities include owned Gigafactory Texas, Fremont Factory, Gigafactory Nevada, and Gigafactory Berlin-Brandenburg, plus Gigafactory Shanghai and Megafactory Shanghai in China where Tesla owns the building and 50-year land use rights, and leased Gigafactory New York and Megafactory Lathrop.
sections.Item 1B: Tesla Inc. states that there were no unresolved staff comments from the SEC staff in Item 1B of its 2025 Form 10-K annual report. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding comment issues requiring further resolution with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) as of the filing date.
sections.Item 1: Tesla Inc. designs, manufactures, sells and leases high-performance fully electric vehicles and energy generation and storage systems, with direct sales supported by a global network of showrooms, service centers, Mobile Service, body shops, Supercharger stations and Destination Chargers. The company says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy, while emphasizing performance, safety, lower ownership costs, vertical integration and ongoing development of full self-driving technology. Tesla operates two reportable segments—automotive and energy generation and storage—and its automotive lineup includes the Model 3, Model Y, Model S, Model X and Cybertruck, alongside automotive regulatory credits, used vehicles, paid Supercharging, insurance services and retail merchandise sales.
sections.Item 1C: Tesla says cybersecurity risk management is integrated into its overall risk management systems and addresses threats such as operational disruption, intellectual property theft, fraud, extortion, employee/customer harm, and violations of data privacy or security laws. The company uses third-party assessments, internal IT Audit, IT security, governance/risk/compliance reviews, penetration testing, a bug bounty program, employee training, and monitoring of data-protection laws, while its incident response process covers preparation, detection and analysis, containment/eradication/recovery, and post-incident analysis overseen by Information Security, Product Security, Compliance, and Legal teams. Tesla also conducts tabletop exercises, engages external auditors and consultants, and states that its Information Security Management System was certified in 2024 to ISO/IEC 27001:2013.
sections.Item 4: Tesla Inc. states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on January 30, 2025. The section contains no mine safety metrics, incidents, citations, or regulatory disclosures.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Tesla Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive disclosure in the filing excerpt. The section appears only as a placeholder, with no reported financial metrics, business updates, risk factors, or named counterparties/regulators beyond Tesla Inc.
sections.Item 9A: This section describes the independent auditor’s PCAOB-standard audit of Tesla’s consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting, emphasizing reasonable assurance against material misstatement from error or fraud and evaluation of design and operating effectiveness of controls. It references the SEC, PCAOB, U.S. federal securities laws, and GAAP, and explains that internal control over financial reporting is intended to ensure reliable financial reporting, accurate records of transactions and asset dispositions, and proper preparation of external financial statements.
sections.Item 5: Tesla, Inc. states that its common stock has traded on The NASDAQ Global Select Market under the symbol TSLA since June 29, 2010, with the initial public offering priced at approximately $1.13 per share on June 28, 2010, adjusted for the August 2020 five-for-one and August 2022 three-for-one stock splits. As of January 22, 2025, Tesla had 9,512 record holders of common stock, has never paid cash dividends and does not expect to do so, and presents a stock performance graph comparing TSLA’s cumulative return from January 1, 2020 through December 31, 2024 against the NASDAQ Composite Index and SIC code 3711 motor vehicle peers.
sections.Item 9: Tesla Inc. states that there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure matters for the 2025 Form 10-K. The Item 9 section reports “None,” indicating no issues to disclose regarding auditor changes or accounting disputes.
sections.Item 7A: Tesla Inc. says its global operations expose it to foreign currency risk because revenue, cost of revenue, and operating expenses are denominated in currencies other than the U.S. dollar, primarily the Chinese yuan and euro, and it generally does not hedge this exposure. The company notes that it is typically a net receiver of non-U.S. dollar currencies and that exchange-rate movements affect U.S.-dollar reported results, with remeasurement and settlement gains or losses on intercompany and cash balances contributing to net income volatility. Tesla estimates that a 10% adverse move in all currencies could have affected pre-tax income by $1.15 billion at December 31, 2024 and $1.01 billion at December 31, 2023, assuming no foreign currency hedging.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 presents Tesla, Inc.’s consolidated financial statements and supplementary data for the year ended December 31, 2024, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, redeemable noncontrolling interests and equity, cash flows, and notes. The independent registered public accounting firm, with PCAOB ID 238, audited Tesla’s 2024 and 2023 balance sheets and three years of results, concluded the statements fairly present the company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows under U.S. GAAP, and found internal control over financial reporting effective as of December 31, 2024. The report also notes a 2024 change in Tesla’s accounting for digital assets, as discussed in Note 2.
sections.Item 9B: Tesla Inc. reported that no directors or officers adopted, modified, or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement during the fiscal quarter ended December 31, 2024, except for director Ira Ehrenpreis. On December 6, 2024, Ehrenpreis adopted a Rule 10b5-1 plan covering the potential exercise of options to purchase 761,961 shares of Tesla common stock and subsequent sales to cover tax withholding and generate $15 million in aggregate net proceeds after commissions and fees, with an expiration date of June 3, 2025.
sections.Item 7: Tesla Inc. says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy through high-performance electric vehicles, solar energy systems, and energy storage products, while increasingly emphasizing AI, robotics, automation, and services such as maintenance, charging, insurance, and financing. sections.Item 9C: Tesla Inc. states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 2025 Form 10-K. The section contains no substantive disclosure beyond this statement and marks the transition to Part III of the annual report.

sections.Item 9B: Tesla disclosed that no directors or officers adopted, modified, or terminated Rule 10b5-1 or non-Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangements in the fiscal quarter ended December 31, 2025, except for CFO Vaibhav Taneja’s plan to sell up to 84,000 shares through January 31, 2027 and director Kathleen Wilson-Thompson’s plan to sell up to 120,948 shares through May 8, 2026. sections.Preamble: Tesla, Inc. filed its Form 10-Q quarterly report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the period ended March 31, 2022, under Commission File Number 001-34756. The preamble identifies Tesla as a Delaware corporation headquartered at 1 Tesla Road, Austin, Texas 78725, with common stock trading on The Nasdaq Global Select Market under the symbol TSLA, and states that the company is a large accelerated filer and not a shell company. As of April 19, 2022, Tesla reported 1,036,009,925 shares of common stock outstanding and confirmed compliance with Exchange Act reporting and electronic filing requirements.
sections.Item 4: Tesla Inc. reports that management, with participation from Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, evaluated disclosure controls and procedures under Rule 13a-15 of the Exchange Act and concluded they were effective as of March 31, 2022 at a reasonable assurance level for timely recording, processing, summarizing, and reporting of required SEC disclosures. The company also states there was no material change in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended March 31, 2022, and that Item 4 Mine Safety Disclosures are not applicable.
sections.Item 1: Tesla, Inc.’s unaudited consolidated balance sheets for March 31, 2022 versus December 31, 2021 show total assets rising to $66.038 billion from $62.131 billion, driven by higher inventory, accounts receivable, property, plant and equipment, and other non-current assets, while cash and cash equivalents remained roughly flat at $17.505 billion. Total liabilities were essentially unchanged at $30.632 billion, with current liabilities increasing to $21.455 billion and debt and finance leases, net of current portion, declining to $3.153 billion; stockholders’ equity increased to $34.085 billion, reflecting additional paid-in capital of $30.485 billion and retained earnings of $3.649 billion.
sections.Item 2: Tesla Inc. says its mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy through high-performance electric vehicles, solar energy generation systems, and energy storage products, while also expanding into artificial intelligence, robotics, and automation. In the first quarter of 2022, Tesla produced 305,407 vehicles and delivered 310,048, deployed 846 MWh of energy storage and 48 MW of solar systems, and generated $18.76 billion in revenue, up $8.37 billion year over year. The company reported $3.32 billion in net income attributable to common stockholders, $18.01 billion in cash and marketable securities, $4.00 billion in operating cash flow, and $1.77 billion in capital expenditures amid supply chain challenges and factory shutdowns.
sections.Item 5: Item 5, Market for Registrant’s Common Equity, contains no additional other information for Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) in this Form 10-Q filed on April 25, 2022. The section states simply “OTHER INFORMATION” and “None,” indicating no disclosures, updates, or market-related matters were reported here.
sections.Item 1A: Tesla’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that the global COVID-19 pandemic and related macroeconomic conditions have disrupted manufacturing, deliveries, and supply chains, including temporary shutdowns of Tesla’s worldwide facilities in 2020 and Gigafactory Shanghai in early 2022. The company warns that port congestion, supplier shutdowns, semiconductor shortages, labor absenteeism, and higher labor costs could continue to impair production, increase expenses, and affect its ability to grow.
sections.Item 6: Tesla, Inc.’s Item 6 in this Form 10-Q is the exhibits index and signature section, listing filed certifications and Inline XBRL materials rather than substantive operating results. Key exhibits include Rule 13a-14(a)/15(d)-14(a) certifications for the Principal Executive Officer and Principal Financial Officer (31.1, 31.2), Section 1350 certifications (32.1), and XBRL documents (101.INS, 101.SCH, 101.CAL, 101.DEF, 101.LAB, 101.PRE, 104). The report is signed on behalf of Tesla by Zachary J. Kirkhorn, Chief Financial Officer and Principal Financial Officer, dated April 22, 2022.
sections.Item 3: Tesla Inc. describes market risk exposures in its 10-Q, emphasizing foreign currency risk from global operations and localized debt denominated mainly in Chinese yuan, euro, pound sterling, and Norwegian krone, with the company generally unhedged and a net receiver of non-U.S. currencies. It estimates that a hypothetical 10% adverse FX move could have affected pre-tax income by a $63 million gain or loss at March 31, 2022 versus $277 million at December 31, 2021, and notes floating-rate debt interest rate risk managed in some cases with derivatives, with a 10% rate change having an immaterial impact on interest expense. The section also states there were no defaults upon senior securities.

The section traces AT&T’s history from its 1984 spin-off from AT&T Corp. under an antitrust consent decree, through major expansion via mergers with Pacific Telesis Group and Ameritech Corporation, the 2005 merger with AT&T Corp., and the 2006 acquisition of BellSouth Corporation, including BellSouth’s 40% economic interest in AT&T Mobility.
sections.Item 7: AT&T Inc. says it actively protects a significant global portfolio of patents, trademarks and service marks in the United States and abroad, and licenses both its own intellectual property and third-party rights, especially as it transitions from a switch-based network to an IP, software-based network. The company notes that infringement claims could require damages, licenses, or changes to products and network functions, but it does not expect these matters or the expiration of IP rights to have a material adverse effect on results of operations. AT&T also states that no customer accounted for 10% or more of consolidated revenues in 2025, 2024 or 2023, and highlights intensifying competition in communications and digital services, particularly in wireless under FCC rules, against two national wireless providers plus regional carriers and resellers.
sections.Item 1B: AT&T Inc. (NYSE: T) states that Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, is not applicable in its 2026 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on February 9, 2026. The section indicates there were no outstanding unresolved comments from the SEC staff requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 1A: AT&T Inc. says adverse U.S. securities markets, higher interest rates, inflation, and rising medical and prescription drug costs could materially increase benefit plan expenses and future funding requirements, especially for its defined benefit pension and postretirement plans. The company notes that lower investment returns, higher benefit obligations, and FASB-driven mark-to-market changes in funded status can negatively affect operating results, while inflationary pressures on devices, network components, labor, and distribution costs may hurt network construction and financial performance. AT&T also highlights dependence on suppliers for telecommunications and technology products and equipment, including handsets, wireless data cards, wireless computing devices, customer premises equipment, mobile hotspots, and network equipment.
sections.Item 3: AT&T Inc. states that it and its subsidiaries are involved in numerous lawsuits, regulatory proceedings, and other matters arising in the ordinary course of business. As of the 2026-02-09 Form 10-K, AT&T says none of the pending legal proceedings are believed to require disclosure as material legal proceedings under Item 3.
sections.Item 1C: AT&T Inc. describes cybersecurity governance centered on Board and Audit Committee oversight of privacy, data security, risk exposures, and incident escalation, with regular reporting from the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), Chief Technology Officer (CTO), and AT&T Legal. The company’s Chief Security Office (CSO), led by the CISO, manages network and information security across multiple countries by setting security policies and standards, monitoring threats and corrective actions, promoting compliance, and overseeing legal, regulatory, and contractual security requirements.
sections.Item 2: At December 31, 2025, AT&T Inc.’s property, plant and equipment was concentrated in outside plant (29%), other equipment including wireless network equipment on towers, furniture, office equipment and vehicles (26%), central office equipment (25%), land, buildings and wireless communications towers (12%), and other miscellaneous property (8%). In the Communications segment, substantially all central office equipment is installed in buildings and on land AT&T owns, while many garages, administrative and business offices, wireless towers, telephone centers and retail stores are leased; tower sites may be either owned or leased.
sections.Item 4: Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, states “Not applicable” for AT&T Inc., indicating the company has no reportable mine safety disclosures under SEC requirements. The excerpt also lists AT&T’s executive officers as of February 1, 2026, including John T. Stankey (Chairman, CEO and President), Pascal Desroches (CFO), Jeffery S. McElfresh (COO), David R. McAtee II (General Counsel), and Darcie (Henry) Cakaric (CHRO), with most having long tenures at AT&T or its subsidiaries.
sections.Item 9: AT&T Inc. reports that during its two most recent fiscal years there was no change in the independent accountant serving as principal auditor of its financial statements. The company also states that the independent accountant did not express reliance on other independent accountants in its reports during that period.
sections.Item 5: AT&T Inc.’s common stock is listed on the New York Stock Exchange and NYSE Texas under ticker “T,” with 671,341 stockholders of record at December 31, 2025, 712,700 at December 31, 2024, and 668,838 as of January 28, 2026. The company declared quarterly common dividends totaling $1.11 per share in both 2025 and 2024, and its stock performance is compared against the S&P 500 Index and S&P 500 Communication Services Index. In the fourth quarter of 2025, AT&T repurchased 72,229,635 shares at an average price of $25.35 per share, with 71,996,824 shares bought under the 2024 Authorization and approximately $5,731 million remaining under that program; the Board also approved an additional $10,000 million repurchase authorization in January 2026.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of AT&T Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K is a reserved section and contains no substantive discussion, only the company name and a standard financial statement convention noting that amounts are presented in dollars in millions except per share amounts. The excerpt provides no additional operating, regulatory, product, or metric disclosures beyond identifying AT&T Inc. and the filing context.
sections.Item 9A: AT&T Inc. reports that its disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of December 31, 2025, based on evaluations by the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, and that no changes in internal control over financial reporting occurred during the most recent fiscal quarter that would materially affect those controls. Management states that AT&T’s internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 31, 2025 under the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013), and notes that Ernst & Young LLP issued the attestation report on the company’s internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 8: AT&T Inc.’s Item 8 financial statements show 2025 total operating revenues of $125.648 billion, up from $122.336 billion in 2024, driven by service revenue of $101.158 billion and equipment revenue of $24.490 billion, with operating income rising to $24.162 billion and net income attributable to AT&T increasing to $21.953 billion. The section also reports 2025 basic and diluted earnings per share of $3.04, compares operating expenses including depreciation and amortization of $20.886 billion and asset impairments/restructuring of $838 million, and presents comprehensive income items such as foreign currency translation adjustments and securities gains/losses.
sections.Item 9C: AT&T Inc. states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 10-K filed on 2026-02-09. The section appears at the end of the filing’s Item 9C and immediately precedes Part III, with no foreign jurisdiction inspection issues, regulators, products, or metrics disclosed.
sections.Item 7A: AT&T Inc. says its primary market risks are changes in interest rates and foreign currency exchange rates, which affect cost of capital, and it manages these risks through debt structure management and derivatives such as interest rate swaps, interest rate locks, foreign exchange contracts, and cross-currency swaps under documented policies. The company states it does not use derivatives for trading or speculation, and notes that its pension and postretirement obligations are highly sensitive to the weighted-average discount rate, which has been volatile and generally lower in recent years, increasing obligations when rates fall. Most of AT&T’s financial instruments are medium- and long-term fixed-rate notes and debentures, and it uses term loans, floating-rate notes, and swaps to control interest expense and monitor interest rate risk limits.
sections.Item 9B: AT&T Inc. states that there was no information required to be disclosed on Form 8-K during the fourth quarter of 2025 that had not already been reported. The company also reports that, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025, none of its directors or officers adopted or terminated any Rule 10b5-1 trading plan or other non-Rule 10b5-1 securities trading arrangement under Rule 16a-1(f), Rule 10b5-1(c), or Item 408 of Regulation S-K.
sections.Item 10: AT&T Inc. states that information on executive officers is provided in Part I and that director, Section 16(a) delinquency, and audit committee disclosures are incorporated by reference from its 2026 definitive proxy statement under Regulation S-K and General Instruction G(3). The company says it has a separately designated standing audit committee under Section 3(a)(58)(A) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, composed of Messrs. Luczo and McCallister and Mses. Mayer and Taylor, and that it has adopted a “Code of Ethics” covering its principal executive, financial, and accounting officers.
sections.Item 13: AT&T Inc. states that the disclosure required by Item 404 of Regulation S-K on certain relationships and related transactions is provided in its Proxy Statement under the heading “Related Person Transactions,” incorporated by reference under General Instruction G(3). The company also incorporates by reference the Item 407(a) director independence disclosure from the Proxy Statement under the heading “Director Independence,” indicating that these governance matters are addressed outside the 10-K text.
sections.Item 14: AT&T Inc. states that the information required by Item 14, “Principal Accountant Fees and Services,” is not repeated in the Form 10-K and is instead incorporated by reference from the company’s Proxy Statement under General Instruction G(3). This section is part of Part IV of the 2026 annual report filed with the SEC and directs readers to the proxy disclosure for details on fees and services provided by the principal accountant.
sections.Item 11: AT&T Inc. states that the executive compensation disclosure required by Item 11 is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement under the headings “Director Compensation,” “2025 Director Compensation Table,” “CEO Pay Ratio,” “Pay Versus Performance,” and “Compensation Discussion and Analysis,” including “Potential Payments upon Change in Control.” The filing also incorporates the Item 407(e)(5) Regulation S-K “Compensation Committee Report,” noting it is furnished in the 2026 Form 10-K and not deemed incorporated by reference into Securities Act or Exchange Act filings.
sections.Item 12: AT&T Inc. states that the Item 12 disclosure on security ownership of certain beneficial owners and management is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement under the headings “Equity Compensation Plan Information” and “Common Stock Ownership,” satisfying the requirements of Item 201(d) and Item 403 of Regulation S-K. The section does not provide ownership tables or specific holdings in the 10-K excerpt itself, instead directing readers to the proxy materials for details on management and stockholder equity compensation and common stock ownership.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of AT&T Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K lists the report of the independent registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID: 42), the consolidated financial statements and notes, and Financial Statement Schedule II—Valuation and Qualifying Accounts, with other schedules omitted as not required or already covered in the statements and notes. It also catalogs incorporated SEC exhibits, including AT&T Services, Inc.’s September 29, 2024 Securities Purchase Agreement involving DIRECTV Entertainment Holdings LLC and TPG Partners IX, L.P., a May 21, 2025 Purchase Agreement involving Lumen Technologies, Inc. and AT&T DW Holdings, Inc., and AT&T’s restated certificate of incorporation, bylaws, and preferred stock designation certificates.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 of AT&T Inc.’s 2026 Form 10-K contains Schedule II valuation and qualifying accounts, showing allowance for credit losses rising from $553 million at year-end 2024 to $650 million at year-end 2025, with 2025 additions of $2.271 billion charged to costs and expenses and $2.174 billion of write-offs/deductions. It also shows the allowance for deferred tax assets declining from $4.338 billion to $3.978 billion in 2025, and includes the report’s signature page signed February 9, 2026 by Pascal Desroches, Senior Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, with John T. Stankey listed as Chairman, CEO and President and Sabrina Sanders as Chief Accounting Officer and Controller.

UNH_10K_2022Q4_2023-02-24_full.txt.document_context: UnitedHealth Group Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022. The company describes itself as a health care and well-being company with two complementary business platforms, Optum and UnitedHealthcare, and four reportable segments: Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare.

sections.Item 1B: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that there are no unresolved staff comments in Item 1B of its 2023 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 24, 2023. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding comments from the Securities and Exchange Commission staff requiring further resolution.
sections.Item 2: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that it owns and leases real properties in the United States and other countries to support its business operations. These facilities are used by its reportable segments for their respective business purposes, and the company believes its current properties are suitable and adequate for anticipated future needs.
sections.Preamble: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE: UNH) filed this Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022, under Commission file number 1-10864, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered at UnitedHealth Group Center, 9900 Bren Road East, Minnetonka, Minnesota 55343. The preamble lists the company’s common stock as registered under Section 12(b) on the New York Stock Exchange, notes no Section 12(g) securities, and indicates UNH is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and Interactive Data Files with the SEC.
sections.Item 1: UnitedHealth Group Inc. describes itself as a health care and well-being company whose mission is to help people live healthier lives and improve the health system through its two complementary platforms, Optum and UnitedHealthcare. Optum combines clinical expertise, technology, and data across Optum Health, Optum Insight, and Optum Rx to serve payers, providers, employers, governments, life sciences companies, and consumers, while UnitedHealthcare provides health benefits through Employer & Individual, Medicare & Retirement, and Community & State businesses. The company reports four segments—Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare—and emphasizes improved access, affordability, outcomes, and experiences through data analytics, pharmacy care services, health care operations, population health, and care delivery.
sections.Item 1A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) opens Item 1A with PSLRA cautionary language explaining that its SEC filings, earnings materials, and executive statements contain forward-looking statements identified by terms like “believe,” “expect,” and “outlook,” which are subject to risks, uncertainties, and assumptions and are not guarantees of future performance. The section emphasizes that actual results may differ materially and highlights a core business risk: failure to accurately estimate, price, and manage medical costs or design benefits effectively could reduce the profitability of risk-based products and services and adversely affect results of operations, financial position, and cash flow.
sections.Item 4: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2023 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 24, 2023. The section contains no mine safety operations, citations, violations, or related metrics, indicating the company has no reportable mining activities under this disclosure item.
sections.Item 3: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the information required for Item 3, Legal Proceedings, is incorporated by reference to Note 12 of the Consolidated Financial Statements in Part II, Item 8, specifically the sections titled “Legal Matters” and “Governmental Investigations, Audits and Reviews.” This section does not provide substantive litigation details itself, but directs readers to the company’s disclosures on legal matters and regulatory investigations, audits, and reviews.
sections.Item 9: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure matters in Item 9 of its 2023 Form 10-K. The section states “None,” indicating no issues to disclose regarding the company’s independent auditors or financial reporting.
sections.Item 7A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. says its primary market risks are interest rate exposure affecting investment income, interest expense, and the fair value of fixed-rate investments and debt, plus foreign currency risk tied mainly to the Brazilian real and Chilean peso. As of December 31, 2022, UNH had $31 billion of floating-rate financial assets, $16 billion of variable-rate financial liabilities, $38 billion of fixed-rate debt securities, and $43 billion of non-swapped fixed-rate term debt, and it manages these risks through diversification, maturity matching, and interest rate swap contracts. The filing includes sensitivity tables showing that a 1% or 2% parallel shift in market rates could materially change annual investment income and interest expense and the fair value of financial assets and liabilities.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 presents UnitedHealth Group Incorporated and Subsidiaries’ audited consolidated financial statements for fiscal years ended December 31, 2022 and 2021, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, changes in equity, cash flows, and notes covering investments, fair value, goodwill and other intangible assets, medical costs payable, debt, income taxes, share-based compensation, contingencies, business combinations, and segment information. The report of independent registered public accounting firm (PCAOB ID No. 34) states that the financial statements fairly present the company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP, and that internal control over financial reporting was audited under PCAOB standards and received an unqualified opinion.
sections.Item 9A: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) states that its disclosure controls and procedures, as defined in Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), were designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and communication of information required in SEC filings, and were evaluated by management with the participation of the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer as of December 31, 2022. Based on that evaluation, the CEO and CFO concluded the controls were effective at the reasonable assurance level, and the company reported no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended December 31, 2022 that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, internal control over financial reporting.
sections.Item 5: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (NYSE: UNH) states that its common stock traded on the New York Stock Exchange and had 10,260 holders of record as of January 31, 2023. The Board of Directors raised the quarterly cash dividend in June 2022 to an annual rate of $6.60 per share from $5.80, and the company repurchased 1.9 million shares in the fourth quarter of 2022 at an average price of $528.87 per share under a 100 million-share authorization with 31.1 million shares remaining available at December 31, 2022. The section also includes a five-year performance graph showing UnitedHealth’s cumulative total return of $258.65 on a $100 investment from December 31, 2017 through December 31, 2022, outperforming the S&P Health Care Index, Dow Jones US Industrial Average, and S&P 500 comparisons.
sections.Item 9C: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 2023 Form 10-K. The section serves as a brief transition into Part III and contains no additional substantive disclosure, metrics, or regulatory issues.
sections.Item 11: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the executive compensation disclosure required by Items 402 and 407(e)(4) and (e)(5) of Regulation S-K is not presented in this Form 10-K section but will instead appear in the company’s definitive proxy statement for its 2023 Annual Meeting of Shareholders. The incorporated proxy statement sections include “Executive Compensation,” “Director Compensation,” “Corporate Governance - Risk Oversight,” and “Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation,” with the referenced information incorporated by reference into the annual report.
sections.Item 10: Item 10 identifies UnitedHealth Group Inc.’s directors as of February 24, 2023, including Stephen Hemsley (Chair), Andrew Witty (Chief Executive Officer), Michele Hooper (Lead Independent Director), Timothy Flynn, F. William McNabb III, Paul Garcia, Valerie Montgomery Rice, M.D., Kristen Gil, and John Noseworthy, M.D., with their principal occupations noted. It also states that executive officer information is provided in Part I, Item 1, and that UnitedHealth Group has adopted a Code of Conduct: Our Principles of Ethics and Integrity for senior financial officers, posted on its website, with additional Regulation S-K governance disclosures to be included in the 2023 proxy statement.
sections.Item 14: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the principal accountant fee disclosures required by Item 9(e) of Schedule 14A are not repeated in this Form 10-K Item 14, but are incorporated by reference from the company’s definitive proxy statement for its 2023 Annual Meeting of Shareholders. The referenced disclosure will appear under “Disclosure of Fees Paid to Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm,” and the section is part of Part IV of the annual report.
sections.Item 13: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the information required by Items 404 and 407(a) of Regulation S-K regarding certain relationships and related transactions and director independence is not presented in the 10-K section itself. Instead, the company incorporates that disclosure by reference to its definitive proxy statement for the 2023 Annual Meeting of Shareholders, where the information will appear under “Certain Relationships and Transactions” and “Corporate Governance.”
sections.Item 12: UnitedHealth Group Inc. reports that as of December 31, 2022, its equity compensation plans had 23 million securities outstanding under shareholder-approved plans with a weighted-average exercise price of $279 and 77 million shares remaining available for future issuance. The approved plans are the UnitedHealth Group Incorporated 2020 Stock Incentive Plan, as amended, and the UnitedHealth Group 1993 Employee Stock Purchase Plan, as amended; no awards are authorized under plans not approved by shareholders, though 459,000 assumed acquisition-related stock options remain outstanding at a $358 weighted-average exercise price with about 4 years remaining. The filing also notes that Item 403 beneficial ownership information will be provided in the definitive proxy statement for the 2023 Annual Meeting of Shareholders and incorporated by reference.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains no Form 10-K summary and consists primarily of the signatures page for UnitedHealth Group Incorporated’s 2023 Annual Report filed with the SEC on February 24, 2023. It records execution under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 by CEO Andrew Witty, CFO John Rex, Chief Accounting Officer Thomas Roos, and directors including Timothy Flynn, Paul Garcia, Kristen Gil, Stephen Hemsley, Michele Hooper, F. William McNabb III, Valerie Montgomery Rice, and John Noseworthy, with Kuai Leong signing as attorney-in-fact.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of UnitedHealth Group Inc.’s 2023 Form 10-K identifies the audited financial statements included in Item 8, including the Reports of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm, Consolidated Balance Sheets, Statements of Operations, Comprehensive Income, Changes in Equity, Cash Flows, and Notes for 2022, 2021, and 2020. It also includes Schedule I, Condensed Financial Information of Registrant (Parent Company Only), and notes that other SEC-required schedules are omitted because they are inapplicable or covered in the consolidated financial statements. The exhibit index lists incorporated governance and debt documents under Item 601 of Regulation S-K, including the Certificate of Incorporation, Amended and Restated Bylaws, and senior indenture materials for UnitedHealth Group Incorporated, filed under SEC File No. 1-10864.

sections.Item 9A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that its disclosure controls and procedures, as defined under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), are designed to ensure SEC-required information is recorded, processed, summarized, and communicated timely to management, including the CEO and CFO. The section also reiterates management’s responsibility for internal control over financial reporting under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(f) and 15d-15(f) to support reliable GAAP financial reporting for UnitedHealth Group Incorporated and Subsidiaries.

UNH_10K_2025-02-27_full.txt.document_context: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024. The excerpt says the company operates through two main businesses, Optum and UnitedHealthcare, and four reportable segments: Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare.

sections.Item 1: UnitedHealth Group Inc. is a health care and well-being company whose mission is to help people live healthier lives and improve the health system through its two complementary businesses, Optum and UnitedHealthcare. Optum uses clinical expertise, technology, data, pharmacy care services, population health, and health financial services across Optum Health, Optum Insight, and Optum Rx to serve patients, payers, providers, employers, governments, and life sciences companies, while UnitedHealthcare provides health benefits through Employer & Individual, Medicare & Retirement, and Community & State. The company reports four segments—Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare—and emphasizes improving access, affordability, outcomes, and consumer and provider experience.
sections.Preamble: UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (NYSE: UNH) filed this Form 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024, under Commission file number 1-10864, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation with principal executive offices in Eden Prairie, Minnesota and Washington, DC. The preamble lists the company’s Common Stock, $.01 par value, as registered under Section 12(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, notes no Section 12(g) securities, and indicates UNH is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required SEC reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1B: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff as of the 2025-02-27 Form 10-K filing. The section is effectively a placeholder indicating no outstanding regulatory issues or comment letters requiring disclosure.
sections.Item 2: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that it owns and leases real properties in the United States and other countries to support its business operations across its reportable segments. The company says these facilities are used for their respective business purposes and believes the current properties are suitable and adequate for anticipated future needs.
sections.Item 1A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) warns that its 2025 Form 10-K contains PSLRA forward-looking statements identified by terms such as “believe,” “expect,” “estimate,” and “outlook,” and that actual results may differ materially due to risks, uncertainties, and inaccurate assumptions. The Item 1A Risk Factors section emphasizes that these statements are not guarantees, are only current as of the filing date, and need not be updated except as required by law. It also introduces a key business risk: failure to estimate, price, and manage medical costs or design benefits effectively could reduce the profitability of UnitedHealth’s risk-based products and services and materially harm results of operations and financial position.
sections.Item 1C: UnitedHealth Group says its cybersecurity and data protection program uses a continuously evolving framework to identify, assess, and mitigate risk, with oversight by the Board’s Audit and Finance Committee and management leadership from the Chief Digital and Technology Officer and Chief Security Officer. The company’s incident management and response program monitors systems for vulnerabilities, contains and remediates incidents, and reports significant threats to the Audit and Finance Committee and, when appropriate, government agencies and other authorities. UnitedHealth also requires third-party partners and contractors to meet data privacy and information security requirements, engages suppliers and internal development teams on remediation and continuity controls, and audits its infrastructure through internal and external auditors while maintaining industry-recognized certifications.
sections.Item 3: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the information required for Item 3, Legal Proceedings, is incorporated by reference to Note 12 of the Consolidated Financial Statements in Part II, Item 8, specifically the sections titled “Legal Matters” and “Government Investigations, Audits and Reviews.” This section does not provide substantive litigation details itself, but directs readers to the company’s disclosures regarding legal matters and government investigations, audits, and reviews.
sections.Item 4: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 27, 2025. The section contains no mine safety operations, incidents, citations, or regulatory disclosures.
sections.Item 9: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its independent accountants on accounting or financial disclosure for the 2025 Form 10-K filed February 27, 2025. Item 9 states that there were no accounting disputes, auditor changes, or related disclosure issues during the period.
sections.Item 5: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (NYSE: UNH) states that its common stock traded on the New York Stock Exchange and had 9,323 holders of record as of January 31, 2025. In June 2024, the Board of Directors raised the quarterly cash dividend to an annual rate of $8.40 per share from $7.52, and the company repurchased 9.1 million shares in fourth quarter 2024 at an average price of $537.14 per share under its long-running share repurchase program, which was amended in June 2024 to authorize up to 35 million additional shares with no expiration date.
sections.Item 7A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. says its primary market risk is exposure to interest-rate changes affecting investment income, interest expense, and the fair value of fixed-rate investments and debt. As of December 31, 2024, it had $33 billion of floating-rate financial assets, $27 billion of floating-rate financial liabilities, $46 billion of fixed-rate debt securities, and $49 billion of non-swapped fixed-rate term debt, and it manages this risk through diversification, maturity matching, and interest rate swap contracts. The company’s sensitivity analysis shows that a 1% or 2% parallel shift in rates could change annual investment income and interest expense and materially move the fair value of financial assets and liabilities, with unrealized gains and losses on available-for-sale debt securities reported in comprehensive income.
sections.Item 9C: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 27, 2025. The section serves as a brief Part III transition and contains no additional foreign jurisdiction, inspection, or regulatory disclosure.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 presents UnitedHealth Group Incorporated’s audited consolidated financial statements and supplementary data for the years ended December 31, 2024 and 2023, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, changes in equity, cash flows, and notes on investments, fair value, goodwill and intangible assets, medical costs payable, debt, income taxes, share-based compensation, contingencies, dispositions, and segment information. The Report of Independent Registered Public Accounting Firm (PCAOB ID No. 34) states that the financial statements fairly present the Company’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP, and that internal control over financial reporting was audited under PCAOB standards and received an unqualified opinion as of December 31, 2024.
sections.Item 9A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) states that its disclosure controls and procedures, as defined in Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e), are designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and communication of information required in SEC reports, and management evaluated them as of December 31, 2024 under the supervision of the CEO and CFO. Based on that evaluation, the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer concluded the controls were effective at the reasonable assurance level, and the company reported no changes in internal control over financial reporting during the quarter ended December 31, 2024 that materially affected, or were reasonably likely to materially affect, those controls.
sections.Item 11: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the executive compensation disclosure required by Items 402 and 407(e)(4) and (e)(5) of Regulation S-K is not presented in this Form 10-K section but will instead appear in the company’s definitive proxy statement for its 2025 Annual Meeting of Shareholders. The filing incorporates by reference the proxy statement sections titled “Executive Compensation,” “Director Compensation,” “Corporate Governance - Risk Oversight,” and “Compensation Committee Interlocks and Insider Participation.”
sections.Item 12: UnitedHealth Group Inc. reports that, as of December 31, 2024, its equity compensation plans authorized 17 million shares of common stock for issuance, with a weighted-average exercise price of $370 and 64 million shares remaining available for future issuance. The approved plans consist of the UnitedHealth Group Incorporated 2020 Stock Incentive Plan, as amended, and the UnitedHealth Group 1993 Employee Stock Purchase Plan, as amended, while no awards are outstanding under plans not approved by shareholders, aside from 60,000 assumed acquisition-related options with a $373 weighted-average exercise price and about 2.4 years remaining term. The company also notes that Item 403 beneficial ownership information will be provided in its definitive proxy statement for the 2025 Annual Meeting of Shareholders and incorporated by reference.
sections.Item 10: UnitedHealth Group Inc. William McNabb III, Paul Garcia, Valerie Montgomery Rice, Kristen Gil, and John Noseworthy. sections.Item 13: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that the information required by Items 404 and 407(a) of Regulation S-K regarding certain relationships and related transactions and director independence is not presented in this Form 10-K section, but will instead be included by reference in the company’s definitive proxy statement for its 2025 Annual Meeting of Shareholders. sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains the Form 10-K signatures for UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) filed February 27, 2025, and states that there is no Form 10-K summary. (UNH) describes itself as a health care and well-being company focused on helping people live healthier lives and improving the health system through its two business platforms, Optum and UnitedHealthcare. The section notes four reportable segments—Optum Health, Optum Insight, Optum Rx, and UnitedHealthcare (including Employer & Individual, Medicare & Retirement, and Community & State)—and references the realignment of UnitedHealthcare operating segments, with forward-looking statements and risk disclosures tied to the PSLRA and the company’s 2021 10-K.
sections.Item 4: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) are designed to ensure timely recording, processing, summarization, and communication of information required in SEC filings, with oversight by the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer. sections.Item 1A: UnitedHealth Group Inc. states that investors should consider the risk factors disclosed in Part I, Item 1A of its 2021 Form 10-K, as those risks could materially affect its business, financial condition, and future results. The company says there have been no material changes to those risk factors in the 2022 Form 10-Q, while noting that additional unknown or currently immaterial risks could still materially adversely affect UnitedHealth Group’s operations and results.
sections.Item 3: UnitedHealth Group Inc. describes its market risk management, noting that it diversifies fixed-income investments across sectors and maturities and uses interest rate swap contracts to match floating-rate assets and liabilities. As of March 31, 2022, it modeled hypothetical 1% and 2% parallel shifts in rates, estimating annual impacts on investment income and interest expense and changes in fair value of financial assets and liabilities, with unrealized gains and losses on available-for-sale debt securities reported in comprehensive income. The disclosure also notes that low short-term rates limited the modeled downside because rates were assumed not to fall below zero, and some investments already had rates below 2%.

UPS_10K_2026-02-17_full.txt.document_context: United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS) filed a 10-K annual report for fiscal year 2025. The company said it delivered an average of 20.8 million packages per day in 2025, totaling 5.2 billion packages, and reported total revenue of $88.7 billion.

sections.Item 8: UPS says its business and operating results could be materially harmed by unknown risks, continued uncertainty in U.S. and global economic conditions, and geopolitical tensions affecting regions including the European Union, Ukraine, the Russian Federation, the Middle East, and the Trans-Pacific region. The company operates in over 200 countries and territories and cites exposure to industrial production, inflation, unemployment, consumer spending, retail activity, and international trade policy, while warning that its industry is rapidly evolving and highly competitive. Competitors include U.S. and international postal services, motor carriers, express companies, freight forwarders, air couriers, large transportation companies, e-commerce companies, retailers, and startups investing in technology and logistics capabilities.
sections.Preamble: United Parcel Service, Inc. sections.Item 1A: UPS, founded in 1907, describes itself as a global package delivery and logistics provider serving over 200 countries and territories through an integrated air and ground network, distribution, contract logistics, ocean freight, airfreight, customs brokerage, and insurance. In 2025, UPS delivered an average of 20.8 million packages per day, totaling 5.2 billion packages, and generated $88.7 billion in revenue. The section also highlights the Company’s Customer First, People Led, Innovation Driven strategy, with emphasis on healthcare, B2B, SMBs, and international markets, and notes its use of the Investor Relations website and Regulation FD for disclosure of material information.
sections.Item 10: United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS) describes a board-level cybersecurity governance framework in which the independent Risk Committee oversees enterprise risks including privacy, technology, information security, AI, cyber incident response, and business continuity, with regular reporting from the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), Chief Legal and Compliance Officer (CLCO), Chief Digital and Technology Officer (CDTO), and Vice President of Compliance and Internal Audit. The committee reviews the cybersecurity insurance program and budget, internal audits and third-party assessments, outside expert briefings, and updates on operational metrics, compliance, training, risk mitigation, and regulatory developments, while the CISO reports to the CDTO and then to the CEO. UPS also says cybersecurity is embedded in its enterprise risk management process and supported by the Information Security & Privacy Governance Council (ISPGC), which meets periodically on information security and privacy matters.
sections.Item 7: UPS’s 2025 Management’s Discussion and Analysis emphasizes its Customer First, People Led and Innovation Driven strategy, with a shift toward higher-value segments such as healthcare, SMBs, and International, while reducing volume from its largest customer by a targeted 50% by June 2026 from 2024 levels. The company reported a 6.6% increase in consolidated revenue per piece, SMB penetration above 30% of total U.S. volume, about $3.5 billion in planned year-over-year cost savings from Network Reconfiguration and Efficiency Reimagined, and more than $11 billion of revenue from its global healthcare portfolio after acquiring Frigo-Trans, Biotech & Pharma Logistics, and Andlauer Healthcare Group. UPS also highlighted the 2024 divestiture of Coyote, the January 2025 insourcing of SurePost as Ground Saver, and a December 2025 USPS agreement for final-mile delivery on portions of Ground Saver and Mail Innovations starting

sections.Item 1C: Verizon Communications Inc. describes a comprehensive cybersecurity program aligned with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework to identify, detect, evaluate, remediate, and recover from cyber risks and incidents affecting its company and network infrastructure. The program relies on commercial, government, and public threat intelligence; manual and automated vulnerability detection; severity-based remediation tracked in a central system of record; and metrics and analysis to monitor trends and management accountability. Verizon also uses tabletop exercises, penetration tests, data recovery testing, internal and external security assessments, threat intelligence monitoring, annual mandatory cybersecurity and data privacy training for all full- and part-time employees, and third-party assessors and consultants to validate cybersecurity maturity.

Verizon said substantially all switching centers are on owned land and in owned buildings, and its U.S. footprint includes administrative and sales offices, customer care centers, retail stores, garage work centers, cell sites, and data centers.

It reports two segments: Verizon Consumer Group, which in 2025 generated $106.8 billion of revenue, about 77% of consolidated revenue, and had approximately 116 million wireless retail connections, 11 million broadband connections, and 2 million Fios video connections; and Verizon Business Group, which provides wireless and wireline services, FWA, broadband, IoT connectivity, corporate networking, voice, security, and managed network services. sections.Item 7A: Verizon Communications Inc. says it is exposed to market risks from interest rates, foreign exchange, investment/equity/commodity prices, and corporate tax rates, and manages these risks with derivatives such as cross-currency swaps, forward-starting interest rate swaps, interest rate swaps, interest rate caps, treasury rate locks, and foreign exchange forwards. The company states it does not use derivatives for trading or fully hedge market risk, instead maintaining a mix of fixed- and variable-rate debt to control borrowing costs and earnings/cash flow volatility, and it uses ISDA master agreements and CSA collateral arrangements with major financial institutions. V_10K_2025-11-06_full.txt.document_context: Visa Inc filed a 10-K annual report for fiscal 2025. The company said it processed 329 billion payments and cash transactions during the year, including 258 billion processed by Visa, and handled $17 trillion in total payments and cash volume across nearly 5 billion payment credentials at more than 175 million merchant locations worldwide.

sections.Preamble: Visa Inc. filed its Form 10-K annual report with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission for the fiscal year ended September 30, 2025, identifying itself as a Delaware corporation headquartered in San Francisco, California, with Commission file number 001-33977 and IRS Employer Identification No. 26-0267673. The preamble lists Visa’s NYSE-registered securities, including Class A Common Stock (V) and multiple senior notes due 2026, 2028, 2029, 2033, 2034, 2037, and 2044, and notes that the company is a well-known seasoned issuer and has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1: Visa Inc. describes itself as a global leader in digital payments and transaction processing, operating the four-party model through authorization, clearing, and settlement among consumers, issuing and acquiring financial institutions, and sellers, while extending its network to digital banks, digital wallets, fintechs, governments, and NGOs. The company emphasizes its proprietary VisaNet network across more than 200 countries and territories, its investment in AI, generative AI, stablecoins, and agentic commerce, and reports fiscal 2025 volumes of 329 billion payments and cash transactions, 258 billion processed by Visa, $17 trillion in total payments and cash volume, nearly 5 billion payment credentials, and access at more than 175 million merchant locations.
sections.Item 1B: Visa Inc.’s Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, states that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff. The section is marked “Not applicable,” indicating no outstanding regulatory correspondence or disclosure issues in this annual 10-K filing.

sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Visa Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is reserved and contains no substantive discussion, financial data, or operational disclosure. The section appears only as “[Reserved]” in the table of contents, with no named entities, metrics, or business developments presented.
sections.Item 1A: Visa Inc. says its global payments technology business faces complex and evolving regulation worldwide, including rules on interchange reimbursement rates, preferred routing, domestic processing and localization, currency conversion, point-of-sale practices, privacy, data use and protection, licensing, anti-money laundering, anti-corruption, competition, money transfer services and sanctions. The company warns that regulators may limit its ability to enforce Visa operating rules, force changes to contractual arrangements and product technology, raise compliance costs and operational complexity, and reduce revenue opportunities if rules vary by country, state or product. Visa also highlights the risk of enforcement actions, monetary damages, civil and criminal penalties, investigations, litigation and reputational harm from noncompliance, alongside increased scrutiny of interchange fees, merchant discount rates and risk management protocols.
sections.Item 1C: Visa describes cybersecurity as a key enterprise risk and a core element of maintaining trust in its global payment services business, with a program designed to identify, analyze, mitigate, monitor, and govern cyber risk under its enterprise risk management framework. The program is built around ISO 27002, the Payment Card Industry Data Security Standards, and applicable legal and regulatory requirements, implemented through annual employee training, Key Controls, and oversight by the Corporate Risk Committee (CRC), Cybersecurity Governance, Risk and Compliance team, and management committees. Visa also notes separate controls for acquired or designated entities that may not be fully integrated with standard platforms, with regular reporting to the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), President of Technology, management committees, and the board of directors.
sections.Item 4: Visa Inc. states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on November 6, 2025.
