On October 28, 2025, the Visa board declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.67 per share of class A common stock, payable December 1, 2025 to holders of record on November 12, 2025, and reiterated that future dividends depend on board discretion and legally available funds. Visa also reported repurchases of 14 million shares of class A common stock during the three months ended September 30, 2025 at an average price of $349.77 per share, leaving approximately $24.9 billion available under its publicly announced repurchase programs.
sections.Item 9B: Visa Inc. reported no other information under Item 9B, stating that there were no trading plans in effect. This section is a brief disclosure in the company’s 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on November 6, 2025.
sections.Item 7A: Visa Inc. says its market risk exposure is driven mainly by foreign currency exchange rates, interest rates, and equity prices, with ongoing monitoring and hedging through foreign currency forward contracts. As of September 30, 2025 and 2024, a hypothetical 10% weakening in functional currencies would increase fair value losses on outstanding forwards by about $422 million and $329 million, while a 10% change in the Euro versus the U.S. dollar would create translation adjustments of $2.3 billion and $2.1 billion, respectively, tied to Visa Europe. Visa also designates Euro-denominated senior notes as a net investment hedge to partially offset foreign exchange translation risk, with related adjustments recorded in accumulated other comprehensive income (loss).
sections.Item 7: Visa Inc. describes its fiscal 2025 Management’s Discussion and Analysis as a review of historical results, financial condition, liquidity, and capital resources, with comparisons primarily against fiscal 2024 and references to prior-year discussion in its 2024 Form 10-K filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. The company emphasizes its role as a global payments technology network operating VisaNet across more than 200 countries and territories, providing authorization, clearing, and settlement services but not issuing cards or extending credit. Fiscal 2025 financial highlights include net revenue of $40.0 billion, operating expenses of $16.0 billion, net income of $20.1 billion, diluted EPS of $10.20, and non-GAAP net income of $22.5 billion, reflecting year-over-year growth in revenue and earnings.
sections.Item 8: Item 8 of Visa Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K presents the consolidated financial statements and supplementary data, including the balance sheets, statements of operations, comprehensive income, changes in equity, cash flows, and notes for the three years ended September 30, 2025. KPMG LLP of San Francisco audited Visa’s consolidated financial statements and internal control over financial reporting and issued an unqualified opinion that the statements fairly present Visa’s financial position, results of operations, and cash flows in conformity with U.S. GAAP, and that internal control over financial reporting was effective as of September 30, 2025.
sections.Item 9C: Visa Inc. states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions that Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on 2025-11-06. The section appears as a brief transition into Part III and contains no additional substantive disclosure, metrics, or named foreign jurisdictions.
sections.Item 9A: Visa Inc. states that its disclosure controls and procedures under Exchange Act Rules 13a-15(e) and 15d-15(e) were evaluated by management, including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, and were effective at the reasonable assurance level as of September 30, 2025. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective under the COSO 2013 Internal Control—Integrated Framework, with that assessment audited by KPMG LLP, while noting the inherent limitations of controls such as human error, override, and resource constraints.
sections.Item 9: Visa Inc. (V) states that Item 9, “Changes in and Disagreements with Accountants on Accounting and Financial Disclosures,” is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K filed on 2025-11-06. The section indicates there were no changes in accountants and no disagreements regarding accounting or financial disclosures.
sections.Item 11: Visa Inc. states that the executive compensation information required by Item 11 of its 2025 Form 10-K will be provided in its Proxy Statement and is incorporated by reference into the annual report. This section contains no substantive compensation details itself, but directs readers to the Proxy Statement for disclosure on executive pay and related governance matters.
sections.Item 10: Visa Inc. states that the information required by Item 10, including directors, executive officers, and corporate governance matters, will be provided in its definitive proxy statement filed under Regulation 14A of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The company says the Proxy Statement will be filed no later than 120 days after the end of the fiscal year ended September 30, 2025, and is incorporated into the Form 10-K by reference.
sections.Item 13: Visa Inc. states that the information required for Item 13, “Certain Relationships and Related Transactions, and Director Independence,” is not presented in the Form 10-K itself but will be included in the company’s Proxy Statement and incorporated by reference. This section therefore points readers to the Proxy Statement for details on related-party transactions, director independence, and any associated governance disclosures.
sections.Item 14: Visa Inc. states that the information required by Item 14, including principal accountant fees and services, is contained in its Proxy Statement and is incorporated by reference into this 10-K. This section is part of Part IV of the annual report filed with the SEC on 2025-11-06 and does not provide the underlying fee details in the filing excerpt itself.
sections.Item 15: Visa Inc.’s Item 15 states that the company’s Consolidated Financial Statements are included by reference to Item 8, while no Consolidated Financial Statement Schedules are filed with the 10-K. The section also notes that the report’s exhibits are listed in the Exhibit Index and that some exhibits were previously filed and are incorporated by reference.
sections.Item 12: Visa Inc. states that the information required by Item 12, Security Ownership of Certain Beneficial Owners and Management and Related Stockholder Matters, is not presented in the 10-K itself but will be included in the company’s Proxy Statement and incorporated by reference. This section therefore directs readers to the Proxy Statement for details on beneficial ownership, management ownership, and related stockholder matters.
sections.Item 2: Visa Inc. states that its corporate headquarters are in the San Francisco Bay Area and that, as of September 30, 2025, it owned or leased office locations worldwide. The company also operated four global data centers in the U.S., the United Kingdom, and Singapore, and said these facilities are suitable and adequate to support its ongoing business needs.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 is the Exhibit Index for Visa Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K and contains no narrative discussion beyond listing incorporated-by-reference filings and agreements. It identifies key corporate governance and financing documents, including Visa Inc.’s Eighth Restated Certificate of Incorporation, Amended and Restated Bylaws, the 2016 Amended and Restated Transaction Agreement with Visa Europe Limited, and multiple debt instruments such as senior notes due 2025 through 2040 and the related indenture with U.S. Bank National Association.

WMT_10K_2025-03-14_full.txt.document_context: Walmart Inc. filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended January 31, 2025. The company said it serves about 270 million customers each week across more than 10,750 stores and eCommerce sites in 19 countries, and reported fiscal 2025 total revenues of $681.0 billion, including $674.5 billion of net sales.

sections.Preamble: Walmart Inc.’s 2025 Annual Report on Form 10-K for the fiscal year ended January 31, 2025 opens with filing references to the Proxy Statement for the June 5, 2025 Annual Shareholders’ Meeting and the company’s consolidated subsidiaries, then introduces Part I’s cautionary statement on forward-looking statements. The section emphasizes SEC Exchange Act Section 21E safe-harbor protections and notes that Walmart’s projections and expectations may address macroeconomic and geopolitical conditions, inflation or deflation, supply chain challenges, tariffs, and other business trends affecting operations.
sections.Item 1B: Walmart Inc. (WMT) states in Item 1B, Unresolved Staff Comments, that there were no unresolved comments from the SEC staff in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed on March 14, 2025. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding regulatory comment issues requiring further disclosure or resolution.
sections.Item 1C: Walmart Inc. says its cybersecurity program is designed to protect the confidentiality, integrity and availability of information while preserving business operations, and is grounded in its “digital trust commitments” and company values of service, excellence, integrity and respect. The Board of Directors oversees cybersecurity risk through the Audit Committee, which receives periodic updates from the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO), Chief Technology Officer (CTO) and other management on the threat landscape, key initiatives, assessments and testing, while the CISO leads Walmart’s Information Security organization and escalation protocols for incidents exceeding reporting thresholds.
sections.Item 1: Walmart Inc. describes itself as a people-led, technology-powered omni-channel retailer serving about 270 million customers weekly through more than 10,750 stores and numerous eCommerce websites across 19 countries, with a strategy centered on price leadership, convenience, and digital integration. The company emphasizes its Everyday Low Price (EDLP) and Everyday Low Cost (EDLC) model, reports three segments—Walmart U.S., Walmart International, and Sam’s Club U.S.—and says fiscal 2025 revenue was $681.0 billion, including $674.5 billion of net sales. Walmart’s principal offices are in Bentonville, Arkansas, and its common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol WMT.
sections.Item 1A: Walmart Inc. says its “Shared Value Priorities” are central to long-term value creation and competitive advantage, focusing on four themes: opportunity, sustainability, community, and ethics and integrity. The company highlights efforts to create jobs and career pathways for associates, support tens of thousands of suppliers, improve supply-chain resilience and responsible sourcing, offer safer and healthier products, reduce waste and greenhouse gas emissions, and procure renewable energy.
sections.Item 3: Walmart Inc. says its amended and restated bylaws make the Delaware Court of Chancery the exclusive forum for certain shareholder claims, including derivative duty-breach actions against current or former directors, officers, associates or shareholders, unless Walmart agrees otherwise in writing. The company warns this forum-selection provision could raise shareholder litigation costs or be found unenforceable, and notes it does not limit federal court jurisdiction over claims under the Exchange Act or Securities Act of 1933. Walmart also highlights that unmet stakeholder expectations on ESG goals and initiatives—such as climate change, waste, packaging and plastic usage—could harm its reputation and expose it to regulatory or litigation risk.
sections.Item 2: As of January 31, 2025, Walmart Inc. reported 10,771 retail units worldwide and 379 distribution facilities, spanning Walmart U.S. (4,605 units), Sam’s Club U.S. (600 units), and Walmart International (5,566 units), with total retail square footage of 1,052,720 thousand square feet. The company disclosed a mix of owned and leased properties, including 6,039 owned and 4,732 leased retail units and 148 owned and 231 leased distribution facilities, and noted that its principal office is in Bentonville, Arkansas.
sections.Item 4: Walmart Inc. (NYSE: WMT) states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is not applicable in its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on March 14, 2025. The section contains no mine safety incidents, citations, penalties, or related metrics, indicating Walmart does not operate mines subject to the Mine Safety and Health Administration disclosure requirements.
sections.Item 6: Item 6 of Walmart Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K is marked “RESERVED” and contains no substantive discussion or disclosed metrics. This section does not add operational, financial, regulatory, or product-specific information beyond the placeholder page reference.
sections.Item 5: Walmart Inc. states that its common stock trades on the New York Stock Exchange under the symbol “WMT,” with 194,162 holders of record as of March 12, 2025, and presents a five-year total shareholder return chart comparing Walmart’s performance to the S&P 500 Index and the S&P 500 Consumer Discretionary Distribution & Retailing Index. The company also discloses that all fiscal 2025 repurchases were made under its $20.0 billion share repurchase program approved in November 2022, with $12.0 billion remaining authorized as of January 31, 2025, and that repurchased shares are constructively retired.
sections.Item 9: Walmart Inc. (WMT) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure matters in Item 9 of its 2025 Form 10-K. The section states simply: “None.”
sections.Item 7: Walmart Inc.’s Item 7 MD&A explains fiscal 2025, fiscal 2024, and fiscal 2023 results and is intended to help readers understand changes in the consolidated financial statements, segment performance, and the primary drivers of operating results. The section emphasizes management’s key metrics—segment operating income, comparable store and club sales, and eCommerce sales—used by the chief operating decision maker to assess the company’s three business segments, including sales from stores and clubs open at least 12 months, omni-channel transactions, and advertising net sales. It also defines how Walmart excludes certain converted, relocated, expanded, or divested store sales from comparable sales calculations, highlighting the company’s focus on measuring underlying performance across its retail and digital ecosystem.
sections.Item 8: Walmart Inc. says its Item 8 “Contingencies” disclosure includes extensive legal proceedings, led by opioid-related litigation in In re National Prescription Opiate Litigation (MDL No. 2804) in the U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio, with about 250 cases pending as of March 10, 2025, plus more than 25 additional opioid cases in U.S., state, federal, and Canadian courts. The company also identifies DOJ opioid civil litigation, derivative suits, securities class actions consolidated as In re Walmart Inc. Securities Litigation in the U.S. District Court for the District of Delaware and the Third Circuit, a False Claims Act case, and ASDA Equal Value Claims, while noting it accrued about $3.3 billion for certain opioid-related settlements in fiscal 2023 and had fully paid that liability by January 31, 2025.
sections.Item 7A: Walmart Inc. says it is exposed to market risk from changes in interest rates, currency exchange rates, and the fair values of certain equity and equity method investments, and that its sensitivity analysis is based on hypothetical scenarios rather than forecasts. For fiscal 2025, the net fair value of Walmart’s interest rate swaps increased $43 million due to market rate fluctuations, and the company manages interest rate exposure through a mix of fixed- and variable-rate debt and interest rate swaps. As of January 31, 2025, Walmart reported $3.068 billion of variable-rate short-term borrowings at a 5.3% weighted-average rate and $35.999 billion of fixed-rate long-term debt with a 4.0% weighted-average rate across maturities through and beyond fiscal 2030.
sections.Item 9C: Walmart Inc. (WMT) states that Item 9C, “Disclosure Regarding Foreign Jurisdictions That Prevent Inspections,” is not applicable in this 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC. The section serves as a brief transition into Part III and contains no additional foreign jurisdiction, inspection, or regulatory disclosure.
sections.Item 10: Item 10 of Walmart Inc.’s 2025 Form 10-K covers directors, executive officers, and corporate governance, directing readers to the company’s separate disclosure on executive officers. The section signals that governance and leadership information for Walmart (NYSE: WMT) is addressed in the filing’s related executive officer materials rather than detailed in this excerpt.
sections.Item 9B: Walmart Inc. disclosed that on November 22, 2024, Kathryn McLay, Executive Vice President, President and Chief Executive Officer of Walmart International, adopted a stock trading plan intended to comply with Rule 10b5-1 under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Under the plan, McLay is scheduled to sell an aggregate of 40,000 shares of Walmart common stock, and the plan is set to terminate in December 2025.
sections.Item 9A: Walmart Inc. describes its disclosure controls and procedures as designed to provide reasonable assurance that information required for timely SEC reporting is accumulated, communicated, recorded, processed, summarized, and reported appropriately, with management judgment acknowledged as an inherent limitation. As of January 31, 2025, management, including the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer, concluded these controls were effective under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, while noting ongoing upgrades to global financial systems, modernization efforts, automation, standardization, shared services migration, and increased monitoring that affect internal control over financial reporting. The section also notes that controls over unconsolidated entities are more limited because Walmart does not control or manage those investments.
sections.Item 11: Walmart Inc. (NYSE: WMT) states that the information required by Item 11, Executive Compensation, is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement, specifically the sections titled “Corporate Governance – Director Compensation” and “Executive Compensation.” This section does not provide compensation figures or program details in the 10-K itself, but directs readers to the proxy materials for executive and director pay disclosures.
sections.Item 12: Walmart Inc. states that the information required by Item 12, “Security Ownership of Certain Beneficial Owners and Management and Related Stockholder Matters,” is incorporated by reference to the “Stock Ownership” section of its Proxy Statement. This section does not provide additional ownership data in the 10-K excerpt itself, but directs readers to the proxy materials for details on beneficial owners, management holdings, and related stockholder matters.
sections.Item 13: Walmart Inc. states that the information required for Item 13, “Certain Relationships and Related Transactions, and Director Independence,” is incorporated by reference from its Proxy Statement. Specifically, Walmart points readers to the “Corporate Governance – Board Processes and Practices” section for details on related-party transactions and director independence.
sections.Item 14: Walmart Inc. states that the information required for Item 14, “Principal Accounting Fees and Services,” is incorporated by reference to the Proxy Statement under “Proposal No. 4 – Ratification of Independent Accountants.” This section does not provide fee amounts or service details in the 10-K excerpt itself, but directs readers to the company’s proxy materials for the independent accountants’ ratification and related accounting fee disclosures.
sections.Item 15: Walmart Inc. (NYSE: WMT) states in Item 15 that the documents filed as part of the 2025 Form 10-K include the company’s Financial Statements, with the section directing readers to the Financial Statements incorporated elsewhere in the report. This exhibits and financial statement schedules section serves as the filing’s index to the report’s supporting documents and schedules rather than presenting substantive operating or financial results.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 contains Walmart Inc.’s Form 10-K signature pages for the fiscal year ended January 31, 2025, filed March 14, 2025, and includes the company’s formal certification under Sections 13 and 15(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Signatories include President and Chief Executive Officer C. Douglas McMillon, Chairman Gregory B. Penner, Chief Financial Officer John David Rainey, Principal Accounting Officer David M. Chojnowski, and directors Cesar Conde, Timothy P. Flynn, Sarah Friar, Carla A. Harris, Thomas W. Horton, Marissa A. Mayer, Robert E. Moritz, Jr., Brian Niccol, Randall L. Stephenson, and Steuart L. Walton.

XOM_10K_2022Q4_2023-02-22_full.txt.document_context: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s 10-K annual report covers the fiscal year ended December 31, 2022. The company said it operates globally in exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas, and in 2022 it held over 8 thousand active patents worldwide while generating approximately $129 million of revenue from technology licensed to third parties.

sections.Preamble: Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE: XOM) filed its 2022 Form 10-K annual report with the SEC under Commission File No. 1-2256, identifying itself as a New Jersey corporation headquartered at 5959 Las Colinas Boulevard, Irving, Texas 75039-2298, with employer identification number 13-5409005 and telephone number (972) 940-6000. The filing lists Exxon Mobil’s registered securities on the New York Stock Exchange, including common stock and notes due 2024, 2028, 2032, and 2039, and indicates the company is a well-known seasoned issuer, a large accelerated filer, and current in its Exchange Act reporting and EDGAR interactive data filing obligations.
sections.Item 1: Exxon Mobil Corporation, incorporated in New Jersey in 1882, operates through divisions and hundreds of affiliates worldwide in exploration and production of crude oil and natural gas, manufacturing and trading petroleum products, petrochemicals, and specialty products, and expanding lower-emission businesses such as carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, and lower-emission fuels. The company says the energy and petrochemical industries are highly competitive and that ExxonMobil is investing in lower-emission energy and emission-reduction technologies, while its affiliates conduct extensive research programs and held over 8 thousand active patents worldwide at the end of 2022.
sections.Item 1A: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 1A Risk Factors emphasizes that government regulations, taxes, environmental rules, and other policies can materially affect capital expenditures, earnings, and competitive position, while the company’s global oil, gas, and petrochemical businesses remain exposed to commodity price and margin volatility. ExxonMobil says it is investing heavily in refining infrastructure, clean-fuels technology, emissions monitoring and remediation, and asset retirement obligations, with worldwide environmental expenditures of $5.7 billion in 2022 (including $3.8 billion in expenses), expected to rise to about $7.3 billion in 2023 and $8.2 billion in 2024.
sections.Item 1B: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states that there were no unresolved staff comments in Item 1B of its 2023 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 22, 2023. The section indicates that the company had no outstanding comments from the Securities and Exchange Commission staff requiring resolution.
sections.Item 7A: Exxon Mobil Corporation says its results can be adversely affected by government and political factors, including restrictions on access to oil and gas resources, leasing and permitting limits, foreign investment barriers, import/export controls, and U.S. or foreign sanctions that can prohibit or restrict business in certain countries. The company also highlights legal uncertainty, regulatory and litigation risk, and policy changes in taxes, royalties, price controls, environmental rules, offshore drilling, water use, emissions, hydraulic fracturing, and plastics that could raise compliance costs, delay projects, or reduce opportunities.
sections.Item 2: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 2 “Properties” section describes its oil and gas producing activities, including how proved reserves are reported and converted to oil-equivalent basis, with reserves measured using the SEC’s first-day-of-the-month average price method and no major post–December 31, 2022 events expected to materially change estimates. At year-end 2022, ExxonMobil reported total developed proved reserves of 11,143 million oil-equivalent barrels, including 4,211 million barrels of crude oil, 729 million barrels of natural gas liquids, 2,288 million barrels of bitumen, 248 million barrels of synthetic oil, and 21,998 billion cubic feet of natural gas across consolidated subsidiaries and equity companies in the United States, Canada/Other Americas, Europe, Africa, Asia, and Australia/Oceania.
sections.Item 4: Exxon Mobil Corporation states that Item 4, Mine Safety Disclosures, is “Not applicable” in its 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC. The section then lists executive officers as of February 22, 2023, including Chairman and CEO Darren W. Woods, CFO Kathryn A. Mikells, and other senior leaders such as Neil A. Chapman, Jack P. Williams, Jr., James R. Chapman, Len M. Fox, Jon M. Gibbs, Liam M. Mallon, Karen T. McKee, and Craig S. Morford, along with their current and prior roles at ExxonMobil and, for some, prior employers like Diageo plc, Dominion Energy, Inc., and Cardinal Health, Inc.
sections.Item 3: Exxon Mobil Corporation discloses environmental legal proceedings using a $1 million threshold, including a DOJ/EPA matter involving XTO Energy, Inc. over the Schnegg well in Powhatan Point, Ohio, where the EPA alleges a Clean Air Act General Duty Clause breach tied to a 2018 well blowout and the DOJ has indicated it may seek a penalty substantially above $1 million. The company also faces a Texas Attorney General lawsuit, State of Texas v. Exxon Mobil Corporation, in Travis County District Court over alleged Texas Clean Air Act violations at the Baytown Olefins Plant in Baytown, Texas, seeking civil penalties, injunctive relief, and recovery of fees and costs for alleged air pollution, outdoor burning, nuisance, and visible emissions from 2018 to 2022.
sections.Item 9: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) reported no changes in accountants and no disagreements with its accountants on accounting or financial disclosure in Item 9 of its 2023 Form 10-K. The section states simply that there were no such matters to disclose.
sections.Item 9B: Item 9B, Other Information, for Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states that there was no additional information to report in this section of the 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC on February 22, 2023. The section is marked “None,” indicating no other material events, disclosures, or updates beyond the rest of the annual report.
sections.Item 5: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states that its common stock primarily trades on the New York Stock Exchange, with additional trading on other U.S. and foreign exchanges, and it had 310,437 registered shareholders at December 31, 2022 and 308,630 at January 31, 2023. On January 30, 2023, ExxonMobil declared a $0.91 per-share dividend payable March 10, 2023, and for the quarter ended December 31, 2022 it repurchased 43.6 million shares at an average price of $107.14, leaving $35.0 billion available under its expanded $50 billion share repurchase program through 2024 announced in the 2022 Corporate Plan Update.
sections.Item 7: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis directs readers to the Financial Section’s “Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations,” indicating that the annual report’s detailed discussion of operating performance, liquidity, capital resources, and financial condition is contained elsewhere in the filing. This section serves as a cross-reference within Exxon Mobil’s 2023 Form 10-K filed with the SEC, rather than providing substantive results itself.
sections.Item 8: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 8 financial section references the consolidated financial statements and notes through “Note 20: Divestment Activities,” along with unaudited “Supplemental Information on Oil and Gas Exploration and Production Activities” and “Frequently Used Terms.” The statements are accompanied by the report of independent registered public accounting firm PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP (PCAOB ID 238) dated February 22, 2023, and the company notes that Financial Statement Schedules were omitted because they were not applicable or the required information is already included in the consolidated statements or notes.
sections.Item 9A: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) reports that its Chief Executive Officer, Chief Financial Officer, and Principal Accounting Officer evaluated disclosure controls and procedures as of December 31, 2022 and concluded they were effective under Securities Exchange Act of 1934 reporting requirements and SEC rules. Management also concluded that internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 31, 2022 based on the COSO Internal Control—Integrated Framework (2013), with PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP auditing that effectiveness, and Exxon Mobil disclosed no material changes in internal control during the last fiscal quarter.
sections.Item 10: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 10 directs readers to the “Information about our Executive Officers” section and incorporates by reference governance disclosures from its 2023 Proxy Statement. The referenced proxy materials cover election of directors, director qualifications, director nomination process and board succession, the Code of Ethics and Business Conduct, director independence, board and annual meeting attendance, and Board Committee membership, including the Audit Committee and Nominating and Governance Committee.
sections.Item 9C: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states in Item 9C of its 2023 Form 10-K that the disclosure regarding foreign jurisdictions that prevent inspections is “Not applicable.” This section contains no substantive discussion of foreign inspection restrictions, regulators, operations, or related metrics, and simply marks the end of Item 9C before Part III.
sections.Item 11: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 11 Executive Compensation disclosure is incorporated by reference to the 2023 Proxy Statement, specifically the sections on Director Compensation, Compensation Committee Report, Compensation Discussion and Analysis, Executive Compensation Tables, Pay Ratio, and Pay Versus Performance. The filing directs readers to those proxy statement sections for details on executive and director pay practices, compensation governance, and related metrics.
sections.Item 14: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 14 cross-references the 2023 Proxy Statement for disclosure of principal accounting fees and services, incorporating by reference the “Audit Committee” portion of “Corporate Governance” and the “Ratification of Independent Auditors” section. This section is part of Part IV of the 2023 Form 10-K and does not provide standalone fee amounts or auditor details in the filing excerpt.
sections.Item 12: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 12 incorporates by reference the “Certain Beneficial Owners” and “Director and Executive Officer Stock Ownership” disclosures from its 2023 Proxy Statement under Item 403 of Regulation S-K. The section reports 42,542,460 securities issuable under equity compensation plans approved by security holders, with 60,288,068 shares remaining available for future issuance, including 59,964,868 under the 2003 Incentive Program and 323,200 under the 2004 Non-Employee Director Restricted Stock Plan. It also describes the director restricted stock arrangement, under which each non-employee director receives 8,000 shares upon first election and 2,500 additional restricted shares annually, subject to forfeiture if the director leaves the Board early.
sections.Item 15: Item 15 of Exxon Mobil Corporation’s 2023 Form 10-K states that the financial statements and related schedules are included in the Financial Section table of contents, and that the company’s exhibits are listed in the Index to Exhibits. This section serves as a cross-reference to the report’s audited financial statements and supporting exhibit materials rather than presenting substantive financial data itself.
sections.Item 13: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 13 states that information on certain relationships and related transactions is incorporated by reference from its 2023 Proxy Statement, specifically the “Related Person Transactions and Procedures” portion of “Director and Executive Officer Stock Ownership” and the “Director Independence” portion of “Corporate Governance.” This section does not provide substantive details in the 10-K itself, but directs readers to the proxy statement for Exxon Mobil’s policies and disclosures regarding related-party transactions and director independence.
sections.Item 16: Item 16 is a table-of-contents style summary of Exxon Mobil Corporation’s 2023 Form 10-K, pointing readers to the financial section, MD&A, consolidated financial statements, and notes covering Russia, debt, litigation, pensions, taxes, divestments, and oil and gas supplemental information. It also highlights 2022 versus 2021 business profile metrics for Exxon’s segments, including Upstream earnings after income taxes of $36,479 million in 2022 versus $15,775 million in 2021, average capital employed of $145,805 million, return on average capital employed of 25.0%, and capital and exploration expenditures of $17,002 million.

XOM_10K_2023Q4_2024-02-28_full.txt.document_context: Exxon Mobil Corporation filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2023-12-31. The company said its core business spans oil and gas exploration and production, refining, petrochemicals, and lower-emission opportunities such as carbon capture, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, and lithium, and it entered into an October 2023 merger agreement to acquire Pioneer Natural Resources in exchange for ExxonMobil common stock, with closing expected in Q2 2024 subject to regulatory approvals.

sections.Preamble: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s 2023 Form 10-K annual report, filed with the SEC under Commission File Number 1-2256, identifies the company as a New Jersey corporation headquartered at 22777 Springwoods Village Parkway, Spring, Texas 77389-1425, with telephone number (972) 940-6000 and IRS Employer Identification Number 13-5409005. The filing lists Exxon Mobil’s securities registered on the New York Stock Exchange, including common stock (XOM) and notes due 2024 (XOM24B), 2028 (XOM28), 2032 (XOM32), and 2039 (XOM39A), and states that Exxon Mobil is a well-known seasoned issuer and a large accelerated filer that has timely filed required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files.
sections.Item 1: Exxon Mobil Corporation, incorporated in New Jersey in 1882, operates globally through divisions and affiliates that explore for and produce crude oil and natural gas, manufacture and market petroleum products, petrochemicals, and specialty products, and pursue lower-emission opportunities such as carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, and lithium. The company highlights intense competition across energy and petrochemical markets and notes that operating data, segment information, and oil and gas reserves are detailed elsewhere in the 10-K. In October 2023 ExxonMobil signed a merger agreement to acquire Pioneer Natural Resources Company in exchange for ExxonMobil common stock, with closing expected in Q2 2024 subject to regulatory approvals.
sections.Item 1B: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states in Item 1B of its 2024 Form 10-K that there are no unresolved staff comments from the SEC. This section indicates that the company had no outstanding issues or comments requiring further resolution with the Securities and Exchange Commission at the time of filing.
sections.Item 7A: Exxon Mobil Corporation says its results are exposed to government and political factors, including restrictions on access to oil and gas resources, leasing and permitting limits, foreign investment curbs, import/export controls, and U.S. or foreign sanctions that can block or constrain operations, acquisitions, or divestitures. The company also highlights regulatory and litigation risk from changing laws, international treaties and accords, and inconsistent or underdeveloped legal frameworks in some countries, which can create unpredictable government actions and make contract enforcement difficult even where arbitration is available.
sections.Item 1A: Exxon Mobil Corporation says its financial and operating results are exposed to commodity-price volatility and broader supply-and-demand shifts in global oil, gas, and petrochemical markets, with lower oil or natural gas prices potentially harming Upstream operations and reserves and higher prices pressuring Energy Products, Chemical Products, and Specialty Products segments. The company also highlights risks from government regulations, taxes, environmental rules, and foreign operations, noting that its lower-emission opportunities in carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, and lithium depend on supportive government policies, technology development, and commercial-scale market growth.
sections.Item 2: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 2 “Properties” section describes its oil and gas producing activities, including the company’s website disclosures, SEC filing availability, and governance documents posted at exxonmobil.com and sec.gov. sections.Item 3: Exxon Mobil Corporation discloses environmental legal proceedings using a $1 million threshold and highlights a Texas enforcement action against ExxonMobil Oil Corporation (EMOC) over alleged unauthorized emissions at the Beaumont Refinery from 2017 to 2020. ExxonMobil Oil Corporation, filed in the 98th Judicial District Court of Travis County, Texas, the State of Texas sought penalties and injunctive relief, and the parties settled in September 2023 for $1.6 million, with court approval in November 2023 and payment completed in December 2023.

sections.Item 1C: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) says cybersecurity is integrated into its enterprise risk management system and overseen by the Audit Committee, which conducts an annual review of the company’s cybersecurity program. The program is managed by the Vice President of IT and ExxonMobil IT/OT Cybersecurity Operations Managers, who handle prevention, detection, investigation, and response, while reporting on strategy, key security metrics, penetration testing, benchmarking, and threat trends to senior management, the Audit Committee, and the Board of Directors. ExxonMobil also uses multi-layered defenses and the NIST Cybersecurity Framework, maintains an incident response plan, conducts annual awareness training and preparedness testing, shares threat information with government and industry groups, and engages third-party experts for penetration testing and breach assessments.

sections.Item 9A: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s CEO, CFO, and Principal Accounting Officer concluded as of December 31, 2023 that the company’s disclosure controls and procedures were effective under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and SEC reporting rules, ensuring timely accumulation, communication, recording, processing, and reporting of required information. XOM_10K_2024Q4_2025-02-19_full.txt.document_context: Exxon Mobil Corporation filed a 10-K annual report for the fiscal year ended 2024-12-31. The excerpt says ExxonMobil was incorporated in New Jersey in 1882 and that on May 3, 2024 it acquired Pioneer Natural Resources, issuing 545 million shares of ExxonMobil common stock valued at $63 billion and assuming $5 billion of debt. It also highlights the company’s core businesses in oil and gas, petrochemicals, and lower-emission opportunities such as carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, and lithium.

sections.Item 1B: Exxon Mobil Corporation (XOM) states that there were no unresolved staff comments in Item 1B of its 2025 Form 10-K annual report filed with the SEC on February 19, 2025. sections.Item 1: Exxon Mobil Corporation, incorporated in New Jersey in 1882, operates globally through divisions and affiliates under names including ExxonMobil, Exxon, Esso, Mobil, and XTO, with a principal business in exploring for and producing crude oil and natural gas and manufacturing, trading, transporting, and selling petroleum products, petrochemicals, and specialty products. The company also emphasizes lower-emission and new business opportunities such as carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, ProxximaTM systems, carbon materials, and lithium, supported by extensive research programs. On May 3, 2024, ExxonMobil acquired Pioneer Natural Resources Company for 545 million shares of common stock valued at $63 billion plus $5 billion of assumed debt, significantly expanding its U.S. unconventional upstream portfolio in a highly competitive energy and petrochemical industry.
sections.Item 1A: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 1A risk factors emphasize that its global oil, gas, and petrochemical businesses are highly exposed to commodity price and margin volatility, with changes in crude oil, natural gas, refined products, and petrochemical prices potentially materially affecting operations, financial condition, and proved reserves, especially in the Upstream, Energy Products, Chemical Products, and Specialty Products segments. The company also highlights risks tied to economic downturns, recessions, trade tariffs and sanctions, currency fluctuations, geopolitical instability, and public health disruptions, all of which can weaken energy and petrochemical demand. ExxonMobil’s lower-emission and new business initiatives, including carbon capture and storage, hydrogen, lower-emission fuels, ProxximaTM systems, carbon materials, and lithium, depend on supportive government policy, technology development, and commercial-scale market growth.
sections.Preamble: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s 2024 Form 10-K annual report, filed with the SEC under Commission File Number 1-2256, covers the fiscal year ended December 31, 2024 and identifies the company as a New Jersey corporation headquartered at 22777 Springwoods Village Parkway, Spring, Texas 77389-1425, with telephone number (972) 940-6000. The filing lists Exxon Mobil’s NYSE-registered securities, including common stock (XOM) and notes due 2028 (XOM28), 2032 (XOM32), and 2039 (XOM39A), and states that Exxon Mobil is a well-known seasoned issuer, has filed all required Exchange Act reports and interactive data files, and qualifies as a large accelerated filer.
sections.Item 2: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 2 — Properties section summarizes year-end 2024 proved oil and gas reserves for consolidated subsidiaries and equity companies, using the SEC reserve-price methodology based on the average first-day-of-month prices over the prior 12 months and a natural gas conversion of 6 billion cubic feet per 1 million barrels. Total developed proved reserves were 12,599 million oil-equivalent barrels and total undeveloped proved reserves were 5,838 million oil-equivalent barrels, with major concentrations in the United States, Canada/Other Americas, Asia, and Australia/Oceania across crude oil, natural gas liquids, bitumen, synthetic oil, and natural gas. Exxon Mobil states that no major discovery or other favorable or adverse event occurred after December 31, 2024 that would materially change estimated proved reserves.
sections.Item 1C: Exxon Mobil Corporation says cybersecurity is integrated into its enterprise risk management system and overseen by the Audit Committee, which conducts an annual review of the company’s cybersecurity program. The program is managed by the Vice President of Information Technology and ExxonMobil IT/operational technology cybersecurity operations managers, and includes multi-layered defenses, an incident response plan, annual awareness training, penetration testing, benchmarking, and use of the National Institute of Standards and Technology Cybersecurity Framework. The company also reports cybersecurity strategy, key security metrics, threat landscape developments, and response plans to senior management, the Audit Committee, or the Board of Directors, and works with governmental and industry groups plus third-party experts to test and improve resilience.
sections.Item 3: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s Item 3 discloses several environmental legal proceedings involving XTO Energy, Inc. and ExxonMobil, including EPA and DOJ actions over alleged Clean Air Act, NSPS, Title V, and General Duty Clause violations at well pad sites in Butler County, Pennsylvania and the Schnegg well in Powhatan Point, Ohio. XTO resolved the Pennsylvania matter with a $4.0 million consent decree entered by the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Pennsylvania in December 2024 and agreed to an $8.0 million penalty for the Ohio matter, while ExxonMobil continues litigation over a citizen suit by Environment Texas Citizen Lobby, Inc. concerning alleged air violations at the Baytown complex, where the Fifth Circuit affirmed a $14.25 million civil penalty payable to the U.S. Treasury.

sections.Item 2: Exxon Mobil Corporation’s first-quarter 2022 discussion highlights a sharp improvement in oil, natural gas, and refining markets driven by post-COVID demand recovery, supply constraints, and Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, with Brent crude and regional gas prices reaching multi-year highs and refining margins above the 10-year range. The company announced plans to discontinue operations and exit the Sakhalin-1 project in Russia, comply with sanctions, and transition operatorship to another party, resulting in $3.4 billion of after-tax charges largely tied to impairment of Sakhalin-related operations. Exxon said Sakhalin contributed about $0.2 billion of after-tax earnings and roughly 65 thousand oil-equivalent barrels per day in Q1 2022, and that exiting the venture would remove an estimated 150 million oil-equivalent barrels from proved reserves, less than 1% of its 18.5 billion oil-equivalent barrels at year-end 2021.
